GENERAL INFORMATION\n\nMedicaid - Title XIX of the Social Security Act has made available to many needy\ncitizens a broad program of health coverage designed to assist designated groups in\nmeeting hospital, medical, and other health costs. The Medicaid Law (Title XIX)\nspecifically designates administrative responsibility for the Program to a State Agency\ndesignated by the individual states and prohibits the Federal Government from\nexercising direct supervision or control over the Program, the manner in which\nmedical services are provided, and the administrative or operation of medical\nfacilities. In recognition of their experience in the health care and insurance fields,\nthe use of insurance organizations as fiscal agents by the states is authorized. The\nAlabama Medicaid Agency is the responsible State Agency in Alabama. The patient is\nfree to choose any qualified institution, agency, or practitioner-offering services\nbased on a written agreement with the State Agency. The responsibility for\ntreatment and the control of care remains with the physician and the nursing facility\nor agency, called a "Vendor" or "Provider of Services", having signed an agreement\nwith the State Agency to furnish services.\n\nIf the applicant has Medicare or any other health insurance plan, the provider must\nfile with those resources before filing Medicaid. The Alabama Medicaid Program will\npay Title XVIII deductible and coinsurance amounts for Medicaid covered services\nrendered Medicaid/Medicare eligible recipients.\n\n\n## I.A NON-DISCRIMINATION MEDICAID PROVIDERS\n\nParticipating providers of services under the Medicaid Program must comply with the\nrequirements of Title VI and VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Section 504 of the\nRehabilitation Act of 1973, the Age Discrimination Act of 1975, and the Americans\nwith Disabilities Act of 1990. Under the provisions of these Acts, a participating\nprovider or vendor of services is prohibited from making a distinction based on race,\ncolor, creed, national origin, age, or disability. The State Agency is responsible for\ninvestigating complaints of non-compliance.\n\n\n## I.B. CONFIDENTIALITY\n\nInformation regarding Medicaid applicants and recipients is required to be\nsafeguarded. Confidential materials or categories of information relating to Medicaid\napplicants and recipients include, but not limited to the following:\n\n(a) Lists of names, addresses, and Medicaid eligibility numbers.\n\n(b) Medical services provided.\n\n(c) Social and economic conditions and circumstances.\n\n(d) Medicaid or other State or Federal government agency evaluation of personal\ninformation.\n\n(e) Medicaid data, including but not limited to, diagnosis and past history or\ndisease or disability.\n\n(f) Fair hearing records.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00002 -->\n\nInformation contained in the applicants/recipient\'s case record is confidential and\nshould not be released to the public. The exception to the policy is that if Medicaid\nhas an agreement with an agency then the Medicaid worker can provide the status of\nthe application and/or review. The agencies in which Medicaid has an agreement with\nare:\n\n(a) Alabama Department of Mental Health (ADMH)\n\n(b) Waiver Administering Agencies (Alabama Department of Mental Health (ADMH),\nAlabama Department of Rehabilitation Services (ADRS), Alabama Department of\nSenior Services (ADSS), and their agents such as the Area on Aging Agency\n(AAA).\n\n(c) Department of Human Resources (DHR)\n\n(d) Social Security Administration (SSA)\n\n(e) Children Health Insurance Program (CHIP)\n\n\n## I.C. THE DISTRICT OFFICE SYSTEM\n\nAlabama accepts the Social Security Administration\'s determination of eligibility for\nSSI money payments. Alabama uses the SSI rules as a basis for Medicaid eligibility for\nreasons of administrative simplicity and economy. Thus, we are a 1634 state.\n\nHowever, eligibility for segments of the SSI population remained undetermined by the\nstate. These included Medicaid benefits for those receiving only state optional\nsupplemental payments, the three-month retroactive eligibility determinations for SSI\nrecipients for the period prior to cash assistance, and eligibility determinations for\nthe institutionalized population. An institutionalized person loses SSI eligibility if\nhis/her income exceeds $50 per month, and a new application must be made so the\nState Medicaid Agency can redetermine eligibility.\n\nAlabama uses a district office system similar to that used by SSA as a model of\nadministration. This allowed the State Medicaid Agency to take responsibility for the\nprogram and to control the eligibility determination process for the highly expensive\ninstitutionalized population. This created a limited and specialized group of\ncaseworkers with the responsibility of determining Medicaid eligibility.\n\nThe caseworkers within the district office system determine eligibility for the\ninstitutionalized population, the SSI-related Medicaid only groups, and the Limited\nMedicaid groups. Out-stationed caseworkers in the district office, hospitals, and\nhealth centers provide easier access to Medicaid coverage for pregnant women and\nchildren with income at or below the required Federal Poverty Level.\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency believes eligibility determination to be as important to\nthe total program as claims processing. Through the district office system and the\nout-stationed workers, the Agency has direct-line command rather than indirect\ncontrol over non-cash eligibility determinations.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-3" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00003 -->\n\n\n## I.D. SOCIAL SECURITYADMINISTRATION\n\nThe Social Security Administration certifies aged, blind, and disabled applicants for\nthe Federal Supplemental Security Income (SSI) Program. In Alabama, individuals\neligible for SSI are eligible for Medicaid under Title XVI, Section 1902(b) of the Social\nSecurity Act and Section 1634, Public Law, 92-603.\n\n\n## D.2. DEPARTMENT OF HUMAN RESOURCES\n\nThe Alabama Department of Human Resources (DHR) certifies eligibility at county DHR\noffices for the following groups not eligible for SSI:\n\nD.2.a. Children who receive Federal or State adoption assistance.\n\nD.2.b. Individuals eligible under standards in effect August 1972\nand thereafter, to receive cash assistance under a state public assistance program,\nexcept for subsequent increase in income solely from increase in Social Security\nmonthly benefits.\n\nD.2.c. Individuals eligible under standards in effect August 1972\nand thereafter, to receive cash assistance under a state public assistance program,\nexcept for subsequent increase in income solely from increase in Social Security\nmonthly benefits.\n\nD.2.d. Individuals not eligible for SSI who, on December 1, 1973,\nwere institutionalized and continue to need the care for which institutionalized and\nmeet eligibility standards in effect December 31, 1973\n\nD.2.e. Aged, blind, and disabled individuals not eligible for SSI and\nwho receive a state cash supplement from DHR and meet SSI resource criteria (SUP).\n\n\n### D.2.f. Foster children\n\n\n## D.3. ALABAMA MEDICAID AGENCY\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency certifies Medicaid eligibility for the following groups:\n\nD.3.a. Individuals who would be eligible for SSI and individuals\nwho have been residents for 30 consecutive days or longer in a hospital or nursing\nhome and have an income up to three (3) times the Federal Benefit Rate (FBR) for an\nindividual (INSTITUTIONAL).\n\nWhen the Social Security Administration considers a hospital a public institution, the\nSocial Security Administration will deny applications for SSI, for claimants who are\nhospitalized, without making a disability determination. If the claimant has been\ndischarged home from a hospital considered a public institution, he/she should\nreapply for SSI and after a final determination has been made, a Retroactive\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-4" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00004 -->\n\nApplication should be submitted to the district office for the institutionalized period.\nHowever, if the claimant is institutionalized for 30 continuous days, is still\nhospitalized, and the hospital is considered to be a public institution, the district\noffice should process the case as they would any other institutional case.\n\nD.3.b. Applicants for SSI may be awarded eligibility for two\nmonths prior to the EO2 month. (RETROACTIVE)\n\nD.3.c. Individuals certified eligible by SSI as of July 1977 and\nsubsequent years that are eligible for Medicaid by continuing to meet SSI standards\nexcept for SSA cost of living increases. (PICKLE or CONTINUOUS)\n\nD.3.d. Those persons in a nursing home not eligible for SSI, and no\nlonger entitled to a state DHR money payment who, on December 31, 1973, were\neligible for DHR aid and continue to meet the December 31, 1973, eligibility criteria\nhave been "grandfathered" into the Medicaid program. (GRANDFATHERED)\n\nD.3.e. Children born to SSI eligible mothers up to one year of age\nas long as the mother remains in the home with the child. (NEWBORNS)\n\nD.3.f. Disabled adult children who lost Medicaid because of the\nreceipt of, or increase in, Social Security and meet criteria of Public Law 99-643.\n(DISABLED ADULT CHILD OR DAC)\n\nD.3.g. Disabled widows and widowers between the ages of 60 and\n65 who lost SSI because of receiving Social Security and who are not receiving\nMedicare and meeting the criteria of Public Law 100-203, Section 9116.\n(WIDOW[ER]S)\n\nD.3.h. Disabled Widows/Widowers between the ages of 50 and 59\nwho would be eligible for SSI except for entitlement to Social Security Resulting from\na change in the definition of disability and who are not eligible for Part A Medicare\nare deemed to be SSI recipients under Section 1634 of the Act. (DEEMED DISABLED\nWIDOW[ER]S)\n\nD.3.i. Aged, blind, and disabled individuals who are eligible under\nthe provisions of expanded Medicaid coverage of the Medicare Catastrophic Coverage\nAct of 1988, the group known as Qualified Medicare Beneficiaries. (QUALIFIED\nMEDICARE BENEFICIARY OR QMB)\n\nD.3.j. Aged, blind, and disabled individuals who are eligible for\nPart B Medicare under Amendment of the Omnibus Reconciliation Act of 1990 (OBRA\n\'90), the group known as Specified Low Income Medicare Beneficiaries. (SPECIFIED\nLOW INCOME BENEFICIARY OR SLMB)\n\nD.3.k. Elderly and disabled individuals in the community who\nwould be eligible for Medicaid in an institution because income and resources from\nparents or a spouse is not deemed available to them. (ELDERLY AND DISABLED OR\nDEEMING WAIVER)\n\nD.3.l. Individuals, under age 65, in the community who lost\nentitlement to Title II Disability Insurance Benefits due to excess earnings exceeding\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00005 -->\n\nthe Substantial Gainful Activity level and meet other requirements. Qualified Disabled\nWorking Individuals or QDWI)\n\nD.3.m.\nIndividuals, who are hospitalized for at least thirty\ndays, are under acute care for at least three days of the hospital stay, and are-\nawaiting placement in the nursing home may be eligible. (POST HOSPITAL EXTENDED\nCARE OR PEC)\n\nD.3.n. Residents of state facilities for individuals with intellectual\ndisabilities may be eligible for Medicaid to cover their cost of care. Eligibility\nrequirements are similar to those of the nursing home program. (FACILITY FOR\nINDIVIDUALS WITH INTELLECTUAL DISABILITIES)\n\nD.3.o. Individuals who are at least 18 years of age, and meet the\ncriteria for Medicaid coverage in a nursing home, but prefer to remain at home, may\nbe eligible for full Medicaid coverage at home. Limited funds are available. (STATE\nOF ALABAMA INDEPENDENT LIVING WAIVER OR SAIL)\n\nD.3.p. Individuals who are diagnosed as intellectually disabled,\nwho live in the community rather than in an institution, and meet the income,\nresource, disability, and medical criteria for an institutional care facility for the\nintellectually disabled may be eligible for this waiver. (INTELLECTUAL DISABILITIES)\n\nD.3.q. Individuals who are entitled to Medicare Part A benefits\nand whose income exceeds 120 percent of the federal poverty level but does not\nexceed 135 percent of the federal poverty level may be eligible as a mandated group\n(Balanced Budget Act of 1997 - Public Law 105-33, Section 4732). (QUALIFYING\nINDIVIDUALS- 1 OR QI- 1)\n\nD.3.r. Individuals who have attained the age of 21 with complex\nmedical conditions such as ventilator-dependency or a tracheotomy. (TECHNOLOGY\nASSISTANCE WAIVER FOR ADULTS)\n\nD.3.s. Individuals who have disabilities or long-term care\nillnesses in a nursing facility for 90 days or more and determined to be eligible for\ntransition into the community and do so within 180 days. (ALABAMA COMMUNITY\nTRANSITION)\n\nD.3.t. Individuals who have a diagnosis of intellectual disabilities\nor Intermediate Care Facility for Individuals with Intellectual Disabilities (ICF/IID)\nlevel of care, are not residing in a group home setting, or on the waiting list for\nintellectual disabilities services may be eligible for this waiver. (LIVING AT HOME)\n\nD.4.u. Individuals who reside at home and receive acute and long-\nterm care services. This is a managed care benefit provided by not-for-profit or\npublic entity. (PROGRAM OF ALL-INCLUSIVE CARE FOR THE ELDERLY).\n\nD.5.v Individuals who have a primary diagnosis of intellectual\ndisabilities with an IQ under seventy (70) that manifests before eighteen (18) years of\nage and does not require an institutional level of care. The Utilization Review Committee (URC) determines\nsanctions to be imposed on providers as well as restrictions and suspension against\nclaimants.\n\nProgram Integrity will notify the district office, nursing home, sponsor and claimant\nof the ineligibility by sending a termination letter. These suspensions are for one\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00012 -->\n\nyear and until the Medicaid recipient repays all of the misspent funds to the Alabama\nMedicaid Agency. FAIR HEARINGS\n\nThe State Plan provides that the office of the Governor, acting through Medicaid, will\nbe responsible for fulfillment of hearing provisions for all matters pertaining to the\nmedical assistance program under Title XIX. Federal law requires that an opportunity\nfor a hearing be granted to most claimants requesting a hearing because his/her\nclaim for medical assistance is denied, is not acted upon with reasonable\npromptness, or because he is aggrieved by any other agency action regarding his\nsituation. Alabama Medicaid Agency Office of General Counsel will make the decision\nas to whether the adverse action is due to State or Federal law.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-4" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00014 -->\n\nThe procedures contained herein have been adopted by Alabama Medicaid Agency to\nsettle formal complaints of persons who are receiving care under the Medicaid\nprogram or who have been denied care under this program because of eligibility\nstandards.\n\nThe hearing authority for all claimant fair hearings is the Commissioner, Alabama\nMedicaid Agency. The Hearing Officer will conduct the hearing and submit his\nfindings and recommendations to the Commissioner who will make the final decision\non each case. A copy of findings and recommendations is sent to the Alabama\nMedicaid Agency and to the appropriate district office manager. A hearing officer\nmust not have been involved in any way with the action in questions. His/her\ninvolvement in this hearing process should not begin until, as a last resort, the Office\nof General Counsel sends him/her the request for the hearing and directs him/her to\nschedule the fair hearing.\n\nFair hearings will usually be in the form of a face-to-face hearing by an impartial\nState Hearing Officer and attended by the complainant or his authorized\nrepresentative. However, any person or organization substantially affected by an\nexisting rule or statute governing the Alabama Medicaid Program may request a\nDocumentary Hearing. See Administrative Code, Chapter 3, for procedure.\n\n\n### B.1. HEARING REQUEST\n\nEach claimant requesting review of his/her case has free choice of whether such\nreview shall be handled through informal complaint and adjustment procedures or\nthrough a hearing. If he/she chooses a hearing, it cannot be delayed or canceled\nwithout his/her consent because of a review by the local office.\n\n\n#### B. 1.a. FORMAT OF REQUEST\n\nAny clear, written statement addressed to the Alabama Medicaid Agency\nindicating/stating that the claimant wants an opportunity to present his/her case to\na higher authority will be accepted. However, it must be determined by Alabama\nMedicaid Agency that the request is within the 60-day time limitation for acceptance\nof appeals, and the determination is made that he/she has a valid basis for appeal.\nThe claimant\'s request must state clearly, why he/she is dissatisfied.\n\n\n#### B. 1.b. WHO CAN REQUEST A HEARING\n\nThe claimant or someone acting on their behalf may make a request for a fair\nhearing. If someone files the appeal other than the claimant, or a person of his\nchoice, there must be a definite statement by the person making the appeal that it is\nbeing made upon authorization of the claimant.\n\nNOTE: The person making the appeal must have the legal authority to represent the\nclaimant, such as legal guardian, conservator, power of attorney, or appointed as\nrepresentative by signature on the agency appointment of representative form (Form\n202.) Medicaid\'s Office of General Counsel (OGC) will make the determination as to\nwho has the authorization to request the hearing. The district office role is to\nforward the hearing request, summary, and any supporting documents to the OGC.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00015 -->\n\n\n#### B. 1.c. WHO RECEIVES HEARING REQUESTS\n\nRequests for fair hearings may be sent to the pertinent district office. All requests\nshould be stamped in on the date received.\n\n\n### B.2. DISTRICT OFFICE PROCEDURES FOR PROCESSING HEARING REQUESTS\n\nUpon receipt of the hearing request, the Medicaid specialist should apply the hearing\nstamp to the front of the case record and fill in the available information. During\neach step of the hearing process, the specialist should add the additional\ninformation.\n\nNOTE: The actions in steps (1) through (3) below must be documented in the case\nrecord (Record of Contact Form).\n\nDetermine if benefits are to be continued or reinstated. If the appeal is received\nwithin ten (10) days of the effective date of action, along with a written request to\ncontinue the benefits, a case to be terminated may remain open and payment\ncontinued; or a liability amount to be increased may remain unchanged. Unreduced\npayments will continue in effect as long as the appeal is active and through the end\nof the month in which the final decision is reached. Eligibility dates on the computer\nshould show that eligibility was extended through the month in which the final\ndecision on eligibility was reached.\n\nException: Delays in conducting the hearing on the appeal caused by the claimant\nor his/her representative that were not due to justifiable causes, such as verified\nillness, will result in taking the action previously indicated on the case unless\navailable facts establish that different action is necessary. The Office of General\nCounsel will be responsible for notifying the claimant. The district office will make\nthis determination.\n\n· Contact the sponsor/person requesting the hearing and advise that if benefits\nare continued or unchanged pending the outcome of the hearing and the hearing\ndecision is in favor of the Agency, Alabama Medicaid Agency may recover from the\nclaimant or sponsor the costs of all benefits paid after the initial effective date of\nthe termination or change.\n\n· Request decision of sponsor to be submitted in writing.\n\n· Advise sponsor that an opportunity will be provided for a conference to discuss\nthe claimant\'s situation with agency staff to obtain an explanation of reasons for the\nproposed action and/or to present information to show that the proposed action is\nincorrect. During the conference the claimant may speak for himself/herself or be\nrepresented by legal counsel or by other spokesman of his/her choice. This\nconference does not alter the person\'s right to a fair hearing.\n\nThe district office caseworker and office manager should immediately review the\nrecord for completeness and accuracy. A written confirmation will be sent in 10 days along with a copy of\nthe withdrawal letter. The date the withdrawal request is received is the date the\nappeal will be considered withdrawn.\n\n\n## B.7. MEDICAID QUALITY CONTROL\n\nA continuing objective of federal, state, and local government is to ensure that\npublic assistance expenditures are made on behalf of eligible beneficiaries in the\ncorrect amount. The Medicaid Quality Control (MQC) system is a management tool\nfor meeting this objective in the Medicaid program. The MQC system is a joint\nfederal/state effort to obtain information on which to base policy and procedural\nchanges that will improve administration of the Medicaid program and control the\nexpenditures of Title XIX funds.\n\nThe Medicaid Quality Control system is based on reviews of statistically valid samples\nof cases selected from the Medicaid eligibility file. The MQC review activities are\ncarried out in accordance with federally established policies and procedures. For\ncase accountability purposes, Medicaid Quality Control should notify the district\noffice whether the requested record is for state or federal audit.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-9" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00019 -->\n\n\n## C.1. ADMINISTRATIVE PERIOD FOR CHANGES\n\nQuality Control procedures provide for a reasonable period for agencies to reflect\nchange in circumstances of eligibility groups. This period of time is referred to as\nthe Administrative period. For MQC purposes the Administrative period is the review\nmonth and month prior to the review month. The Administrative period provides for\ntime allowances such as: advance notice, claimant reporting, agency investigations,\nand agency-imposed time allowances for claimant action. MQC uses the case status\nfor the review month in determining the type of error, the dollar amount of the\nerror, and if an error would exist even with application of the Administrative period.\n\n. No error will be charged if the error is because the case record data, as the\nMQC review month, is incorrect or does not reflect a change in circumstances, which\noccurred during the review month (calendar or fiscal), or the month prior to the\nreview month.\n\n. However, an error would be charged if the case record data were adjusted\nincorrectly during this period.\n\n. An error would exist if the status were incorrect as of the review month and\nwould still be incorrect disregarding the change in circumstances that occurred\nduring the Administrative period.\n\nNOTE: The Administrative period does not apply to State policy changes.\n\nNOTE: The Administrative period does not alleviate the need for recoupment and\nprocessing of an Identification of Possible Overpayment form.\n\n\n## C.2. JUDGMENTAL DECISIONS\n\nIn cases where the error cited is a matter of individual, subjective interpretation of\nwritten policy, the action taken by the district office worker based on his or her\nprofessional judgment shall prevail. CMS has previously advised that in cases where\npolicy is obviously ambiguous and could be interpreted as the worker has interpreted\nit, Federal QC will not cite errors. The district office worker should state the issue\nand develop the steps that led to the judgmental decision.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-10" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00020 -->\n\n\n## II.D. SYSTEM INCOME ELIGIBILITY VERIFICATION\n\nFederally funded public assistance agencies had to develop a system for exchanging\ninformation regarding the income and resources of claimants with various state and\nfederal agencies. In order to prevent fraud and abuse by making a more accurate\neligibility determination, the Income Eligibility Verification System (IEVS) was\ndeveloped to handle this information.\n\n\n### D.1. DHR DATA MATCHES\n\nThe Department of Human Resources (DHR) was designated by the Governor as the\nsingle State Agency responsible for initiating tape matches for all programs including\nMedicaid, Food Stamps, and Public Assistance.\n\n\n### D.2. AGENCIES PROVIDING INFORMATION\n\nIEVS regulations require that Medicaid request information on claimants from the\nfollowing agencies:\n\n\n### D.2.a. SOCIAL SECURITY ADMINISTRATION (SSA)\n\nFrom SSA we receive:\n\nBENDEX data for claimants for whom the information has not previously been\nrequested. This is information about SSA benefit payments. The district offices have\non-line access to this file. Any discrepancies between BENDEX and CARES is sent by\nmonthly report to the district offices.\n\nBEER (BENDEX Earnings Exchange Record) data for claimants for whom the\ninformation has not previously been requested. This is information about net\nearnings from self-employment, military pay, interstate wages and retirement\nincome maintained by SSA. BEER data will be sent to the district offices on a\nseparate report. This information must be investigated and independently verified\nby the worker.\n\n\n### D.2.b. INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE (IRS)\n\nFrom IRS we receive information on earned and unearned income (interest on\nsavings, dividends, stocks, bonds, etc.). This is not tax-return information. This file\nis compiled from information submitted by the interest payer. The federal tax\ninformation, or FTI, is requested at application and annually thereafter for\nclaimants. The claimant match will be completed the month prior to\nredetermination. Reports are generated at the state office and are sorted by district\noffice and worker number. The printouts are then submitted to the appropriate staff\nfor action. FTI must be investigated and independently verified by the worker.\n\n\n### D.2.c. ALABAMA DEPARTMENT OF LABOR (ADOL)\n\nFrom ADOL we receive:\n\nInformation on claimants on a monthly basis for wages, and on a weekly basis for\nunemployment compensation. This data must be independently verified through the\nemployer.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00021 -->\n\nUnemployment Compensation (UC) data for all pending, active denied or terminated\nclaimants. Since ADOL is the source of UC payment, it is not necessary to verify this\ninformation unless the claimant reports a discrepancy.\n\n\n### D.3. USE OF SOCIAL SECURITY NUMBERS\n\nThe agency must require, as a condition of eligibility, that each claimant for\nMedicaid services furnish his or her social security number.\n\nThe claimant must be advised of the uses the agency will make of the SSN, including\nits use for verifying income and eligibility. This notice is incorporated into the form\n204/205.\n\n\n### D.4. INVESTIGATIONS\n\nIEVS regulations require that within 45 days of receipt of information (by the state\noffice) from one of the sources listed above, the agency must take the following\nsteps:\n\n\\- Request verification if appropriate.\n\n\\- Determine whether the information affects eligibility or the liability amount.\n\n\\- Make an entry in the case file.\n\n\\- Notify the claimant of any adverse action.\n\nNOTE: IEVS/FTI may be disclosed only to an authorized individual, such as the\nclaimant and/or authorized representative/ sponsor or, in the case of a deceased\nclaimant, the representative/sponsor, the executor, administrator, or trustee of the\nestate. Therefore, when IEVS/FTI is received that prompts a contact with a financial\ninstitution, use the Asset Verification System (AVS). See Section II.E. for the use of\nAVS.\n\n\n### D.4.a. TIMELINESS REQUIREMENTS\n\nThe number of determinations delayed beyond 45 days from receipt of an item of\ninformation must not exceed twenty percent of the number of items of information\nreceived on all claimants. The state office will be responsible for monitoring\ntimeliness requirements. The district office managers are responsible for seeing that\nall information received is acted on promptly.\n\n\n### D.4.b. COMPLETION OF FORM 209\n\nIncome Eligibility Verification System Investigation Report (Form 209) is to be\ncompleted on each case in which information is received through IEVS. This report is\nsubmitted to the state office for statistical reporting to CMS. If the investigation\nreveals unreported or discrepant information, the form 209 should be separated.\nThe bottom portion should be completed and mailed in to the IVES coordinator and\nthe top part of the form should be maintained in the IVES case file.\n\n(1) If the information received is already contained in the case record and\npreviously verified, no investigation is required. The caseworker should make an\nentry in the case narrative to this effect and destroy Form 209 by shredding or\nincinerating.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00022 -->\n\nExample: FTI received on John Smith indicates that he received $60.00 interest in\nthe previous year from account #030607 at First Alabama Bank of Montgomery. The\ncase record reveals that this account information was furnished at application and\nhas been verified with the information received from AVS.\n\n(2)\nIf the information received is new to the case, it should be treated as a\nlead and investigated. Except for Unemployment Compensation Data, any\ninformation must be independently verified with the bank, employer, sponsor, etc.\nInformation may be discussed with the sponsor, claimant, or a third party after the\nindependent verification is received. The source of the information may be revealed\nat this time.\n\n(a)\nIf the information is proven to be invalid or valid but has no\nbearing on the case, make an entry in the narrative to that effect and destroy Form\n209 by shredding or incinerating. Some information received will be invalid due to an\nunverified Social Security Number. Hits received through ADOL may be wages earned\nby another person who is using our claimant\'s SSN in error. An Agency form letter has\nbeen developed to verify wages with employers. If the employer indicates on this\nform letter that the reported earnings were for another individual, we will accept\nthat as documentation that our claimant did not receive those earnings. If we have\nverified the SSN through BENDEX or MBR Query, we have no further responsibility to\ncorrect the problem of the SSN used in error.\n\n(b)\nIf the information causes the claimant to be ineligible or changes\nthe liability payment, notify the claimant in writing of the action to be taken, make\nappropriate entry in case record, complete form 209 and complete the Identification\nof Possible Overpayment form, as needed.\n\nD.5.\nSAFEGUARDING INFORMATION\n\nAll information received through IEVS is to be treated as confidential and restricted\nto purposes directly related to the administration of the Medicaid program. IEVS\ndata will be safeguarded as required in Alabama Medicaid Internal Memorandum No.\n206 "Release of Confidential Information" and the district office Security Plan.\n\nAll district offices have copies of Section 7213(a) of the Internal Revenue Code which\nmakes unauthorized disclosure of Federal return information a crime that may be\npunishable by a $5,000.00 fine, five years imprisonment, or both. District office\npersonnel will also have copies of Section 7431 of the Internal Revenue Code that\npermits a taxpayer to bring suit for civil damages for unauthorized disclosure of\nreturns and return information.\n\nAn on-site review of safeguard procedures will be conducted annually by the central\noffice IEVS Coordinator.\n\n\n#### D.6. STORAGE/FILING OF IEVS DOCUMENTS IN DISTRICT OFFICES\n\nPrintouts received through IEVS containing case information are to be filed in the\ncase record with the exception of FTI. Printouts containing FTI must be stored in one\ncentral locked filing cabinet. IRS will conduct field audits to assure that this\nrequirement is met.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-13" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00023 -->\n\nAs a condition for receiving FTI, the receiving agency must provide and maintain\nassurance that the information is being used only for the purposes of determining\neligibility and benefits for the programs it is administering. This must be completed\nto the satisfaction of IRS. The agency must implement safeguards designed to\nprevent unauthorized uses of the information and to protect the confidentiality of\nthat information. The following procedures are based on instructions for\nsafeguarding data found in IRS Publication 1075.\n\n\n### D.6.a. RECEIPT OF FTI\n\nUpon receipt of FTI, the assigned district office person will divide the FTI according\nto caseworker code. The FTI will be placed in individual file folders so that they will\nnot be commingled with other case records and will be thus identified as classified,\nconfidential information. The assigned person will place the folders in the secure\nlocation filed by worker number and in alphabetical order.\n\n\n### D.6.b. REVIEW\n\nThe caseworker will review the FTI and compare it to the existing information in the\nclaimant\'s case record to determine if an investigation and independent verification\nis needed. When the caseworker has completed the review of the FTI, the record\nwill be given to the district office manager who is the assigned Security Officer for\nthe district office. At the end of each workday, the worker must return the FTI to\nthe locked file cabinet. In the absence of the Security Officer, the assistant district\noffice manager may receive the records.\n\n\n#### D.6.c. SECURITY\n\nThe Security Officer will maintain and secure a key and locking file cabinet. All FTI\nrecords will be stored in this locked file cabinet. It will be the responsibility of the\nSecurity Officer to assure that no FTI folders are left on workers\' desks at the end of\nthe workday. If the worker has not completed review of the FTI, the record will be\nlocked up in the district office IEVS file until the next workday.\n\n\n#### D.6.d. REMOVAL OF RECORDS\n\nWhenever FTI records are removed from storage, they must be charged out to an\nindividual having a need for the information. The Security Officer will maintain a\nwritten register of the charge-out that includes date, name, and title of the person\nreceiving the record.\n\n\n#### D.6.e. MAINTENANCE OF RECORDS\n\nThe district office will maintain FTI records in the designated IEVS locked storage\nfile. The folders on the cases in which data continue to be received should be\nretained. If no FTI is received for the current year or the previous year, the FTI\npreviously received can be destroyed by shredding or incinerating. ASSET VERIFICATION SYSTEM\n\nSection 1940 of Title VII requires Medicaid to implement an electronic Financial\nInstitution (FI) Verification system for verifying the assets of the aged, blind, and\ndisabled Medicaid applicants and recipients. The Asset Verification System (AVS),\nwill be used in determining financial eligibility. The AVS will search for open, closed,\nlocal, and out-of-state financial accounts from disclosed and non-disclosed FIs.\n\n\n### E.1. TRANSFER OF ASSETS\n\nWhen comparing the response to what the applicant has reported, if the response\nreveals an applicant gave away or sold assets for less than its fair market value, then\nit is presumed that they were transferred for the purpose of qualifying or remaining\neligible for Medicaid. However, all transferred assets can be returned to the\napplicant to negate the transfer (as if it never happened). If only a partial amount of\nthe assets is returned, credit should be given for the partial amount returned.\n\n\n#### E.2. REBUTTAL OF TRANSFER\n\nThe applicant or recipient has the burden to rebut the presumption that the transfer\nwas made for the purpose of qualifying or maintaining Medicaid eligibility. The\napplicant or recipient must be informed and provided the opportunity to rebut the\npresumption prior to the imposition of a transfer penalty. The applicant must\nprovide clear and convincing evidence that the transfer was exclusively for another\npurpose. Acceptable evidence that the transfer did not occur for the purposes of\nqualifying or maintaining Medicaid eligibility may be a submitted on a "Statement of\nClaimant or Other Person" (Form 234). See also Chapter 6 Section 0.1.d.\n\n\n#### E.3. INELIGIBILITY WORKS UNDUE HARDSHIP\n\nIf the applicant admits to transferring an asset for less than fair market value for the\npurpose of qualifying for Medicaid, an exemption from a transfer penalty may be\ngiven if the applicant can demonstrate by clear and convincing verification that the\nimposition of a transfer penalty will cause them to suffer an undue hardship. This form requires a fee.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Other document as listed in the explanation that was created at least 5 years before the application for Medicaid</td>\n<td>This document must be one of the following and show a U.S. place of birth: - Bureau of Indian Affairs Roll of Alaska Natives - U.S. State Vital Statistics official notification of birth registration. - A delayed U.S. public birth record that is recorded more than 5 years after the person\'s birth. - Statement signed by the physician or midwife who was in attendance at the time of birth.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Institutional admission papers from a nursing home, skilled nursing care facility or other institution and was created at least 5 years before the initial application date and indicates a U.S. place of birth</td>\n<td>Admission papers generally show biographical information for the person including place of birth; the record can be used to establish U.S. citizenship when it shows a U.S. place of birth.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td rowspan="3">Medical (clinic, doctor, or hospital) record and was created at least 5 years before the initial application date and indicates a U.S. place of birth</td>\n<td>Medical records generally show biographical information for the person including place of birth; the record can be used to establish U.S. citizenship when it shows a U.S. place of birth.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Note: An immunization record is not considered a medical record for purposes of establishing U.S. citizenship.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Note: For children under 16 the document must have been created near the time of birth or 5 years before the date of application.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Citizenship Affidavit (applies to U.S. born citizens and naturalized citizens)</td>\n<td>Affidavits should ONLY be used in rare circumstances. The affidavit must be supplied by the applicant/recipient and at least two additional individuals of whom one is not related to the applicant/recipient. Each of the two additional individuals must attest to having a personal knowledge of the event(s) establishing the applicant\'s/recipient\'s claim of citizenship. The person(s) making the affidavit must be able to provide proof of his/her own citizenship and identity for the affidavit to be accepted.</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-v" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00032 -->\n\nA separate affidavit must be obtained from the applicant/recipient or\nother knowledgeable individual (person with legal authority to\nrepresent the claimant) explaining why documentary evidence does\nnot exist or cannot be obtained.\n\nAffidavits must be signed under penalty of perjury and need not be\nnotarized.\n\n\n<table>\n<caption>Table 2. 1 IDENTITY DOCUMENTS (ID)</caption>\n<tr>\n<th colspan="2">ID TABLE</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<th>Documents to Establish Identity</th>\n<th>Explanation</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Certificate of Degree of Indian Blood, or other U.S. American Indian/Alaska Native tribal document</td>\n<td>Acceptable if the document carries a photograph of the applicant/recipient, or has other personal identifying information relating to the individual.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td rowspan="2">Any identity document described in section 274A(b)(1)(D) of the Immigration and Nationality Act</td>\n<td>Use 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v)(B)(1). This section includes the following acceptable documents for Medicaid purposes: - Driver\'s license issued by the State either with a photograph of the individual or other identifying information of the individual such as name, age, sex, race, height, weight or eye color. - School identification card with a photograph of the individual.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>- U.S. military card or draft record. - Identification card issued by the Federal, State, or local government with a photograph or identifying information such as name, sex, weight, height, eye color, hair color, etc. - Military dependent\'s identification card. - U.S. Coast Guard Merchant Mariner card. Exception: Do not accept a voter\'s registration card or Canadian driver\'s license as listed in 8 CFR 274a.2(b)(1)(v)(B)(1).</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Database Cross Matches</td>\n<td>A cross match with a Federal or State governmental, public assistance, law enforcement or corrections agency\'s data system may be conducted to establish identity (i.e. child support, child protective services, juvenile detention, motor vehicle, etc.) if that agency establishes true identity.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Three or more corroborating documents</td>\n<td>Three or more corroborating documents (original or certified copies) such as marriage licenses, divorce decrees, high school and college diplomas from accredited institutions (including general education and high school equivalency diplomas), employer ID cards, and property deeds may be accepted to verify the identity of an individual_provided such documents have not been used to</td>\n</tr>\n</table>', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

GENERAL CATEGORICAL ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA\n\nIn order to qualify for Medicaid in a medical institution, the claimant must meet\ngeneral categorical criteria of age, disability or blindness; residence, and citizenship\nfor the appropriate coverage group. There are certain other general categorical\nrequirements that must be met by all claimants.\n\n\n## III.B. AGE REQUIREMENT\n\nTo be eligible if not disabled, the claimant must be 65 years of age or older. This\nfactor is based on Social Security verification, birth certificate, passport or census\nreport.\n\nException: If the claimant alleges, he/she is 70 years old or older, his/her statement\nmay be accepted. The case may be certified when all other points have been\nverified. Date of birth should be verified at review or during the next action on the\ncase.\n\n\n## III.C. DISABILITY\n\nIf under age 65, a claimant must be disabled. Disability is defined as the inability to\nengage in\nany substantial gainful activity by reason of any medically determinable physical or\nmental impairment(s) which can be expected result in death, or which has lasted or\ncan be expected to last for a continuous period of not less than twelve (12) months.\n\nAn impairment is likely to result in death if:\n\n· Based on established medical knowledge, and under the particular\ncircumstances in the\ncase, it is found to be terminal; or\n\n. It actually results in death.\n\nConsider a claimant (child or adult) disabled when he/she is receiving RSDI or SSI\nbased on his/her disability or blindness. For all other persons under age 65, the Social\nSecurity Disability Determination Unit or Medicaid Medical Review Team will establish\ndisability determination upon submission of competent medical testimony. Medical\ntestimony may be in the form of written reports or copies of medical records from\nhospitals, clinics, VRS, VA, industrial organizations, etc., or written statements from\nlegally licensed physicians. Medical information based on previous examination is\nacceptable if it clearly establishes current disability. DHR Determination for\nDisability can be accepted if it is a permanent or presumptive finding. The\npresumptive finding can be accepted because disabilities that are presumed to be\napproved for SSI are normally approved for SSA.\n\nNOTE: Application for Medicaid is accepted for individuals with presumptive\ndisabilities who are not applying for SSI.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00035 -->\n\nSpecial information may be used as needed in conjunction with medical information\nto establish that a disability exists. This information must be from a knowledgeable\nsource willing to have the medical data made available to all parties during a\nhearing.\n\nNOTE: When current medical examination is needed to obtain information about\nthe disability, a determination that the claimant meets other eligibility requirements\nare made before referring the claimant for a medical examination. (A payment not\nto exceed $15 may be authorized to a physician for completion of medical\nexamination).\n\n\n## III.D. BLINDNESS\n\nA claimant meets blindness criteria when his/her central visual acuity is 20/200 or\nless (with glasses) or a limited visual field of 20 degrees or less in the better eye. A\nclaimant determined to be blind for purposes of SSI is also considered blind for\nMedicaid eligibility purposes.\n\n\n## III.E. UNITED STATES CITIZENSHIP\n\nFor purposes of qualifying as a United States citizen, the United States, as defined in\nthe Immigration and Nationality Act, includes the 50 states, the District of Columbia,\nPuerto Rico, Guam, Virgin Islands, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Nationals from\nAmerican Samoa or Swain\'s Island shall also be regarded as United States citizens for\npurposes of Medicaid.\n\nApplicants for Medicaid must:\n\n· Indicate that he/she is a citizen or national of the United States or an alien in\na satisfactory immigration status (henceforth qualified alien) as provided in\nF.1 by signing the application.\n\n· All non-citizen claimants must provide documents to establish immigration\nstatus as described in F.1 from the United States Citizenship and Immigration\nServices (USICS).\n\nUse the documents presented to verify immigration status in accordance with the\nprovisions of F.1. We are required to provide Medicaid eligibility pending verification\nof immigration status to an individual who claims to meet requirements in paragraph\nF.1. or F.6. and who meets all other non-immigration Medicaid eligibility\nrequirements.\n\nMedicaid applicants/recipients who declare under penalty of perjury to be a citizen\nor national of the United States must provide documentation of citizenship and\nidentity.\n\nThe Deficit Reduction Act (DRA) of 2005 requires states to document U.S. citizenship\nand identity. Medicaid applicants/recipients, who declare under penalty of perjury\nto be a citizen or national of the United States, must provide documentation of\ncitizenship and identity. Only family members applying for or receiving medical\nbenefits are required to provide documentation.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00036 -->\n\nThe law specifies certain forms of acceptable documentary evidence of citizenship\nand identity and provides for the use of additional forms of documentation as\nestablished by federal regulations.\n\n\n### E.1. EXEMPT INDIVIDUALS\n\nThe citizenship and identity documentation requirement will not apply to individuals\ndeclaring to be citizens or nationals of the United States who are eligible for\nMedicaid and:\n\n· are entitled to or enrolled for Medicare benefits,\n\n· are receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) benefits,\n\n· are receiving Social Security benefits because of their own disability (Social\nSecurity Disability Income (SSDI) only)\n\n· with respect to whom child welfare services are made available under Title IV-\nB of Social Security Act; or\n\n· with respect to whom adoption or foster care assistance is made available\nunder Title IV-E of the Social Security Act.\n\nSupplemental Security Income (SSI) recipients and Medicare beneficiaries requesting\nMedicaid are exempt from the citizenship/identity documentary requirement since\nproviding this documentation is a condition of eligibility for the SSI and Medicare\nprograms. When an applicant/recipient indicates current SSI eligibility or Medicare\nentitlement, the worker should conduct a database cross match.\n\nNote: For SSI eligibility, the worker should check the SDX (CARES) and SVES. For\nMedicare entitlement, the worker should check the BENDEX, SVES, and/or TBQ. Any\nother documentation obtained from SSA that verifies SSI eligibility or Medicare\nentitlement is acceptable. ACCEPTABLE DOCUMENTATION\n\nThe law specifies certain forms of acceptable documentary evidence of citizenship\nand identity and provides for the use of additional forms of documentation as\nestablished by federal regulations.\n\nTo establish U.S. citizenship, documentary evidence must show:\n\n\\- A U.S. place of birth, or\n\n\\- That the person is a U.S. citizen.\n\nNOTE: Children born in the U.S. to foreign sovereigns or diplomatic officers are not\nU.S. citizens.\n\nTo establish identity, documentary evidence must show identifying information\nrelating to the person named on the document.\n\nWhen establishing citizenship and identity, a document must be sought first from a\nlist of primary documents Citizenship Documents (CD) (Table 1). These tables are\nlisted at the beginning of the chapter. If an applicant/recipient presents a primary\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-3" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00037 -->\n\ndocument listed in CD Table 1, no other information will be required. When a\nprimary document listed in CD Table 1 is not available, the eligibility worker must\nseek to obtain documentation from a secondary source listed in CD Table 2. If a\ndocument from CD Table 2 is not available, the worker will seek to obtain a\ndocument from CD Table 3, if unavailable, then CD Table 4. Documents in CD Tables\n3 and 4 should be obtained only when documents in CD Table 1 or CD Table 2 are\nunavailable as they are less reliable forms of documentation. When citizenship is\ndocumented using a document from CD Tables 2, 3, or 4 a document verifying\nidentity (Identity Table) is required.\n\nNOTE: During the interview process, the worker should progress sequentially from CD\nTable 1 through CD Table 4 with the applicant/recipient to obtain the most reliable\nform of citizenship documentation available and use the Identity Table (ID) for\nacceptable documentation of identity. Acceptable documents on each level are\nlisted below and outlined in more detailed in the CD Tables.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-4" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00038 -->\n\n\n#### E.2.a. PRIMARY DOCUMENTS (CD TABLE 1)\n\nPrimary evidence of citizenship and identity is documentary evidence of the highest\nreliability that conclusively establishes that the person is a U.S. citizen. If an\napplicant/recipient presents evidence from the list of primary documents CD Table 1,\nno additional document is required.\n\nAcceptable primary documentation for citizenship and identification includes:\n\no A U.S. passport or U.S. passport card. Exception: Do not accept any passport\n☐\nthat was issued with a limitation. However, such a passport may be used as\nproof of identity.\n\no A Certificate of Naturalization (Form N-550 or N-570).\n☐\no A Certificate of United States Citizenship (Form N-560 or N-561).\n☐\n\no Tribal documents (See CD Table 1 for specifics).\n☐\n\n\n#### E.2.b. SECONDARY DOCUMENTS (CD TABLE 2)\n\nSecondary evidence of citizenship is documentary evidence of satisfactory reliability\nand is used ONLY when primary evidence of citizenship is not available. Any of the\ndocuments below may be accepted as secondary evidence of U.S. citizenship if there\nis nothing indicating the person is not a U.S. citizen (i.e. lost U.S. citizenship).\nAdditionally, a second document from the Identity Table establishing identity must\nalso be presented.\n\nAcceptable secondary documentation to verify proof of citizenship includes:\n\no A U.S. public birth certificate issued before the person was 5 years of age.\n☐\n\no A Certificate of Birth Abroad (Form FS-545 or Form DS-1350).\n☐\n\n☐\no A United States Citizen Identification Card (Form I-197 or the prior version\nForm I-179).\n\no A Report of Birth Abroad of a Citizen of the United States (Form FS-240).\n☐\n\no An American Indian Card with a classification code "KIC" (Issued by the DHS to\n☐\nidentify U.S. citizen members of the Texas Band of Kickapoos living near the\nU.S./Mexican border) (Form I-872).\n\no A final adoption decree showing child\'s name and U.S. place of birth. Children\n☐\nborn outside the United States and adopted by U.S. citizens may establish\ncitizenship using the process established the Child Citizenship Act of 2000 (Pub.\nL. 106-395 enacted on October 30, 2000).\n\no Evidence of civil service employment by the U.S. government before June\n1976.\n\no An official U.S. military record of service showing a U.S. place of birth (e.g.\nDD-214).\n\no A Northern Mariana Identification Card (Issued by the DHS to a collectively\nnaturalized citizen of the U.S. who was born in the Northern Mariana Islands\nbefore November 3, 1986).\n\no A data verification with the Department of Homeland Security\'s SAVE program\nfor naturalized citizens.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00039 -->\n\n\n#### E.2.c. THIRD LEVEL DOCUMENTS (CD TABLE 3)\n\nThird level documents may be used ONLY when:\n\n· primary evidence cannot be obtained within the reasonable opportunity period,\nand\n\n· secondary evidence does not exist or cannot be obtained, and\n\n· the applicant/recipient alleges being born in the U.S.\n\nAdditionally, a second document from the ID Table establishing identity MUST also be\npresented.\n\nAcceptable third level documentation to verify proof of citizenship includes:\n\n\\- An extract of a U.S. hospital record on hospital letterhead indicating a U.S.\nplace of birth established at the time of the person\'s birth and created at least\n5 years before the initial application date. Note: For children under 16 the\ndocument must have been created near the time of birth or 5 years before the\ndate of application.\n\n\\- A life, health, or other insurance record showing a U.S. place of birth and\ncreated at least 5 years before the initial application date.\n\n\\- Religious record recorded in the U.S. within 3 months of birth. Note: The\nrecord must be an official record with the religious organization.\n\n\\- Early school record showing a U.S. place of birth.\n\n\n#### E.2.d. FOURTH LEVEL DOCUMENTS (CD TABLE 4)\n\nFourth level documents are of lowest reliability and should ONLY be used in the\nrarest of circumstances. This level of evidence is used only when primary evidence is\nunavailable, both secondary and third level evidence do not exist or cannot be\nobtained within the State\'s reasonable opportunity period, and the applicant alleges a\nU.S. place of birth. Additionally, a second document from the ID Table establishing\nidentity MUST also be presented.\n\nAcceptable fourth level documentation to verify proof of citizenship includes:\n\n· Federal or State census record showing U.S. citizenship or a U.S. place of birth\nand applicant\'s/recipient\'s age or date of birth.\n\n· Institutional admission papers from a nursing home, skilled nursing care facility\nor other institution and was created at least 5 years before the initial\napplication date and indicates a U.S. place of birth.\n\n· Medical (clinic, doctor, or hospital) record created at least 5 years before the\ninitial application date that indicates a U.S. place of birth. (For children under\n16, the document must have been created near the time of birth or 5 years\nbefore the date of application.).\n\n· Other document created at least 5 years before the application for Medicaid.\nThis document must be one of the following and show a U.S. place of birth:\nBureau of Indian Affairs Roll of Alaska Natives, U.S. State Vital Statistics official\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-6" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00040 -->\n\nnotification of birth registration, a delayed U.S. public birth record that is\nrecorded more than 5 years after the person\'s birth, or a statement signed by\nthe physician or midwife who attended the birth. PRESENTATION OF DOCUMENTS\n\nAll documents presented by the applicant/recipient must be either originals or copies\ncertified by the issuing agency. To be\nawarded Medicaid and then receive the 90-day reasonable opportunity period, the\nindividual must meet all other points of eligibility including verification of SSN.\nEligibility cannot be determined if the individual\'s SSN is not verified as enumeration\nis a point of eligibility that must be established.\n\nIf the data submitted by Medicaid matches, the SSN will be verified and SVES will\ndisplay a one-byte SSN verification code of "V", "F", "M", "P", "R", "X", or "Z". The\n"UD" code denotes a person who is not known to be a citizen and is now deceased.\n\nWhen the declaration of citizenship is consistent with data in SSA files, a copy of the\nSVES page showing citizenship code "DA" should be printed, placed in the file, and\nthe record of contact updated. (Code "DA" indicates the person is a citizen and\ncitizenship has been verified by SSA. Documentation of both citizenship and identity\nare considered met when a "DA" is indicated.) SVES citizenship code "DC" will\nrequire further investigation as this indicates the person was a citizen and the SSN\nbelongs to a deceased citizen.\n\nThe usual processing procedures should be followed when eligibility cannot be\ndetermined because the individual failed to provide C & I documentation and other\nnecessary information (i.e. income verification, insurance information, etc.) in\nresponse to a 30-day checklist. If the information is not received within the 30-day\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-10" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00044 -->\n\ntimeframe and additional time has not been requested, the case should be denied or\nterminated.\n\nNOTE:\n\nWhen a SVES data match is used to document citizenship for an individual who is\notherwise eligible and the data indicates the individual\'s declaration of citizenship or\nnationality is consistent with SSA\'s files, documentation of C & I is considered to have\nbeen met.\n\nWhen a SVES data match is not used to document citizenship, the individual should be\ngiven a 90-day reasonable opportunity period to provide satisfactory C & I documents.\nCD Table 1 through CD Table 4 should be used sequentially to obtain the most reliable\nform of citizenship documentation available. Individuals who meet the eligibility requirements of Medicaid\nbut are not citizens of the United States are Medicaid eligible only if they meet\nspecific requirements. Those non-citizens who do not meet the specific requirements\nmay be eligible to receive treatment for an emergency medical condition as defined\nin F.9.\n\nMedicaid eligibility for aliens is based on whether the alien is a qualified or non-\nqualified alien and whether their date of arrival in the U.S. was before, on, or after\nAugust 22, 1996 (date of welfare reform law). A qualified alien may or may not be\neligible. A non-qualified alien or an ineligible alien is only eligible for emergency\nservices. The previous categories of lawful permanent residents and aliens\npermanently residing in the United States under color of law (PRUCOL) no longer\napply.\n\n\n### F.1. QUALIFIED ALIENS\n\nA qualified alien is an alien who is a:\n\n-A lawful, permanent resident,\n-A refugee,\n-An asylee,\n\n-An alien who has had deportation withheld under section 243(H) of the Immigration\nand Nationality Act (INA)\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00045 -->\n\n-An alien granted parole for at least 1 year by the USCIS, or\n\n-An alien granted conditional entry under immigration law in effect before April 1,\n1980.\n\nThis definition eliminates most of the PRUCOL categories as well as PRUCOL as an\neligibility classification.\n\n\n## F.2. QUALIFIED ALIENS IN THE U.S. BEFORE 8/22/96\n\nAliens who are members of the following groups and who otherwise meet Medicaid\neligibility requirements are eligible for Medicaid if they were living in the United\nStates before 8/22/96:\n\n-A lawful permanent resident,\n\n-American Indian Born in Canada. An American Indian born in Canada may freely\nenter and reside in the U. S. and is considered to be lawfully admitted for permanent\nresidence if he/she is of at least one-half American Indian blood. As such, he/she is a\nqualified alien. This does not include a spouse or child of such an Indian nor a non-\ncitizen whose membership in an Indian tribe or family is created by adoption, unless\nsuch person is of at least 50 percent or more Indian blood. Such aliens are to be\nconsidered qualified aliens because they are considered lawful permanent residents.\nNOTE: Examples of acceptable types of documentation are birth or\nbaptismal certificate issued on a reservation, Tribal records, letter from\nthe Canadian Department of Indian Affairs, or school records.\n\n-A refugee (207 of INA),\n\n-An asylee (208 of INA),\n\n-An alien who has had deportation withheld under 243(h) of the Immigration and\nNationality Act (INA),\n\n-An alien granted parole for at least 1 year by the CIS (21(d)(5) of INA),\n\n-An alien granted conditional entry under 203(a)(7) of the immigration law in effect\nbefore April 1, 1980,\n\n-A battered immigrant who meets certain requirements,\n\n-Honorably discharged veterans and aliens on active duty in the United States armed\nforces and the spouse or unmarried dependent child(ren) of such individuals,\n\n-Cuban and Haitian (208) entrants; and\n\n-Amerasian (209) immigrants.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00046 -->\n\n\n## F.3. QUALIFIED ALIENS IN THE U.S. ON OR AFTER 8/22/96\n\nAliens entering the U.S. on or after 8/22/96 who are members of the following groups\nand who otherwise meet Medicaid eligibility criteria are eligible for Medicaid:\n\n-Refugees (207 of INA) for 7 years from date of entry,\n\n-Asylees are eligible for 7 years from date of entry,\n\n-Aliens whose deportation has been withheld under 243(h) of the INA for 7 years from\ngrant of withholding,\n\n-Honorably discharged veterans and aliens on active duty in the United States\' armed\nforces and the spouse or unmarried dependent child(ren) of such individuals\nNOTE: Acceptable documentation as determined by Department of\nDefense and VA of honorable discharge or active duty status include\nthe following documents:\n\nDischarge: An original of the veteran\'s discharge papers,\nDD Form 214, issued by the branch of service in which the applicant\nwas a member. NOTE: Discharge must indicate "Honorable." Cannot\nbe "Under honorable conditions" or any other wording.\n\nActive Duty Military: An original of the applicant\'s current\norders posting the applicant to a military, air, or naval base.\n\nA self-declaration under penalty may be accepted pending\nreceipt of acceptable documentation;\n\n-Cuban and Haitian (208) entrants are eligible for 7 years from date of entry,\n\n-Amerasian (209) immigrants for 5 years from date of entry; and\n\n-Lawful permanent residents (LPR) who have been in this country at least 5 years.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

EMERGENCY MEDICAL CONDITION TREATMENT DEFINED\n\nNon-qualified and ineligible aliens who meet the residence and other Medicaid criteria\nwill be eligible for Medicaid only for treatment of medical conditions meeting the\nfollowing definition:\n\n(a) To be eligible for emergency services, an alien must meet all eligibility\nrequirements for a Medicaid program except the declaration of satisfactory\nimmigration status and verification of that status:\n\n(b) Such care and services are necessary for the treatment of an emergency\nmedical condition of the alien, as defined below, provided such care and services are\nnot related to either an organ transplant procedure or routine prenatal or post-\npartum care; and\n\n(c) The alien has, after sudden onset, a medical condition (including emergency\nlabor and delivery) manifesting itself by acute symptoms of sufficient severity\n(including severe pain) such that the absence of immediate medical attention could\nreasonably be expected to result in:\n\n\\- Placing the patient\'s health in serious jeopardy,\n\n\\- Serious impairment to bodily functions, or\n\n\\- Serious dysfunction of any bodily organ or part.\n\nNOTE: All labor and delivery is considered emergency labor and delivery.\n\n\n#### F.11. EMERGENCY MEDICAL SERVICES FOR ALIENS\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency may authorize coverage of services provided to\nqualified and non-qualified aliens who are ineligible because of their alien status only\nif these services are related to an injury or illness which constitutes a medical\nemergency. Follow-up care for emergency conditions as well as non-emergency\nconditions is not covered. It is the responsibility of the alien who received emergency\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-22" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00056 -->\n\nservices to apply for coverage of emergency care through one of the district offices or\nany of the outstation worker locations. The alien must specify who provided\nemergency services and where they were provided in order for the Medicaid Agency to\nmake necessary verification of the medical care received. A Medicaid representative\nmust receive requests for emergency care by the end of the third month after the\nmonth of service to be considered.\n\nNOTE: When fraudulent or falsified documents are presented at application in the\ndistrict office, the application should be denied, and the case referred to the\nMedicaid Fraud Hotline. Refer to Chapter 2 Section A. 1.a. for action on existing (on-\ngoing) cases.\n\n\n#### F.12. EMERGENCY MEDICAID SERVICES PROCEDURES\n\nThe district office and out-stationed caseworkers will certify qualified and non-\nqualified aliens who are ineligible because of their alien status but are financially and\ncategorically eligible (except for enumeration and citizenship or alien status\nrequirements) for emergency medical services. They will document specific services\nfor which the alien is requesting payment.\n\nFor illegal aliens, immigrants, or non-qualified aliens, the individual must meet\nMedicaid\'s emergency services criteria. All other points of eligibility must be met\nexcept having an SSN, prior to sending to Central Office. This must be documented in\nthe case record. Copies of the medical bills incurred for the emergency must also be\nsent. The district office worker completes the top section and the section for "Cases\nrequesting Emergency Services" on the form 256. This information should be faxed or\nscanned/emailed to the appropriate Western or Eastern Region electronic mailbox.\nOnce a final determination has been made, the administrative support or designated\nstaff in the Western or Eastern Director\'s office will forward the information, award,\nand denial notices to the applicant and fax a copy of the award/denial to the district\noffice worker along with a "cc" to the appropriate district office manager.\n\nNOTE: If both a disability determination and an emergency services determination\nare needed, complete the sections for "Cases requesting a disability determination"\nand "Cases requesting Emergency Services." This information should be faxed or\nscanned/emailed to the administrative support staff in the Western or Eastern\nDirector\'s Office.\n\nWhen a request for Disability Determination and/or Emergency Services is received in\nthe Western or Eastern Director\'s office:\n\n1\\. Log request in on spreadsheet located in MediShare under the Western Region\nBeneficiary Services tab; under Eligibility and LTC Determinations.\n\n2\\. Email or scan the information to the Medical and Quality Review Office.\n\n3\\. Completed disability determinations will be returned to this office by Medical and\nQuality Review Office staff if the disability is denied. If the disability &/or the\nemergency service are approved, the record is forwarded to the Medical Services\nDivision and will be returned by Medical Services. NOTE: Medical documentation is\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-23" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00057 -->\n\nkept in the Medical and Quality Review Office for denied disability &/or emergency\nservices.\n\n4\\. Fax or scan/email the Form 256 to the district office worker.\n\n5\\. Update the Eligibility and LTC Determinations spreadsheet in MediShare.\n\n\n## F.13. INELIGIBLE ALIENS\n\nSome aliens may be lawfully admitted to the United States but only for a temporary\nor specified period of time as legal non-immigrants. These aliens are never qualified\naliens. However, in some cases an alien in a currently valid non-immigrant\nclassification may meet the State residence rules. When this is the case, such an\nalien is eligible for Medicaid for the treatment of emergency medical conditions as\ndefined in F.9 if the individual also meets the other eligibility criteria of a Medicaid\nprogram.\n\nAmong otherwise ineligible aliens are visitors, tourists, some workers, and diplomats\nwho are currently lawfully admitted as legal non-immigrants. These aliens would\nhave the following types of INS documentation: Form I-94 Arrival-Departure Record;\nForm I-185, Canadian Border Crossing Card; Form I-186, Mexican Border Crossing Card;\nForm SW-434, Mexican Border Visitor\'s Permit; Form I-95A, Crewman\'s Landing\nPermit. These aliens are not eligible for Medicaid because of the temporary nature of\ntheir admission status. The following categories of individuals are ineligible aliens:\n\n· Foreign government representatives on official business and their families and\nservants,\n\n· Visitors for business or pleasure, including exchange visitors,\n\n· Aliens in travel status while traveling directly through the U.S.,\n\n· Crewmembers on shore leave,\n\n· Treaty traders, investors, and their families,\n\n· Foreign students,\n\n· International organization representatives and personnel and their families and\nservants,\n\n· Temporary workers including agricultural contract workers, and\n\n· Members of foreign press, radio, film, or other information media and their\nfamilies.\n\n\n## III.G. STATE RESIDENT\n\nA claimant must be a resident of Alabama during the period covered by application,\nmust indicate intent to remain in Alabama, and must be capable of indicating such\nintent. For those who are temporarily away from the state, for example military\nassignment, Alabama is still their state of residence.\n\nNOTE: If an applicant is a resident of Alabama but their Medicaid has not terminated\nin another state, it does not prohibit them from applying for and being approved for\nAlabama Medicaid. It is the other state\'s responsibility to terminate their coverage.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-24" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00058 -->\n\nThe other state\'s failure to do so will not prevent the applicant from having access to\nmedical coverage and services in the state they currently reside.\n\n\n## G.1. ABILITY TO STATE INTENT.\n\nA claimant is considered incapable of stating intent if he/she meets any of the\nfollowing conditions:\n\n(a) His/her IQ is 49 or less or he/she has a mental age of 7 or less, based on\ntests acceptable to the intellectual disability agency in the state.\n\n(b) Medical documentation obtained from a physician, psychologist, or other\nperson licensed by the state in the field of intellectual disability supports the\nclaimant is incapable of indicating intent.\n\n(c) Is judged legally incompetent.\n\n\n## G.2. NON-INSTITUTIONAL RESIDENT.\n\nIf a claimant is not in an institution and under 21 years of age and incapable of stating\nintent, his/her state of residence is the state where he/she is living.\n\n\n## G.3. NON-INSTITUTIONAL RESIDENT.\n\nIf a claimant is not in an institution and over 21 years of age, his/her state of\nresidence is;\n\n(a) The state where he/she is living with the intent to remain (including if\nwithout a fixed address).\n\n(b) The state where he/she is employed or seeking employment (whether\ncurrently employed or not).\n\n(c) The state where he/she is living but not capable of stating intent to remain.\n\n\n## G.4. APPLICATION\n\nWhen it is determined the claimant is a resident of another state, for the period for\nwhich he/she is applying for assistance, the application will be denied unless there is\nan interstate residency agreement. ELIGIBILITY FOR OTHER PROGRAM BENEFITS\n\nAs a condition of eligibility, a claimant is required to apply for any payments or\nbenefits from other sources for which he/she may be eligible. A claimant ceases to\nbe eligible if he fails to take appropriate steps to apply for and, if eligible, to obtain\nsuch benefits. See Income, Chapter VII, A. 3rd paragraph, for details.\n\n\n## III.J. ASSIGNMENT OF THIRD-PARTY PAYMENTS\n\nThird party benefits are any benefits for which an entity is or may be liable to pay all\nor part of the medical costs of a claimant of Medicaid. To be eligible for Medicaid, an\napplicant/recipient must assign all third-party benefits to the state.\n\nAn applicant/recipient must cooperate in identifying third party coverage as well as\nprovide information and assist the Medicaid Agency in pursuing those benefits. If an\napplicant/recipient refuse to provide information, he/she must be advised by the\nworker of his/her rights to request a good cause exemption to cooperate with\nMedicaid.\n\nGood cause is where an applicant/recipient has a valid reason for not participating in\na program requirement.\n\nExamples of good cause are:\n\n\\- if the applicant states that it is against the child\'s best interest or\n\n\\- there is a threat of physical or mental harm if they cooperate with identifying third\nparty coverage or obtaining payments for medical care.\n\nIf an applicant thinks they are in danger of bodily harm or emotional damage, then\nevidence must be submitted to substantiate this claim.\n\nWhen a request for a good cause exemption is made due to circumstances that may\nbe against the child\'s best interest or physical or emotional harm, the factors listed\nbelow should be considered.\n\n\\- Cooperation is reasonably anticipated to result in physical or emotional harm\nof a serious nature to the child for whom support is sought or to the adult with\nwhom the child is living, to the point of impairing the adult capacity to care\nfor the child adequately, or\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-27" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00061 -->\n\n\\- At least one of the following circumstances exists and proceeding with Third\nParty assignment activities would, in a particular case, be detrimental to the\nindividual for whom Third Party assignment is sought.\n\n\\- The child was conceived because of incest or forcible rape.\n\n\\- Legal proceedings for adoption of the child are pending before a court of\ncompetent jurisdiction.\n\n\\- The applicant/recipient is currently being assisted by a public or licensed\nprivate social agency to resolve the issue of whether to keep the child or\nrelinquish for adoption, and the discussions have not gone for more than three\nmonths.\n\nExemptions for claim of emotional harm factors:\n\n\\- The present emotional state of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- The emotional health history of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- Intensity and probable duration of the emotional impairment;\n\n\\- The degree of cooperation to be required; an\n\n\\- The extent of involvement of the child in the paternity establishment or\nsupport enforcement activity to be undertaken.\n\nExemptions for claim of physical harm factors:\n\n\\- Based on either actual or anticipated physical abuse to the client and/or child which\nwould result if cooperation were required.\n\n\\- An investigation will be conducted only when the applicant/recipient states there is\nno documentary evidence to substantiate the good cause claim, such as in the case of\n"battered wives" who have not told anyone about
