INSTITUTIONALIZED 21 OR OLDER INCAPABLE OF INDICATING INTENT\n\nFor any institutionalized claimant who became incapable of indicating intent at or\nafter age 18, the state of residence is the state in which the claimant is physically\npresent, except where another state makes a placement.\n\n\n## G.6. OTHERS\n\nFor any other institutionalized claimant, the state of residence is the state where the\nclaimant is living with the intention to remain there.\n\n\n## G.7. INTERSTATE RESIDENCY AGREEMENTS\n\nThe only time the above residence requirements would not be in effect would be\nwhen the state entered into a residency agreement with another state. When this has\noccurred, the state where the claimant physically resides is his residence for Medicaid\npurposes.\n\nStates with which Alabama has entered into agreement are:\n\n\n<table>\n<tr>\n<th>California</th>\n<th>Georgia</th>\n<th>Iowa</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Pennsylvania</td>\n<td>Texas</td>\n<td>Wisconsin</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\nWhere two or more states cannot resolve which state is the state of residence, the\nstate where the claimant is physically located is the state of residence.\n\n\n## G.8. APPLICATION\n\nWhen it is determined the claimant is a resident of another state, for the period for\nwhich he/she is applying for assistance, the application will be denied unless there is\nan interstate residency agreement. The notice of denial should:\n\n(1) contain an explanation to the guardian or responsible relative as to the\nactual state of residence and how this was determined, and\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-26" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00060 -->\n\n(2) advise the family to apply for Medicaid in the other state.\n\n\n## III.H. MEDICAL APPROVAL\n\nThe nursing facility claimant must be certified financially and as needing the level of\ncare received. Medicaid district offices determine and certify claimants for financial\neligibility. Alabama Medicaid Agency will not authorize payment when it has been\ndetermined that the claimant does not meet the medical need requirements for the\nlevel of care required.\n\n\n## III.I. ELIGIBILITY FOR OTHER PROGRAM BENEFITS\n\nAs a condition of eligibility, a claimant is required to apply for any payments or\nbenefits from other sources for which he/she may be eligible. A claimant ceases to\nbe eligible if he fails to take appropriate steps to apply for and, if eligible, to obtain\nsuch benefits. See Income, Chapter VII, A. 3rd paragraph, for details.\n\n\n## III.J. ASSIGNMENT OF THIRD-PARTY PAYMENTS\n\nThird party benefits are any benefits for which an entity is or may be liable to pay all\nor part of the medical costs of a claimant of Medicaid. To be eligible for Medicaid, an\napplicant/recipient must assign all third-party benefits to the state.\n\nAn applicant/recipient must cooperate in identifying third party coverage as well as\nprovide information and assist the Medicaid Agency in pursuing those benefits. If an\napplicant/recipient refuse to provide information, he/she must be advised by the\nworker of his/her rights to request a good cause exemption to cooperate with\nMedicaid.\n\nGood cause is where an applicant/recipient has a valid reason for not participating in\na program requirement.\n\nExamples of good cause are:\n\n\\- if the applicant states that it is against the child\'s best interest or\n\n\\- there is a threat of physical or mental harm if they cooperate with identifying third\nparty coverage or obtaining payments for medical care.\n\nIf an applicant thinks they are in danger of bodily harm or emotional damage, then\nevidence must be submitted to substantiate this claim.\n\nWhen a request for a good cause exemption is made due to circumstances that may\nbe against the child\'s best interest or physical or emotional harm, the factors listed\nbelow should be considered.\n\n\\- Cooperation is reasonably anticipated to result in physical or emotional harm\nof a serious nature to the child for whom support is sought or to the adult with\nwhom the child is living, to the point of impairing the adult capacity to care\nfor the child adequately, or\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-27" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00061 -->\n\n\\- At least one of the following circumstances exists and proceeding with Third\nParty assignment activities would, in a particular case, be detrimental to the\nindividual for whom Third Party assignment is sought.\n\n\\- The child was conceived because of incest or forcible rape.\n\n\\- Legal proceedings for adoption of the child are pending before a court of\ncompetent jurisdiction.\n\n\\- The applicant/recipient is currently being assisted by a public or licensed\nprivate social agency to resolve the issue of whether to keep the child or\nrelinquish for adoption, and the discussions have not gone for more than three\nmonths.\n\nExemptions for claim of emotional harm factors:\n\n\\- The present emotional state of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- The emotional health history of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- Intensity and probable duration of the emotional impairment;\n\n\\- The degree of cooperation to be required; an\n\n\\- The extent of involvement of the child in the paternity establishment or\nsupport enforcement activity to be undertaken.\n\nExemptions for claim of physical harm factors:\n\n\\- Based on either actual or anticipated physical abuse to the client and/or child which\nwould result if cooperation were required.\n\n\\- An investigation will be conducted only when the applicant/recipient states there is\nno documentary evidence to substantiate the good cause claim, such as in the case of\n"battered wives" who have not told anyone about physical harm to which they have\nbeen subjected, or when the application is not credible without supportive evidence.\n\nThe following is a list of documentary evidence that is necessary to support the good\ncause claim:\n\n\\- A birth certificate, medical or law enforcement documentation.\n\n\\- Court documents or other court recordings.\n\n\\- Written statement from a government service unit or licensed private agency.\n\n\\- Medical documentation that indicate the current/past emotional or physical health\nhistory of the adult or the child for whom support would be sought. Or written\nstatements from a mental health professional indicating a diagnosis or prognosis\nconcerning the emotional health of the adult or the child for whom the Third-Party\nassignment would be sought.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-28" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00062 -->\n\n\\- Court, medical, criminal, social services, psychological, law enforcement records or\nservice case records in the Department of Human Resources.\n\n\\- Sworn statement(s) from individual(s), other than the applicant/recipient, with\npersonal knowledge of the circumstance(s) which provide the basis for the good cause\nclaim. ELIGIBILITY THROUGH ANOTHER AGENCY\n\nWhen a claimant becomes eligible for Medicaid through another agency, Medicaid\nterminates the case. District offices should send a Notice of Action to claimants\nadvising them of their termination due to coverage through another agency.\n\n\n## III.L. REQUIREMENTS FOR ELIGIBILITY UNDER A SPECIAL INCOME LEVEL\n\nEligibility under a special income level is not effective until the claimant has been in\na medical institution for a period of thirty continuous days. A "period of 30\ncontinuous days" is defined as being from the day of admission to the medical\ninstitution and ending no earlier than 12:00 midnight of the thirtieth day following the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-29" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00063 -->\n\nbeginning of the period. REDETERMINATIONS OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nThere must be a review of conditions of eligibility at least annually to make sure that\na claimant is still eligible.\n\n\n## III.O. THIRTY (30) CONTINUOUS DAYS\n\nThe special income test (institutional income standard) is effective with the\ncompletion of 30 continuous days of institutionalization in a medical institution.\nMedicaid eligible claimants (those receiving SSI and/or Optional State\nSupplementation or those potentially eligible for SSI or State Supplementation),\nliability amount should be zero ("0") during the partial month with liability being\nshown the following month. With all other claimants, the liability amount should be\nshown when institutionalized residence is established (30 continuous days\nrequirement is met).\n\nNOTE: Premature or newborn infants applying for disability through SSI who are born\nin a hospital are treated as individuals. There is no deeming from parents while\ninfant remains in hospital.\n\n\n## III.U. PERIODS OF ENTITLEMENT\n\nAccrual rights are those rights of claimants to have their eligibility for Medicaid\ndetermined for certain minimum periods.\n\nAny child under age 19 will have continuous Medicaid eligibility for 12 months,\nregardless of any changes in circumstances, after the initial award or a\nredetermination, unless the child moves out of state, becomes 19 years of age, or\ndies. Children under the age of 19 will have their Medicaid eligibility continued for up\nto 12 months, or until their 19th birthday, from the time of award or redetermination\nof eligibility. Redeterminations are conducted at least once per year. Medicaid\nrecipients are still required to report changes in their situation.\n\nContinued SSI cases will have a DC Tracking Code of "CE" if they have been terminated\nfrom SSI and will continue Medicaid. The D.O. Review date will be the date the case\nwill terminate. Department of Youth Services cases will have DC Tracking Code of\n"DY" while are in custody of DYS.\n\n\n## III.V. RETROACTIVE COVERAGE\n\nThe accrual rights for Medicaid in an institution is the three (3) month period\nimmediately preceding the month in which the application was received provided:\n\n\n## V.1. ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS MET\n\nAll eligibility requirements are met for each month. The criteria of eligibility used in\nmaking this determination will be dependent on claimant\'s living arrangement during\nthat period of time.\n\n\n## V.2. INCURRED MEDICAL EXPENSE\n\nClaimant has incurred medical expenses for those months.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-31" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00065 -->\n\nNOTE: The Record of Contact must indicate if the claimant is eligible for Medicaid\nthree (3) months prior to the month of application or any subsequent month prior to\ncertification. PARTIAL MONTH\n\nIn determining Medicaid eligibility during the partial month in a medical institution,\nthe 30 continuous days requirement applies unless one of the following situations\nexists:\n\n\n## W.1. AT ADMISSION CLAIMANT RECEIVED OPTIONAL STATE SUPPLEMENT\n\nClaimant received Optional State Supplementation payment through the County\nDepartment of Human Resources at the time of admission to the medical facility, or\n\n\n## W.3. EXCEPTIONS TO 10 DAYS ADVANCE NOTICE</td>\n<td>13</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>IV.I.</td>\n<td>ACCRUAL RIGHTS</td>\n<td>14</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>IV.J.</td>\n<td>HEARING REQUESTS</td>\n<td>14</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>IV.K.</td>\n<td>OFFICE PROCEDURES</td>\n<td>14</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td></td>\n<td>K.1. FILING AND REPORTING</td>\n<td>14</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00068 -->\n\n\n# CHAPTER IV. APPLICATION AND REDETERMINATION\n\n\n## IV.A. APPLICATION PROCESS\n\n\n## A.1. OPPORTUNITY TO APPLY\n\nAll individuals wishing to make application for Medicaid must have the\nopportunity to do so, without delay. An application may be submitted by mail, in\nperson, fax, on-line, telephone, and other commonly acceptable forms of\ntechnology. Fax, electronic, and phone signatures are acceptable.\n\n\n## A.2. COVERAGE GROUPS DETERMINATION\n\nA claimant who applies for Medicaid under any category is entitled to have eligibility\ndetermined under all possible eligibility groups.\n\nWhen determining eligibility for a coverage group that provides limited Medicaid\nservices (i.e., QMB, SLMB, QI 1) or when an individual has been determined ineligible\nfor any coverage group, the worker should determine if the claimant may be eligible\nunder another coverage group [i.e., widow(er), DAC, Pickle, etc.]. The case should\nbe awarded under the coverage group that provides the most Medicaid services.\n\nNote: A fleeing felon who loses Medicaid because he is no longer actually receiving\nan SSI benefit could still be eligible for Medicaid provided, he meets the\nrequirements for some other group covered under the state\'s Medicaid plan.\nTherefore, benefits can be awarded under the limited program if the client meets all\nthe eligibility requirements.\n\n\n## A.3. EX PARTE\n\nWhen a claimant\'s Medicaid is terminated, the worker should determine if the\nclaimant might be eligible under another coverage group.\n\n\n## A.4. APPLICATION FORM\n\nThe official application form for institutional Medicaid programs is Agency Form\n204/205. The official application form for the limited coverage programs i.e. QMB,\nSLMB, and QI 1 and for the Qualified Disabled Working Individual Program (QDWI) is\nAgency Form 211. Agency Form 204/205 is used to redetermine eligibility for\ncontinued Medicaid benefits for former SSI recipients. These serve as sources of\nbasic data to obtain information necessary to clear eligibility for Medicaid programs.\n\nApplications for all programs are sent to the district office serving the county in\nwhich the claimant is residing at time of application.\n\n\n## A.5. REQUIREMENTS FOR APPLICATION\n\nAn application will be considered as a claim for Medicaid benefits if the following\nrequirements are met:\n\nA.5.a. An acceptable Medicaid Agency application must be\nsigned. Xerox or facsimile copies are also acceptable. The application process will\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00069 -->\n\nbegin when the Alabama Medicaid Agency receives a signed application. Support\npersonnel should inspect applications to ensure applications are signed. Unsigned\napplications should be returned and should not be entered into the Medicaid system,\nas they are not considered to be an acceptable application.\n\nThe date received will be the date the signed application is opened, and date\nstamped by Medicaid or Medicaid\'s representative or the date it is received online.\nThe date received is used as the date that protects the accrual rights. Exception:\nUpon receipt of an application that is mailed, use the date shown by the U.S.\npostmark as the filing date if using the date, the application is received will result in\na loss of benefits. If the postmark is unreadable or there is no postmark, use the\ndate the application is signed (if dated) or 5 days before receipt of the application,\nwhichever date is later.\n\nA.5.b. It must be filed at a Medicaid district office.\nEXCEPTION: Applications for waiver services programs must be filed through the\nappropriate provider agency (i.e., Department of Senior Services {E & D and TA\nWaivers}, Department of Rehabilitation Services {State of Alabama Independent\nLiving Waiver}, or Department of Mental Health {ID and LAH Waivers}).\n\nA.5.c. The claimant, or someone authorized to act on\nclaimant\'s behalf, must sign the application. A person who signs an application for\nsomeone else will be required to provide evidence of his or her authority to sign the\napplication for the person claiming benefits. This information must be verified prior\nto approving an application. A Xerox or faxed signature is acceptable.\n\nIf the claimant signs the application form using an "X" for his or her signature, the\nsignature must have two (2) witnesses or be notarized.\n\nAn Alabama Medicaid Agency employee may serve as an appointed representative for\nan individual applying for Medicaid. A worker cannot determine eligibility for a\nfamily member.\n\n\n## IV.B. REAPPLICATION\n\nIf an application has been denied and the claimant wishes to submit a new\napplication, the previously denied application may be used as a reapplication if the\nreapplication is made within 60 days of the previously denied application. The actual\napplication form that was denied may be used for the reapplication process.\n\nIf the reapplication is not made within 60 days, a new application form must be\ncompleted.\n\nExample: Application denied on October 10 for failure to provide information.\nSponsor visits district office in November to bring needed verification and make\nanother application. Because sponsor wishes to reapply and comply with agency\npolicy within 60 days, a new application form will not be necessary. In this case, use\nthe following procedure.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00070 -->\n\n\n## B.1. REAPPLICATION PROCEDURE\n\nUse the following steps when reapplication is applicable:\n\nB. 1.a. Mark through "Application" on page one of the denied\napplication and write "Reapplication" in red.\n\nB. 1.b. Have the claimant or sponsor sign and date the\n"Reapplication" form. This signature should be under the section explaining the\nresponsibilities of the person signing the form.\n\nB. 1.c. Indicate in the Record of Contact Form that the\nreapplication has been received.\n\n\n## B.2. ACCRUAL RIGHTS - REAPPLICATION\n\nAccrual rights for Medicaid will start with the date of the reapplication.\n\n\n# IV.C. REACTIVATIONS\n\nIn situations where a claimant has been terminated based on an annual review and\nthe claimant subsequently complies with policy, the Medicaid Worker may reactivate\neligibility without a new application form if the evidence is presented within 90 days\nof the termination notice. This same procedure would be applicable also when a\ncase is terminated for failure to provide information/verification due to a change in\nthe claimant\'s income or resources. If the requested verification is received within\n90 days of the termination notice, eligibility should be reinstated for previous months\nas long as all eligibility points are met in those months, this includes QMB cases.\n\n\n# C.1. REACTIVATION PROCEDURE\n\nIndicate in red in the Record of Contact the date of the reactivation. Document or\nexplain the receipt of verification or necessary information that reestablishes\neligibility.\n\n\n# IV.D. CLAIMANT\'S/ REPRESENTATIVE\'S RESPONSIBILITIES\n\nIt is the responsibility of the claimant or person with legal authority of the claimant\nfor Medicaid to provide information sufficient to establish the eligibility of each\nindividual for whom assistance is requested, including, but not limited to, the\nfurnishing of a social security number, and the establishment of age, residence,\ncitizenship, blindness, disability, and financial eligibility (income and resources) in\neach of the months in which Medicaid benefits are sought.\n\n\n# D.1. A copy\nof legal authority representative papers, a durable power of attorney, or similar\nevidence of authority must be provided. However, a facsimile copy of the Appointment of Representative form is\nacceptable.\n\nNOTE: In the case of an application accepted on a deceased individual, the\nAppointment of Representative form must still be completed.\n\n\n# IV.E. REDETERMINATIONS OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nAn eligibility redetermination (annual review) must be made for every claimant at\nleast once every 12 months. A 30-day checklist will be sent when requesting\nadditional information at annual review. If additional time is requested to gather the\ninformation, the case record should be documented to include the date of contact,\nwhy the information requested is not submitted timely and additional time given.\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency determines continuing eligibility and, if necessary,\nadjusts claimant\'s record to indicate available income and reflect change(s) in\nhis/her circumstances. A redetermination may be necessary before the annual\nreview for claimants whose circumstances have changed. An interim action may be\nnecessary at any time before a review.\n\nNOTE: In situations where a claimant has been terminated based on an annual review\nand the claimant subsequently complies with policy, the Medicaid Worker may\nreactivate eligibility without a new application form if the evidence is presented\nwithin 90 days of the termination notice. This same procedure would be applicable\nalso when a case is terminated for failure to provide information/verification due to\na change in the claimant\'s income or resources. If the requested verification is\nreceived within 90 days of the termination notice, eligibility should be reinstated for\nprevious months as long as all eligibility points are met in those months, this includes\nQMB cases.\n\n\n## F.1. CHANGES BETWEEN REVIEWS\n\nA change may be reported to the district office in many ways.\n\nAmong them are:\n\nF.1.a. Orally - Changes may come to the attention of the\nworker from the claimant, provider, or references in person or by telephone. Oral\nreports must be detailed in the Record of Contact.\n\nF.1.b. Correspondence - A letter from the claimant, sponsor,\nor notice from provider may be received.\n\nF.1.c. Intra-Agency - Other workers in the agency may notify\nAlabama Medicaid Office of a change in a claimant\'s situation.\n\n\n# IV.G. INQUIRY\n\nIf a case record is not established on an inquiry, complete a Record of Contact\nregarding what was discussed. ELIGIBILITY DETERMINATION PROCESS - INTRODUCTION\n\nAlabama Medicaid Agency is responsible for determining initial and continuing\neligibility. In determining eligibility, the Medicaid caseworker must show that every\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00073 -->\n\neligibility requirement is met. Each point of eligibility must be substantiated,\nverified, and documented in the case record.\n\n\n## H.1. PERSONAL INTERVIEW\n\nA personal interview is required for every application received by Alabama Medicaid\nAgency except for the Limited Medicaid programs.\n\nIt is not required, prior to denial, to have a personal contact i.e. face to face or\ntelephone with claimants who are active SSI recipients, Limited Medicaid programs\nwhere applicants do not have Medicare Part A coverage, and applicants who\nobviously have excess liquid resources.\n\nInterviews may be held with the applicant/authorized representative by telephone or\nin the district office. When a claimant cannot be interviewed personally, his\nrepresentative may be interviewed and may complete the form and sign for him/her.\nAn Appointment of Representative form is applicable.\n\nA personal contact (face-to-face or telephone) with the claimant or\nsponsor/authorized representative is necessary when an application is received with\ninsufficient information before negative action (denial) is taken unless multiple\nattempts have failed, or no telephone number is provided, and no response is\nreceived to written correspondence.\n\n\n## H.2. INVESTIGATION\n\nIn preparation for the investigation, clear the files for available records on the\nclaimant and study the material in the record to become familiar with the facts at\nhand.\n\n\n## H.3. DATA SOURCE\n\nThe claimant is the most important source of information in the eligibility\ndetermination or the redetermination of his/her eligibility. Expect him/her to\nprovide all and complete information as well as verification about his/her situation.\nIf he/she cannot provide sufficient evidence as needed, the eligibility worker should\noffer help in obtaining and securing verification. If the claimant does not respond to\nthe first written request for information within 30 days, the application will be\ndenied.\n\nIf the applicant/sponsor request additional time to gather the requested information,\ndocument the case record to include the date of contact, why the information\nrequested not submitted timely and, additional time given.\n\n\n## H.4. FAILURE OR REFUSAL TO COOPERATE\n\nWhen a claimant fails or refuses to give his/her authorization for the clearance of\nfacts (such as bank clearance) or fails or refuses to provide needed information\nwithin his/her capability, explain clearly why the information is needed and that\nhe/she has one of three choices:\n\nH.6.a. INTERPRETATION REQUEST\n\nIf the district office worker and manager have both thoroughly evaluated a case and\nneed a clarification of a policy (interpretation), an interpretation request should be\nprepared. All relevant attachments (documentation) should be listed on the\nInterpretation Request Form 238. The completed Interpretation Request should be\nsent to the Medicaid Central Office West or East Division. The interpretation request\nand all appropriate documentation should be forwarded to the Policy and Training\nUnit. The Policy and Training Unit will make an interpretation based on the\ninformation submitted or will refer the request to the Medicaid Office of General\nCounsel.\n\n\n### H.7.a. WHEN TO PROCESS INTERPRETATION REQUEST\n\nBefore submitting an interpretation request, the case must be either:\n\n\\- A pending application,\n\n\\- An active case, or\n\n\\- A denied case when a hearing has been requested or a reapplication is\nreceived.\n\nDo not send an interpretation request if:\n\n\\- You have received an inquiry and no formal application is made.\n\n\\- A case is denied or terminated, and the applicant has not requested a hearing\nor has not reapplied.\n\n\n#### H.8. STANDARD OF PROMPTNESS\n\nMedicaid is required to meet a federal regulatory standard of promptness in clearing\nall applications. No more than 45 days shall elapse between the date of application\nand the date the award/denial notice is mailed. (EXCEPTION: In order to secure\ndisability determination, the time limit for clearing disability applications shall be no\nmore than 90 days). The agency must document the reason for delay in processing of\nall applications beyond the specified time limit. The case narrative should be docu-\nmented. A decision as to eligibility on redeterminations will be made within 30 days.\n\n\n#### H.8.a. REASONS FOR EXCEEDING TIME LIMITS\n\nThe regulatory standards may be exceeded in certain instances:\n\n(1) The claimant and/or the agency, after repeated attempts, have failed\nto secure the necessary facts regarding eligibility and further time is indicated to\nmake the follow-up.\n\n(2) Circumstances not within the claimant\'s control or some administrative\nemergency that could not reasonably be controlled by the agency. The agency must\ndocument the reason for delay in the case record. Example: The 30-continuous day\nstay requirement has not been met.\n\n(3) The claimant requests additional time within the 10 days of advance\nnotice of negative action so additional information bearing on eligibility can be\nsecured.\n\nNOTE: The agency must not use the time standards -\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00079 -->\n\n(1)\nAs a waiting period before determining eligibility; or\n\n(2)\nAs a reason for denying eligibility when the delay is administrative and\ncaused by the agency.\n\n\n#### H.9. DISPOSITION - APPLICATIONS\n\nEvaluate all information at hand to determine if all points of eligibility are met.\nImmediately upon an eligibility determination, whether eligibility can be found,\nineligibility can be found, or eligibility cannot be determined, the Medicaid district\noffice must notify Medicaid claimants. A notice must be sent in advance of any\ndecision terminating or reducing Medicaid benefits.\n\n\n#### H.9.a. DISPOSITION - CERTIFICATION OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nCertify a claimant when the investigation is complete. The record of contact must\nbe documented to reflect that all points of eligibility are met. Notify the claimant in\nwriting of the disposition of his case.\n\nFor nursing home cases, send a copy of the award notice to the sponsor and nursing\nhome.\n\n\n#### H.9.b. DISPOSITION - DENIAL OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nDeny an application when a claimant fails to meet one or more requirements of\neligibility. Notify claimant in writing of the action taken giving reason(s) why the\naction was taken.\n\nDeny an application when the claimant cannot be located or requests that the\napplication be withdrawn. Send a Notice of Action to the claimant when he/she\nwithdraws his application in order to confirm his/her request. Applications that are\nwithdrawn should be retained in the case record.\n\nNOTE: Do not deny an application based solely on death of a claimant unless some\nresponsible person requests the application be withdrawn. Send notice to the\nsponsor or representative of a deceased claimant.\n\n\n##### H. 10. DISPOSITION/ REDETERMINATIONS AND/OR OTHER CHANGES\n\nWhen the information is complete, and it has been documented that there have been\nno changes in the claimant\'s circumstances, change the review date on the\napplication file.\n\nChanges that could affect eligibility may be discovered at review or at any time\nbetween the regularly scheduled reviews. Such changes must be treated promptly\nand efficiently. For all changes, follow procedures outlined below.\n\n(1)\nBefore action is taken to reduce or terminate Medicaid benefits, follow\nprocedures in Advance Notification of Action paragraph below.\n\n(2)\nProcess Application File Page (AFP) as a change of status to reflect any\nchange. Show the review as being completed provided all procedures on review are\nfollowed. The computer will generate notice of Action.\n\n(3)\nProcess AFP as a termination if continuing eligibility is not established.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00080 -->\n\n(4)\nSubmit a Identification of Possible Overpayment form on all cases where\nchanges have occurred causing overpayments or ineligibility in previous months.\n\n\n# H. 10.a. ACTION TO BE TAKEN\n\nTake action within five (5) days to investigate any report of possible ineligibility\nreceived between regular investigations. If the investigation indicates that Medicaid\nshould be discontinued or reduced, take action to terminate benefits or change\nliability amount.\n\nNOTE: Do not withhold Medicaid during the period in which the investigation is being\nconducted.\n\nUpon verification of facts indicating ineligibility, submit an Identification of Possible\nOverpayment form.\n\nH.11.\nADVANCE NOTIFICATION OF ACTION TO BE TAKEN\n\nWhen Medicaid benefits are to be reduced or terminated, a notice of action must be\nsent to the claimant to provide notification in advance of action to be taken. The\nfollowing procedures are applicable when adverse action is necessary:\n\nTerminations: The Notice of Action must be sent in order to provide 10 days advance\nnotice of the effective date of termination.\n\nReduced benefits/increased liability: The notice of action must be sent to provide 10\ndays advance notice of payment made by the fiscal agent to the nursing home.\nExample: The claimant received an increase in VA on August 10. The liability\namount for August can be changed as late as September 1. Use nursing home\ncalendar for particular month as well as payroll schedule for fiscal year in making this\ndetermination.\n\n\n# H. 11.a. CONTENTS OF NOTICE OF ACTION\n\nThe Notice of Action is computer generated and must contain:\n\n\\- a statement of what action the agency intends to take;\n\n\\- the reasons for the intended action;\n\n\\- the Administrative Code Regulations that support or require the action;\n\n\\- an explanation of the individual\'s hearing rights (attachment);\n\n\\- an explanation of the circumstances under which Medicaid is continued if a\nhearing is requested.\n\n\n# H. 11.b. EXCEPTIONS TO 10 DAYS ADVANCE NOTICE\n\nIn the following situations, the agency may dispose with "timely" notice (that is, the\nnotice need not be mailed ten (10) days in advance of the intended action, but\nadequate notice must be mailed no later than the date of the action):\n\n(1)\nThe agency has verified information confirming death of the claimant.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

CASE RECORD AND RELATED PROCEDURES\n\nA separate file folder for each individual will be created by identifying the\nindividual\'s name and social security number on the index label. RECEIPT OF APPLICATION\n\nUpon receipt of an application in a District Office, records are set up according to\neach individual office\'s standard procedure. The following is required on each\napplication:\n\nA.1.\nAssign a case number.\n\nA.2.\nPrepare a case file folder: Maintain a case record on each individual\nwho makes an application. In general, an individual who has legal title to property has the\nright to control and dispose of it. If property cannot be disposed of, it is not a\ncountable resource.\n\nIf alleged resources (after exclusions) exceed the resource limit and there are no legal\nencumbrances (a lien, or judgment, etc.) on any countable resources, deny the\napplication. In determining a resource\'s equity value, the Agency deducts only legal\nencumbrances. There is no provision for deducting other debts or obligations that do\nnot constitute an encumbrance.\n\nMedicaid is a program based on need. The value of a claimant\'s or a married couple\'s\nresources is one of two "need" criteria used in determining eligibility. The other need\ncriterion is income (Chapter 7).\n\nException: The Medicare Savings Programs (QMB, SLMB and QI-1) are not subject to\nthe resource need test with the exception of the QDWI Program.\n\n\n## VI.B. DEFINITION\n\nResources are cash and any other personal property as well as any real property, that\nan individual (or spouse, if any):\n\n\\- owns;\n\n\\- has the right, authority or power to convert to cash. This means that the\nclaimant has legal title to property and has the right to control and dispose of\nit. (If property cannot be disposed of, it is not a countable resource.);\n\n\\- is not legally restricted from using it for his/her food or shelter.\n\n\n## VI.C. RESOURCE LIMIT\n\nResources include all liquid assets which are owned singly, deemed or assessed from a\nparent\'s or spouse\'s assets, or in joint names which can be accessed by the claimant\n(e.g., bank accounts, savings accounts, etc.) and used for basic needs such as food\nand/or shelter. They also include all non-liquid assets which are less readily\nconverted to cash, such as real or personal property (e.g., automobile, land,\nequipment, etc.).\n\nIn order to be eligible for Medicaid, an individual claimant must not have total\ncountable resources in excess of $2,000.00. The couple limit for non-institutional\ncases is $3,000.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-9" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00096 -->\n\nFor institutional programs and home and community-based waiver, an increased\namount of resources is protected for the community spouse through the pooling and\nsubsequent assessment of the couple\'s resources.\n\n\n## VI.D. DEFINITIONS OF REAL AND PERSONAL PROPERTY\n\n\n### D.1. REAL PROPERTY\n\nReal property is land, including buildings or immovable objects attached permanently\nto the land.\n\n\n### D.2. PERSONAL PROPERTY\n\nPersonal property is any property that is not real property such as cash, jewelry,\nhousehold goods, tools, automobiles, and mobile homes.\n\n\n# VI.E. UNKNOWN ASSETS\n\nIn some instances the claimant may have ownership interest in an asset and is unaware\nthat he/she has an ownership interest. If this is the case, the asset is not a resource\nduring the period in which the claimant was unaware of his/her ownership. The value\nof the previously unknown asset, including any monies (such as interest) that have\naccumulated on it through the month of discovery, is income (not a resource) in the\nmonth of discovery; if the asset is, or could be, used to purchase food or shelter. The\npreviously unknown asset is a resource subject to the usual resource counting rules\nstarting the month after the month of discovery. Any asset which cannot be used to\npurchase food or shelter is not considered to be income.\n\nException: Any item received which would be an excluded non-liquid resource, if\nretained, is not income. This exception does not apply to receipt of items of food or\nshelter even if the items would become partially or totally excluded non-liquid\nresources if retained into the month following the month of receipt.\n\nExample 1: A community takes up a collection to buy the claimant a specially\nequipped van which is his/her only vehicle. The value of this gift is not income\nbecause the van does not provide him/her with food or shelter and will be an excluded\nnon-liquid resource in the month following the month of receipt.\n\nExample 2: The claimant inherits a house which is his/her principal place of\nresidence. The value of this inheritance is income because the house provides\nhim/her with shelter, and receipt of an item which can provide shelter is considered\nto be income.\n\n\n# VI.F. FIRST-OF-THE-MONTH RULE\n\nA resource determination is made as of the first moment of each calendar month and\nis applicable to the entire month. A claimant\'s countable resources on the first day of\nthe month will be counted for the entire month. Therefore, if a claimant has excess\nresources on the first day of the month, he/she cannot be eligible for Medicaid until\nthe first day of the following month, and then only if his/her resources are at or below\nthe limit on the first moment of that month. For income posted early, deductions may\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-10" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00097 -->\n\nbe given for the month in which the income is intended as well as checks written prior\nto the first of the month that have not cleared the bank. This applies to the month in\nwhich a determination is being made.\n\n\n## F.1. INCREASE/DECREASE IN VALUE OF RESOURCE\n\nAny increase/decrease in the value of a claimant\'s resources must be considered in the\nresource determination as of the first moment of the month following the month in\nwhich the value changed.\n\n\n## F.2. RECEIPTS FROM THE SALE, EXCHANGE OR REPLACEMENT OF A RESOURCE\n\nIf a claimant sells, exchanges, or replaces a resource, what he/she receives in return\nis not income. It is a different form of resource. However, if the resource was\npreviously excluded, it may have been converted from an excluded resource to a\ncountable resource.\n\nA conversion of an excluded resource within the look back period to a countable\nresource is subject to the rules pertaining to spend-down. When equity value that was\npreviously excluded as a resource is converted to cash, it becomes a countable\nresource. The cash received from the conversion must be treated as any other\ncountable resource and spend-down must be verified.\n\nExample: A claimant has ownership interest in property that would meet one of the\nresource exclusions. During the look back period, a mortgage/lien is placed on the\nproperty in effect transferring the ownership interest to the mortgage/lien holder. If\nthere is a repayment agreement and the document is actuarially sound, no penalty will\nbe imposed for the transfer if the claimant/sponsor verifies receipt of compensated\nvalue. A penalty will be imposed for the amount the claimant/ sponsor is unsuccessful\nin verifying compensation.\n\n\n# VI.G. INCOME vs. RESOURCES\n\nIt is important to distinguish between resources and income to know which rules to\nuse. An item cannot be counted as both income and a resource in the same month.\nIncome received in cash or in kind during a month is counted as income.\n\n\n## G.1. INCOME\n\nItems received in cash or in kind during a month are counted as income.\n\n\n## G.2. RESOURCES\n\nItems retained as of the first moment of the month following receipt are counted as a\nresource.\n\nNOTE: Exceptions to this rule are stated under specified types of income and\nresources.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00098 -->\n\n\n# VI.H. LIQUID vs. NON-LIQUID RESOURCES\n\n\n## H. 1. DEFINITIONS\n\nLiquid resources are resources in the form of cash or in any other form which can be\nconverted to cash within 20 workdays.\n\nNon-liquid resources are resources which are not in the form of cash and which\ncannot be converted to cash within 20 workdays.\n\nWorkdays are days other than Saturdays, Sundays, federal and state holidays.\n\n\n## H.2. SIGNIFICANCE OF LIQUID/NON-LIQUID DISTINCTION\n\nLiquid resources do not qualify for exclusion as property essential to self-support\nunless these resources represent necessary assets of a trade or business.\n\nLiquidity/non-liquidity has no effect on whether or not a resource is countable. If a\nresource does not qualify for exclusion, then it must be counted in the eligibility\ndetermination process, regardless of whether it is considered a liquid or a non-liquid\nresource.\n\n\n## VI.I. LIQUID RESOURCES\n\nCash is always liquid: In addition, certain non-cash items are usually liquid.\n\n\n## I.1. ASSUMPTION OF LIQUIDITY', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

2026-04-02T20:25:32.334538805Z [2026-04-02 20:25:32,333] [DEBUG] openai._base_client: Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/deployments/text-embedding/embeddings', 'headers': {'Authorization': '<redacted>'}, 'files': None, 'post_parser': <function Embeddings.create.<locals>.parser at 0x7da1cdbd6a20>, 'json_data': {'input': '## VI.I. LIQUID RESOURCES\n\nCash is always liquid: In addition, certain non-cash items are usually liquid.\n\n\n## I.1. ASSUMPTION OF LIQUIDITY\n\nUnless there is evidence to the contrary, eligibility workers will assume that the\nfollowing types of resources are liquid:\n\n\\- stocks, bonds, and mutual fund shares;\n\n\\- checking and savings accounts and time deposits;\n\n\\- United States Savings Bonds and Treasury bills, notes and bonds; and\n\n\\- mortgages and promissory notes.\n\n\n## I.2. ASSUMPTION OF NON-LIQUIDITY\n\nUnless there is evidence to the contrary, eligibility workers will assume that the\nfollowing types of resources are non-liquid:\n\n\\- household goods and personal effects;\n\n\\- automobiles, trucks, tractors and other vehicles;\n\n\\- machinery and livestock;\n\n\\- buildings, land and other real property rights; and\n\n\\- non-cash business property.\n\n\n# VI.J. VALUATION OF RESOURCES\n\n\n## J.1. DEFINITIONS OF VALUES\n\nCurrent Market Value (CMV) is the price of a resource for which it can reasonably be\nexpected to sell on the open market in the particular geographic area involved.\n\nEquity value (EV) is the CMV of a resource minus any encumbrance on it.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00099 -->\n\nEncumbrance is a legally binding debt against a specific property. Such a debt\nreduces the value of the encumbered property but does not have to prevent the\nproperty owner from transferring ownership (selling) to a third party. However, if the\nowner of the encumbered property does sell it, the creditor will usually require debt\nsatisfaction from the proceeds of the sale.\n\n\n## J.2. VALUATION\n\nGeneral Rule: For Medicaid purposes, the value of a resource is the amount of the\nclaimant\'s or the claimant\'s and spouse\'s equity in it.\n\nException: SSI-Related cases - When there is only one automobile, and it does not\nqualify for exclusion based on its use, its value is the current market value rather than\nequity value.\n\n\n## VI.K. RESOURCES WITH ZERO VALUE\n\nProperty does not cease to be a resource simply because it has no current market\nvalue. Even though there is no value to count, the property remains a resource (i.e.\nproperty with zero equity value). If the property develops a market value at a later\ntime, there has been an increase in the value of a resource rather than a receipt of\nincome.\n\n\n### VI.L. WHOSE RESOURCES CAN COUNT\n\nIn addition to resources that actually belong to a claimant, the resources of a spouse\nor parent are considered to be available to the claimant in certain instances. In this\nsituation we consider the resources of the spouse or parent along with the resources\nactually belonging to the claimant in determining total countable resources. Deeming\nof resources applies regardless of whether or not they are actually available to the\nclaimant.\n\n\n#### L.1. DEEMING OF RESOURCES - INTRODUCTION\n\nDeeming provisions recognize family responsibility as they apply from spouse-to-spouse\nor parent-to-child. The intent of deeming is to consider the income and resources of\nan ineligible spouse or ineligible parent available to meet the claimant\'s needs.\n\nDeeming does not apply when the spouse or parent(s) is (are) eligible.\n\n\n#### L.2. DEEMING - DEFINITION\n\nDeeming identifies the process of considering another person\'s income and resources\nto be available for meeting a claimant\'s basic needs of food and/or shelter.\n\n\n#### L.3. PROCESS TO DETERMINE COUNTABLE RESOURCES SPOUSE-TO-SPOUSE\n\nPolicy Principle - Spouse to Spouse: Total countable resources are the combination\nof the resources of the eligible individual and the ineligible spouse after all applicable\nresource exclusions are applied. Allocate to each eligible child(ren) in the household\nan amount equal to the individual resource limit. Total countable resources are\ncompared with the resource limitation for a couple. If the remaining amount of the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-13" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00100 -->\n\nresources does not exceed the limit, the claimant meets the resource eligibility\nrequirement. If countable resources exceed the limit, the claimant is ineligible.\n\nProcedure - Spouse to Spouse: Verify and document the total countable resources\nfor the couple. Subtract the individual resource amount for each eligible child from\nthe couple\'s countable resources.\n\nSubtract the appropriate couple resource limit from the couple\'s countable resources.\nAny excess remainder is excess resources.\n\n\n##### L.4. PROCESS TO DETERMINE COUNTABLE RESOURCES-PARENT- TO-CHILD\n\nPolicy Principle: A child\'s total countable resources are the combination of the value\nof the deemed resources and the non-excluded resources of the child.\n\nDeeming: - Consider the parent(s) resource limit (one parent or two)\n\n\\- Consider other eligible children (individual limit).\n\n\\- Countable resources which exceed the above are countable to the\nclaimant.\n\nResource Limit - Procedure: A child\'s total countable resources, after deeming, are\ncompared with the resource limit for an individual with no spouse. If the resources do\nnot exceed the limit as of the first moment of the month, the child meets the resource\neligibility requirement. If countable resources exceed the limit as of the first moment\nof the month, the child is ineligible because of excess resources.\n\n\n#### L.5. RESOURCE DEEMING FOR SSI RELATED CASES\n\nThe resource deeming from parent to child is required to determine continued\neligibility for the "grandfathered children" program.\n\nNote: For all types of deeming, there is no resource allocation for ineligible children in\nthe household.\n\n\n##### Example - Parent to Child Deeming (Resources)\n\nExample 1:\n\nChild living with Ineligible Parents - Child meets resource eligibility requirement\n· Mr. and Mrs. Blake live together with their son, Thomas, who is age 16 and\nblind. Thomas has no resources of his own. Mr. and Mrs. Blake applied for SSI\non behalf of Thomas on January 23.\n\nThe parents\' resources are as follows:\n\n$4,000 - savings belonging to both Mr. and Mrs. Blake\n\nThe resource computation follows:\n\n$4,000-parent\'s countable resources subject to deeming\n-3,000-couple resource limitation\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-14" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00101 -->\n\n$1,000-resource value deemed\n+0-child\'s resources\n$1,000\n\nSince Thomas\' countable resources do not exceed the resource limit for an individual\n($2,000), Thomas meets the resource eligibility requirement.\n\n\n###### Example 2:\n\nTwo children living with ineligible parent and ineligible stepparent-children meet\nresource eligibility requirement\n\n. John and Joan Goode, ages 15 and 16, are both disabled and live with their\nmother and stepfather. John\'s only resources are five U.S. savings bonds worth\n$50 each. Joan\'s resources are a $175 savings account.\n\nThe parents own:\n\n\\- one automobile valued at $3,000\n\n\\- savings account with balance of $2,500\n\n\\- cash on hand - $200\n\n\\- household goods and personal effects valued at $4, 100\n\nMrs. Smith, John and Joan\'s mother, files for SSI payments on their behalf on July 17.\n\nThe resource computation follows:\n\nParents\' excluded resources\n\n\\- One automobile\n\n\\- Household goods and personal effects up to $2,000\n\nParents\' Countable Resources\n\n$2,500-savings account\n$200-cash on hand\n\n\\+ $2, 100-household goods and personal effects (amount over $2,000 limit)\n\n$4,800-parents\' total countable resources\n\n\\- $3,000-couple resource limit\n$1,800-deemed\n\nJohn\'s Countable Resources -\n$250- savings bonds\n\\+ $900- resource value deemed\n$1,150- countable resources\n\nJoan\'s Countable Resources -\n$175- savings account\n+$900- resource value deemed\n$1075- countable resources\n\nSince neither John\'s nor Joan\'s countable resources exceed the resource limit, they\nboth meet the resource eligibility requirement.\n\nExample 3:\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-15" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00102 -->\n\nTwo children living with ineligible parent and ineligible stepparent - one child found\nineligible first and other child becomes ineligible as a result\n\n. THE HOME\n\nA claimant\'s home, regardless of value, is an excluded resource.\n\nA home is defined as property:\n\n\\- in which the claimant (and spouse, if any) has an ownership interest; and\n\n\\- which is used by the claimant (and spouse, if any) as his/her principal place of\nresidence.\n\nIt is the property that meets the definition of the home, not just the dwelling.\nTherefore, all mobile homes, buildings, and/or dwellings located on the property are\nconsidered a part of the home. Additionally, the home includes any land that\nappertains thereto and any related outbuildings. In considering whether real property\nappertains to the home plot, do not consider easements or public rights of way (e.g.,\nstreets, roads, utility lines, etc.) which run through or by the land and separate the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-17" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00104 -->\n\nland from the home plot or from the rest of the land. The home can be either real or\npersonal property, fixed or mobile, and located on land or water.\n\nBefore it will be excluded as a home, however, it must be determined that the\nproperty is the claimant\'s principal place of residence. Thus, only one residence may\nbe excluded as the home.\n\n\n# M.1.a. INTENT TO RETURN\n\nThe home may be excluded as a resource if the claimant intends to return to the\nhome. Absences from the home such as trips, visits, and hospitalizations do not affect\nthe home exclusion as long as the claimant intends to return home (however, a lien\nmay be required). When the home is no longer the principal place of residence, its\nvalue is counted as a resource.\n\nStatement of intent to return home must be indicated on the Application form;\nStatement of Claimant or Other Person form; or the Intent to Return Home form.\nRedevelop the intent to return home annually. The claimant\'s or sponsor\'s statement\nof intent is accepted without challenge unless the statement is self-contradictory.\n\nWhen the statement is self-contradictory and does not make the claimant\'s intent\nclear, obtain clarification from secondary sources, such as a physician, close relative,\nor person in a position to know. When clarification from secondary sources is received\nand the intent is still not clear, then complete the intent to return home more\nfrequently.\n\nNOTE: The home is excluded as a resource if the claimant intends to return home but\nthe intent statement does not override policy regarding developing need for a lien.\n(See Section VI.P. for policy regarding liens).\n\nEXCEPTIONS: Even if the claimant leaves the home without the intent to return, the\nproperty remains an excluded resource as long as:\n\n\\- a spouse or dependent relative of the claimant continues to live there while the\nclaimant is institutionalized (See VI.M.1.b.); or\n\n\\- its sale would cause undue hardship, due to loss of housing, to a co-owner of\nthe property (See VI.M.4.).\n\n\n## M. 1.b. SPOUSE OR DEPENDENT RELATIVE LIVING IN THE HOME\n\nThe home may be excluded as a resource if a spouse or dependent relative has lived\nthere immediately prior to claimant\'s admission to the nursing home. Dependency\nmay be any kind (e.g., financial, emotional, medical, etc.). A lien is required when\nproperty is an excluded resource for a dependent relative (See VI.P.1. for exceptions\nto lien requirements).\n\nThe worker should review the resource section of the application to see if a relative\nresides in the home. This has to\nbe redeveloped annually.\n\nNOTE: The home is excluded as a resource if a spouse or dependent relative resides\nthere; however, a lien may be required. GENERAL INFORMATION\n\nMedicaid - Title XIX of the Social Security Act has made available to many needy\ncitizens a broad program of health coverage designed to assist designated groups in\nmeeting hospital, medical, and other health costs. The Medicaid Law (Title XIX)\nspecifically designates administrative responsibility for the Program to a State Agency\ndesignated by the individual states and prohibits the Federal Government from\nexercising direct supervision or control over the Program, the manner in which\nmedical services are provided, and the administrative or operation of medical\nfacilities. In recognition of their experience in the health care and insurance fields,\nthe use of insurance organizations as fiscal agents by the states is authorized. The\nAlabama Medicaid Agency is the responsible State Agency in Alabama. The patient is\nfree to choose any qualified institution, agency, or practitioner-offering services\nbased on a written agreement with the State Agency. The responsibility for\ntreatment and the control of care remains with the physician and the nursing facility\nor agency, called a "Vendor" or "Provider of Services", having signed an agreement\nwith the State Agency to furnish services.\n\nIf the applicant has Medicare or any other health insurance plan, the provider must\nfile with those resources before filing Medicaid. The Alabama Medicaid Program will\npay Title XVIII deductible and coinsurance amounts for Medicaid covered services\nrendered Medicaid/Medicare eligible recipients.\n\n\n## I.A NON-DISCRIMINATION MEDICAID PROVIDERS\n\nParticipating providers of services under the Medicaid Program must comply with the\nrequirements of Title VI and VII of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, Section 504 of the\nRehabilitation Act of 1973, the Age Discrimination Act of 1975, and the Americans\nwith Disabilities Act of 1990. Under the provisions of these Acts, a participating\nprovider or vendor of services is prohibited from making a distinction based on race,\ncolor, creed, national origin, age, or disability. The State Agency is responsible for\ninvestigating complaints of non-compliance.\n\n\n## I.B. CONFIDENTIALITY\n\nInformation regarding Medicaid applicants and recipients is required to be\nsafeguarded. Confidential materials or categories of information relating to Medicaid\napplicants and recipients include, but not limited to the following:\n\n(a) Lists of names, addresses, and Medicaid eligibility numbers.\n\n(b) Medical services provided.\n\n(c) Social and economic conditions and circumstances.\n\n(d) Medicaid or other State or Federal government agency evaluation of personal\ninformation.\n\n(e) Medicaid data, including but not limited to, diagnosis and past history or\ndisease or disability.\n\n(f) Fair hearing records.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00002 -->\n\nInformation contained in the applicants/recipient\'s case record is confidential and\nshould not be released to the public. The exception to the policy is that if Medicaid\nhas an agreement with an agency then the Medicaid worker can provide the status of\nthe application and/or review. The agencies in which Medicaid has an agreement with\nare:\n\n(a) Alabama Department of Mental Health (ADMH)\n\n(b) Waiver Administering Agencies (Alabama Department of Mental Health (ADMH),\nAlabama Department of Rehabilitation Services (ADRS), Alabama Department of\nSenior Services (ADSS), and their agents such as the Area on Aging Agency\n(AAA).\n\n(c) Department of Human Resources (DHR)\n\n(d) Social Security Administration (SSA)\n\n(e) Children Health Insurance Program (CHIP)\n\n\n## I.C. THE DISTRICT OFFICE SYSTEM\n\nAlabama accepts the Social Security Administration\'s determination of eligibility for\nSSI money payments. Alabama uses the SSI rules as a basis for Medicaid eligibility for\nreasons of administrative simplicity and economy. Thus, we are a 1634 state.\n\nHowever, eligibility for segments of the SSI population remained undetermined by the\nstate. These included Medicaid benefits for those receiving only state optional\nsupplemental payments, the three-month retroactive eligibility determinations for SSI\nrecipients for the period prior to cash assistance, and eligibility determinations for\nthe institutionalized population. An institutionalized person loses SSI eligibility if\nhis/her income exceeds $50 per month, and a new application must be made so the\nState Medicaid Agency can redetermine eligibility.\n\nAlabama uses a district office system similar to that used by SSA as a model of\nadministration. This allowed the State Medicaid Agency to take responsibility for the\nprogram and to control the eligibility determination process for the highly expensive\ninstitutionalized population. This created a limited and specialized group of\ncaseworkers with the responsibility of determining Medicaid eligibility.\n\nThe caseworkers within the district office system determine eligibility for the\ninstitutionalized population, the SSI-related Medicaid only groups, and the Limited\nMedicaid groups. Out-stationed caseworkers in the district office, hospitals, and\nhealth centers provide easier access to Medicaid coverage for pregnant women and\nchildren with income at or below the required Federal Poverty Level.\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency believes eligibility determination to be as important to\nthe total program as claims processing. Through the district office system and the\nout-stationed workers, the Agency has direct-line command rather than indirect\ncontrol over non-cash eligibility determinations.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="I-3" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00003 -->\n\n\n## I.D. While collection activity is conducted through the Third-\nParty Division, the identification of possible overpayments comes from a variety of\nsources.\n\n\n### A.1.a. REPORTING\n\nWhen the Medicaid eligibility worker discovers a possible overpayment, whether due\nto fraud, ineligibility, administrative error, failure to report income or any other\nreason, the information regarding the possible overpayment should be verified prior\nto submission of the possible overpayment. Verification of income on file, including proof of the income that was used\nto determine eligibility during the overpayment period.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00011 -->\n\n3\\. If the\noverpayment is the result of misstatement of facts at application, send a\ncopy of the record of contact back to that date. Otherwise, send the\nrecord of contact beginning with the date the overpayment reportedly\nbegan or the review period prior to the discovery of the change.\n\n5\\. Legal interpretation, hearing decision, or any other relevant material that\ncould have a bearing on the recoupment.\n\n6\\. If recipient failed to disclose information at application, send a copy of the\noriginal application. If a change was not reported timely, and it covers\nmore than one review period, send copies of all applicable review forms\nduring the overpayment period that confirm the recipient/sponsor failed to\nreport changes.\n\nWhen an overpayment report is submitted, the name and social security number of\nthe adult payee must always be used. Never register an overpayment in a child\'s\nname.\n\nThe Medicaid eligibility worker should contact the Beneficiary Services Division -\nPolicy & Training Unit with any questions or concerns regarding the overpayment.\n\nEXCEPTION: In the event that an overpayment does not exceed $300.00, no recovery\naction will be taken. It will not be necessary to make an overpayment referral, nor\nwill it be necessary to request reimbursement from the claimant in these situations.\n\n\n### A.1.b. VOLUNTARY REIMBURSEMENT\n\nWhen voluntary reimbursement is received in any Medicaid Office (i.e., claimant or\nsponsor turns over income such as a lump sum check to avoid having excess\nresources), the case should be mailed to the Overpayments Section. All pertinent\ndocumentation (see A.1.a.) and the payment should be sent to the Overpayments\nSection. If a check is received by the district office that is made payable to the\nrecipient, the check should be properly endorsed to the Alabama Medicaid Agency\nprior to forwarding it to the Overpayments Section.\n\n\n### A.1.c. INQUIRIES\n\nAny inquiries regarding collection activity should be referred to the Overpayments\nSection.\n\n\n## A.2. ABUSE, FRAUD, AND DELIBERATE MISUSE/SUSPENSION\n\nThe Program Integrity Division guards against abuse, fraud, and deliberate misuse of\nthe Medicaid program benefits by its individual providers and claimants. This division\nwill initiate the Recipient AR Report on cases that they have investigated and\ndiscovered suspected fraud. The Utilization Review Committee (URC) determines\nsanctions to be imposed on providers as well as restrictions and suspension against\nclaimants.\n\nProgram Integrity will notify the district office, nursing home, sponsor and claimant\nof the ineligibility by sending a termination letter. These suspensions are for one\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00012 -->\n\nyear and until the Medicaid recipient repays all of the misspent funds to the Alabama\nMedicaid Agency. When a Medicaid recipient is suspended, they cannot receive any\nMedicaid benefits even if they would be otherwise eligible. However, state law does\nprovide for temporarily lifting a recipient\'s suspension to allow for pregnancy-related\nservices.\n\nThe computer file is updated with the suspension code of 5 and DC Track Indicator\nCode 05. These indicate cases that are terminated or suspended due to fraud or\nabuse.\n\nException: Code can only be removed in the following situations:\n\n· When a suspended recipient requests a timely hearing,\n\n· To allow for pregnancy-related benefits for an eligible recipient and,\n\n· When a suspension has been served (one year and paid in full).\n\nCopies of letters requesting restitution to the agency, follow-up correspondence, and\npayment(s) received will be provided the district office for inclusion with the case\nrecord.\n\nWhen the Program Integrity Division has received reimbursement and/or has\ndetermined that the claimant\'s suspension period is completed, the claimant will be\nremoved from termination by removing the suspension code and changing the DC\nTrack code to 99 (previously suspension). The district office and the recipient will be\nnotified that the recipient\'s suspension has been satisfied so the reapplication\nprocedure may be initiated. Retro-eligibility cannot be awarded in months where\nthere was a suspension period.\n\nNote: Please contact the Program Integrity Division prior to denying applications of\napplicants and/or recipients who are under suspension with a code of (05).\n\n\n## A.3. APPLICABLE FEDERAL AND STATE STATUES\n\nFederal Section 1128B of the Social Security Act provides criminal penalties for\nanyone who knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made false representation\nconcerning application for benefits, or continuances thereof, of misuse thereof.\nSection (c) and (d) thereof are particular to providers.\n\nSections (c) and (d):\n\n"Whoever knowingly and willfully makes or causes to be made, or induces or seeks to\ninduce the making of, any false statement or representation of a material fact with\nrespect to the conditions or operation of any institution, facility, or entity in order\nthat such institution, facility, or entity may qualify (either upon initial certification\nor upon recertification) as a hospital, rural primary care hospital, skilled nursing\nfacility, nursing facility, intermediate care facility for individuals with intellectual\ndisabilities, home health agency, or other entity [including an eligible organization\nunder Section 1876(b)]for which certification is required under title XVIII or a State\nhealth care program, or with respect to information required to be provided under\nsection 1124A, shall be guilty of a felony and upon conviction thereof shall be fined\nnot more than $25,000 or imprisoned for not more than five years, or both.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-3" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00013 -->\n\nWhoever knowingly and willfully charges, for any service provided to a patient under\na State plan approved under title XIX, money or other consideration at a rate in\nexcess of the rates established by the State, or charges, solicits, accepts, or\nreceives, in addition to any amount otherwise required to be paid under a State plan\napproved under title XIX, any gift, money, donation, or other consideration (other\nthan a charitable, religious, or philanthropic contribution from an organization or\nfrom a person unrelated to the patient)\n\n(1) as a precondition of admitting a patient to a hospital, nursing facility, or\nintermediate care facility for individuals with intellectual disabilities, or\n\n(2) as a requirement for the patient\'s continued stay in such a facility, when the\ncost of the services provided therein to the patient is paid for (in whole or in\npart) under the State plan, shall be guilty of a felony and upon conviction\nthereof shall be fined not more than $25,000 or imprisoned for not more than\nfive years, or both."\n\nIn addition, there are several statutes regarding false claims, altering drafts, or\notherwise making false representation; they provide for punishment by fines and\nimprisonment or both.\n\nState - Administrative Code Rule No. 560-X-25 -. 09 is discussed in Part VI.\n\nState - Alabama Code Section 22-6-8 (1975) revokes or FAIR HEARINGS\n\nThe State Plan provides that the office of the Governor, acting through Medicaid, will\nbe responsible for fulfillment of hearing provisions for all matters pertaining to the\nmedical assistance program under Title XIX. Federal law requires that an opportunity\nfor a hearing be granted to most claimants requesting a hearing because his/her\nclaim for medical assistance is denied, is not acted upon with reasonable\npromptness, or because he is aggrieved by any other agency action regarding his\nsituation. Alabama Medicaid Agency Office of General Counsel will make the decision\nas to whether the adverse action is due to State or Federal law.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-4" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00014 -->\n\nThe procedures contained herein have been adopted by Alabama Medicaid Agency to\nsettle formal complaints of persons who are receiving care under the Medicaid\nprogram or who have been denied care under this program because of eligibility\nstandards.\n\nThe hearing authority for all claimant fair hearings is the Commissioner, Alabama\nMedicaid Agency. The Hearing Officer will conduct the hearing and submit his\nfindings and recommendations to the Commissioner who will make the final decision\non each case. A copy of findings and recommendations is sent to the Alabama\nMedicaid Agency and to the appropriate district office manager. A hearing officer\nmust not have been involved in any way with the action in questions. His/her\ninvolvement in this hearing process should not begin until, as a last resort, the Office\nof General Counsel sends him/her the request for the hearing and directs him/her to\nschedule the fair hearing.\n\nFair hearings will usually be in the form of a face-to-face hearing by an impartial\nState Hearing Officer and attended by the complainant or his authorized\nrepresentative. However, any person or organization substantially affected by an\nexisting rule or statute governing the Alabama Medicaid Program may request a\nDocumentary Hearing. See Administrative Code, Chapter 3, for procedure.\n\n\n### B.1. HEARING REQUEST\n\nEach claimant requesting review of his/her case has free choice of whether such\nreview shall be handled through informal complaint and adjustment procedures or\nthrough a hearing. If he/she chooses a hearing, it cannot be delayed or canceled\nwithout his/her consent because of a review by the local office.\n\n\n#### B. 1.a. FORMAT OF REQUEST\n\nAny clear, written statement addressed to the Alabama Medicaid Agency\nindicating/stating that the claimant wants an opportunity to present his/her case to\na higher authority will be accepted. However, it must be determined by Alabama\nMedicaid Agency that the request is within the 60-day time limitation for acceptance\nof appeals, and the determination is made that he/she has a valid basis for appeal.\nThe claimant\'s request must state clearly, why he/she is dissatisfied.\n\n\n#### B. 1.b. WHO CAN REQUEST A HEARING\n\nThe claimant or someone acting on their behalf may make a request for a fair\nhearing. If someone files the appeal other than the claimant, or a person of his\nchoice, there must be a definite statement by the person making the appeal that it is\nbeing made upon authorization of the claimant.\n\nNOTE: The person making the appeal must have the legal authority to represent the\nclaimant, such as legal guardian, conservator, power of attorney, or appointed as\nrepresentative by signature on the agency appointment of representative form (Form\n202.) Medicaid\'s Office of General Counsel (OGC) will make the determination as to\nwho has the authorization to request the hearing. The district office role is to\nforward the hearing request, summary, and any supporting documents to the OGC.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00015 -->\n\n\n#### B. 1.c. WHO RECEIVES HEARING REQUESTS\n\nRequests for fair hearings may be sent to the pertinent district office. All requests\nshould be stamped in on the date received.\n\n\n### B.2. DISTRICT OFFICE PROCEDURES FOR PROCESSING HEARING REQUESTS\n\nUpon receipt of the hearing request, the Medicaid specialist should apply the hearing\nstamp to the front of the case record and fill in the available information. During\neach step of the hearing process, the specialist should add the additional\ninformation.\n\nNOTE: The actions in steps (1) through (3) below must be documented in the case\nrecord (Record of Contact Form).\n\nDetermine if benefits are to be continued or reinstated. If the appeal is received\nwithin ten (10) days of the effective date of action, along with a written request to\ncontinue the benefits, a case to be terminated may remain open and payment\ncontinued; or a liability amount to be increased may remain unchanged. Unreduced\npayments will continue in effect as long as the appeal is active and through the end\nof the month in which the final decision is reached. Eligibility dates on the computer\nshould show that eligibility was extended through the month in which the final\ndecision on eligibility was reached.\n\nException: Delays in conducting the hearing on the appeal caused by the claimant\nor his/her representative that were not due to justifiable causes, such as verified\nillness, will result in taking the action previously indicated on the case unless\navailable facts establish that different action is necessary. The Office of General\nCounsel will be responsible for notifying the claimant. The district office will make\nthis determination.\n\n· Contact the sponsor/person requesting the hearing and advise that if benefits\nare continued or unchanged pending the outcome of the hearing and the hearing\ndecision is in favor of the Agency, Alabama Medicaid Agency may recover from the\nclaimant or sponsor the costs of all benefits paid after the initial effective date of\nthe termination or change.\n\n· Request decision of sponsor to be submitted in writing.\n\n· Advise sponsor that an opportunity will be provided for a conference to discuss\nthe claimant\'s situation with agency staff to obtain an explanation of reasons for the\nproposed action and/or to present information to show that the proposed action is\nincorrect. During the conference the claimant may speak for himself/herself or be\nrepresented by legal counsel or by other spokesman of his/her choice. This\nconference does not alter the person\'s right to a fair hearing.\n\nThe district office caseworker and office manager should immediately review the\nrecord for completeness and accuracy. This is to ensure worker can\nconverse with sponsor or someone acting responsibly for the claimant during the time\nthe original record is out of the office.\n\n\n### B.3 PROCEDURES FOR THE ALABAMA MEDICAID AGENCY MONTGOMERY\n\nThe district office manager will forward the record directly to the Regional\nSupervisor of Beneficiary Services-Western or Eastern Division.\n\nUpon receipt of the summary and documents, the Western or Eastern Regional\nSupervisor will endeavor by written and/or verbal communication with the claimant,\nsponsor, or Alabama Medicaid Agency (district office) to settle the question.
