Return such information to the provider and ask that it be returned in a\nform that can be used in the event of a hearing. Any information that designated\nonly as confidential can be used in an appeal hearing and will be treated as any other\nconfidential material.\n\n\n### B.5.a. TYPES OF HEARING EXHIBITS\n\nHearing exhibits are confined to the following:\n\n\\- The eligibility case record\n\n\\- Summary of the case by district office personnel\n\n\\- Alabama Medicaid Agency Administrative Code\n\n\\- Alabama Medicaid Agency current manuals and official numbered policy\nmemorandums\n\n\\- Policy interpretations used by workers in the district office\n\n\\- Such other documents as the attorney representing the agency thinks are\nnecessary.\n\n\n#### B.5.b. REVIEW OF EXHIBITS\n\nThe claimant and/or his/her designated representative may, upon request, review\nthe case record at any time prior to the hearing. Copies of the case record may be\nobtained at the district office in accordance with agency procedures regarding\nreproduction cost. Worker should ask anyone other than the claimant or sponsor for\nidentification, a release, authorized representative form, or other written\nauthorization signed by the claimant or sponsor, and contact the Office of General\nCounsel informing them of the circumstances. In questionable situations, the Office\nof General Counsel should be contacted before the person is allowed to examine the\ncase record. The exhibits that will be introduced by the agency at the time of the\nhearing, not in the record, may be reviewed or copies obtained from the Office of\nGeneral Counsel.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-8" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00018 -->\n\n\n## B.6. INTERIM ACTION- WITHDRAWAL\n\nDuring the interim of scheduling a fair hearing, circumstances may change which\nwould warrant the withdrawal of a hearing request. The following conditions must\nbe met to withdraw a hearing request:\n\n\\- A written request withdrawing the hearing must be received.\n\n\\- The claimant, person with legal authority to represent the claimant, or duly\nappointed legal counsel can withdraw the request.\n\n\\- The request to withdraw a hearing may be sent or given to Alabama Medicaid\nAgency, Montgomery, or the district office involved.\n\nThe office that receives the withdrawal request will telephone the other office the\nday it is received. A written confirmation will be sent in 10 days along with a copy of\nthe withdrawal letter. The date the withdrawal request is received is the date the\nappeal will be considered withdrawn.\n\n\n## B.7. ACTION UPON NOTIFICATION OF HEARING DECISION\n\nIf the Agency\'s decision is reversed by the results of the fair hearing, follow\ninstructions set forth in the letter from the State Office. If appropriate action is not\nclear from the decision letter, consult the Director of Beneficiary Services- Western\nor Eastern Division for instructions.\n\nIf the results of a fair hearing uphold the Agency\'s initial termination, Medicaid\nbenefits that were continued pending results of a fair hearing should be terminated.\n\nWhen terminating a case as a result of a fair hearing, use code #58.\n\nIf the Office of General Counsel has denied the request for a fair hearing, the case\nwill be terminated using code #59.\n\nAn Identification of Possible Overpayment form and supporting documentation should\nbe completed and submitted to Beneficiary Services Division - Policy & Training Unit.\nThe Policy & Training Unit will forward the Identification of Possible Overpayment\nform to Third-Party Division to initiate recoupment action.\n\n\n## II.C. MEDICAID QUALITY CONTROL\n\nA continuing objective of federal, state, and local government is to ensure that\npublic assistance expenditures are made on behalf of eligible beneficiaries in the\ncorrect amount. The Medicaid Quality Control (MQC) system is a management tool\nfor meeting this objective in the Medicaid program. The MQC system is a joint\nfederal/state effort to obtain information on which to base policy and procedural\nchanges that will improve administration of the Medicaid program and control the\nexpenditures of Title XIX funds.\n\nThe Medicaid Quality Control system is based on reviews of statistically valid samples\nof cases selected from the Medicaid eligibility file. The MQC review activities are\ncarried out in accordance with federally established policies and procedures. For\ncase accountability purposes, Medicaid Quality Control should notify the district\noffice whether the requested record is for state or federal audit.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-9" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00019 -->\n\n\n## C.1. ADMINISTRATIVE PERIOD FOR CHANGES\n\nQuality Control procedures provide for a reasonable period for agencies to reflect\nchange in circumstances of eligibility groups. This period of time is referred to as\nthe Administrative period. For MQC purposes the Administrative period is the review\nmonth and month prior to the review month. The Administrative period provides for\ntime allowances such as: advance notice, claimant reporting, agency investigations,\nand agency-imposed time allowances for claimant action. MQC uses the case status\nfor the review month in determining the type of error, the dollar amount of the\nerror, and if an error would exist even with application of the Administrative period.\n\n. No error will be charged if the error is because the case record data, as the\nMQC review month, is incorrect or does not reflect a change in circumstances, which\noccurred during the review month (calendar or fiscal), or the month prior to the\nreview month.\n\n. However, an error would be charged if the case record data were adjusted\nincorrectly during this period.\n\n. An error would exist if the status were incorrect as of the review month and\nwould still be incorrect disregarding the change in circumstances that occurred\nduring the Administrative period.\n\nNOTE: The Administrative period does not apply to State policy changes.\n\nNOTE: The Administrative period does not alleviate the need for recoupment and\nprocessing of an Identification of Possible Overpayment form.\n\n\n## C.2. CORRECTIVE ACTION\n\nMedicaid Quality Control sends a report to the district office to provide information\nso the record can be updated. A copy of the report is sent to the Director of\nBeneficiary Services- Western or Eastern Division. Upon receipt of the report by the\ndistrict office, take the following action:\n\n\\- Pull the case record to determine what action should be taken\n\n\\- Any change or corrective action should be taken immediately. If recoupment\nis indicated, complete the Identification of Possible Overpayment form\nimmediately to submit to Beneficiary Services Division - Policy & Training Unit.\n\n\\- If information, in case record, indicate action taken by the Medicaid Office is\ncorrect and should not be changed, process MQC-3.\n\n\n## C.3. JUDGMENTAL DECISIONS', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

If recoupment\nis indicated, complete the Identification of Possible Overpayment form\nimmediately to submit to Beneficiary Services Division - Policy & Training Unit.\n\n\\- If information, in case record, indicate action taken by the Medicaid Office is\ncorrect and should not be changed, process MQC-3.\n\n\n## C.3. JUDGMENTAL DECISIONS\n\nIn cases where the error cited is a matter of individual, subjective interpretation of\nwritten policy, the action taken by the district office worker based on his or her\nprofessional judgment shall prevail. CMS has previously advised that in cases where\npolicy is obviously ambiguous and could be interpreted as the worker has interpreted\nit, Federal QC will not cite errors. The district office worker should state the issue\nand develop the steps that led to the judgmental decision.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-10" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00020 -->\n\n\n## II.D. SYSTEM INCOME ELIGIBILITY VERIFICATION\n\nFederally funded public assistance agencies had to develop a system for exchanging\ninformation regarding the income and resources of claimants with various state and\nfederal agencies. In order to prevent fraud and abuse by making a more accurate\neligibility determination, the Income Eligibility Verification System (IEVS) was\ndeveloped to handle this information.\n\n\n### D.1. DHR DATA MATCHES\n\nThe Department of Human Resources (DHR) was designated by the Governor as the\nsingle State Agency responsible for initiating tape matches for all programs including\nMedicaid, Food Stamps, and Public Assistance.\n\n\n### D.2. AGENCIES PROVIDING INFORMATION\n\nIEVS regulations require that Medicaid request information on claimants from the\nfollowing agencies:\n\n\n### D.2.a. SOCIAL SECURITY ADMINISTRATION (SSA)\n\nFrom SSA we receive:\n\nBENDEX data for claimants for whom the information has not previously been\nrequested. This is information about SSA benefit payments. The district offices have\non-line access to this file. Any discrepancies between BENDEX and CARES is sent by\nmonthly report to the district offices.\n\nBEER (BENDEX Earnings Exchange Record) data for claimants for whom the\ninformation has not previously been requested. This is information about net\nearnings from self-employment, military pay, interstate wages and retirement\nincome maintained by SSA. BEER data will be sent to the district offices on a\nseparate report. This information must be investigated and independently verified\nby the worker.\n\n\n### D.2.b. INTERNAL REVENUE SERVICE (IRS)\n\nFrom IRS we receive information on earned and unearned income (interest on\nsavings, dividends, stocks, bonds, etc.). This is not tax-return information. This file\nis compiled from information submitted by the interest payer. The federal tax\ninformation, or FTI, is requested at application and annually thereafter for\nclaimants. The claimant match will be completed the month prior to\nredetermination. Reports are generated at the state office and are sorted by district\noffice and worker number. The printouts are then submitted to the appropriate staff\nfor action. FTI must be investigated and independently verified by the worker.\n\n\n### D.2.c. ALABAMA DEPARTMENT OF LABOR (ADOL)\n\nFrom ADOL we receive:\n\nInformation on claimants on a monthly basis for wages, and on a weekly basis for\nunemployment compensation. This data must be independently verified through the\nemployer.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00021 -->\n\nUnemployment Compensation (UC) data for all pending, active denied or terminated\nclaimants. Since ADOL is the source of UC payment, it is not necessary to verify this\ninformation unless the claimant reports a discrepancy.\n\n\n### D.3. USE OF SOCIAL SECURITY NUMBERS\n\nThe agency must require, as a condition of eligibility, that each claimant for\nMedicaid services furnish his or her social security number.\n\nThe claimant must be advised of the uses the agency will make of the SSN, including\nits use for verifying income and eligibility. This notice is incorporated into the form\n204/205.\n\n\n### D.4. INVESTIGATIONS\n\nIEVS regulations require that within 45 days of receipt of information (by the state\noffice) from one of the sources listed above, the agency must take the following\nsteps:\n\n\\- Request verification if appropriate.\n\n\\- Determine whether the information affects eligibility or the liability amount.\n\n\\- Make an entry in the case file.\n\n\\- Notify the claimant of any adverse action.\n\nNOTE: IEVS/FTI may be disclosed only to an authorized individual, such as the\nclaimant and/or authorized representative/ sponsor or, in the case of a deceased\nclaimant, the representative/sponsor, the executor, administrator, or trustee of the\nestate. Therefore, when IEVS/FTI is received that prompts a contact with a financial\ninstitution, use the Asset Verification System (AVS). See Section II.E. for the use of\nAVS.\n\n\n### D.4.a. TIMELINESS REQUIREMENTS\n\nThe number of determinations delayed beyond 45 days from receipt of an item of\ninformation must not exceed twenty percent of the number of items of information\nreceived on all claimants. The state office will be responsible for monitoring\ntimeliness requirements. The district office managers are responsible for seeing that\nall information received is acted on promptly.\n\n\n### D.4.b. COMPLETION OF FORM 209\n\nIncome Eligibility Verification System Investigation Report (Form 209) is to be\ncompleted on each case in which information is received through IEVS. This report is\nsubmitted to the state office for statistical reporting to CMS. If the investigation\nreveals unreported or discrepant information, the form 209 should be separated.\nThe bottom portion should be completed and mailed in to the IVES coordinator and\nthe top part of the form should be maintained in the IVES case file.\n\n(1) If the information received is already contained in the case record and\npreviously verified, no investigation is required. The caseworker should make an\nentry in the case narrative to this effect and destroy Form 209 by shredding or\nincinerating.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00022 -->\n\nExample: FTI received on John Smith indicates that he received $60.00 interest in\nthe previous year from account #030607 at First Alabama Bank of Montgomery. The\ncase record reveals that this account information was furnished at application and\nhas been verified with the information received from AVS.\n\n(2)\nIf the information received is new to the case, it should be treated as a\nlead and investigated. Except for Unemployment Compensation Data, any\ninformation must be independently verified with the bank, employer, sponsor, etc.\nInformation may be discussed with the sponsor, claimant, or a third party after the\nindependent verification is received. The source of the information may be revealed\nat this time.\n\n(a)\nIf the information is proven to be invalid or valid but has no\nbearing on the case, make an entry in the narrative to that effect and destroy Form\n209 by shredding or incinerating. Some information received will be invalid due to an\nunverified Social Security Number. Hits received through ADOL may be wages earned\nby another person who is using our claimant\'s SSN in error. An Agency form letter has\nbeen developed to verify wages with employers. If the employer indicates on this\nform letter that the reported earnings were for another individual, we will accept\nthat as documentation that our claimant did not receive those earnings. If we have\nverified the SSN through BENDEX or MBR Query, we have no further responsibility to\ncorrect the problem of the SSN used in error.\n\n(b)\nIf the information causes the claimant to be ineligible or changes\nthe liability payment, notify the claimant in writing of the action to be taken, make\nappropriate entry in case record, complete form 209 and complete the Identification\nof Possible Overpayment form, as needed.\n\nD.5.\nSAFEGUARDING INFORMATION\n\nAll information received through IEVS is to be treated as confidential and restricted\nto purposes directly related to the administration of the Medicaid program. IEVS\ndata will be safeguarded as required in Alabama Medicaid Internal Memorandum No.\n206 "Release of Confidential Information" and the district office Security Plan.\n\nAll district offices have copies of Section 7213(a) of the Internal Revenue Code which\nmakes unauthorized disclosure of Federal return information a crime that may be\npunishable by a $5,000.00 fine, five years imprisonment, or both. District office\npersonnel will also have copies of Section 7431 of the Internal Revenue Code that\npermits a taxpayer to bring suit for civil damages for unauthorized disclosure of\nreturns and return information.\n\nAn on-site review of safeguard procedures will be conducted annually by the central\noffice IEVS Coordinator.\n\n\n#### D.6. STORAGE/FILING OF IEVS DOCUMENTS IN DISTRICT OFFICES', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

STORAGE/FILING OF IEVS DOCUMENTS IN DISTRICT OFFICES\n\nPrintouts received through IEVS containing case information are to be filed in the\ncase record with the exception of FTI. Printouts containing FTI must be stored in one\ncentral locked filing cabinet. IRS will conduct field audits to assure that this\nrequirement is met.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-13" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00023 -->\n\nAs a condition for receiving FTI, the receiving agency must provide and maintain\nassurance that the information is being used only for the purposes of determining\neligibility and benefits for the programs it is administering. This must be completed\nto the satisfaction of IRS. The agency must implement safeguards designed to\nprevent unauthorized uses of the information and to protect the confidentiality of\nthat information. The following procedures are based on instructions for\nsafeguarding data found in IRS Publication 1075.\n\n\n### D.6.a. RECEIPT OF FTI\n\nUpon receipt of FTI, the assigned district office person will divide the FTI according\nto caseworker code. The FTI will be placed in individual file folders so that they will\nnot be commingled with other case records and will be thus identified as classified,\nconfidential information. The assigned person will place the folders in the secure\nlocation filed by worker number and in alphabetical order.\n\n\n### D.6.b. REVIEW\n\nThe caseworker will review the FTI and compare it to the existing information in the\nclaimant\'s case record to determine if an investigation and independent verification\nis needed. When the caseworker has completed the review of the FTI, the record\nwill be given to the district office manager who is the assigned Security Officer for\nthe district office. At the end of each workday, the worker must return the FTI to\nthe locked file cabinet. In the absence of the Security Officer, the assistant district\noffice manager may receive the records.\n\n\n#### D.6.c. SECURITY\n\nThe Security Officer will maintain and secure a key and locking file cabinet. All FTI\nrecords will be stored in this locked file cabinet. It will be the responsibility of the\nSecurity Officer to assure that no FTI folders are left on workers\' desks at the end of\nthe workday. If the worker has not completed review of the FTI, the record will be\nlocked up in the district office IEVS file until the next workday.\n\n\n#### D.6.d. REMOVAL OF RECORDS\n\nWhenever FTI records are removed from storage, they must be charged out to an\nindividual having a need for the information. The Security Officer will maintain a\nwritten register of the charge-out that includes date, name, and title of the person\nreceiving the record.\n\n\n#### D.6.e. MAINTENANCE OF RECORDS\n\nThe district office will maintain FTI records in the designated IEVS locked storage\nfile. The folders on the cases in which data continue to be received should be\nretained. If no FTI is received for the current year or the previous year, the FTI\npreviously received can be destroyed by shredding or incinerating. A destruction log\nshould be maintained of all destroyed Form 209s including those on cases where an\ninvestigation was not necessary.\n\n\n#### D.6.f. DISPOSAL OF RECORD\n\nThe central office will maintain a copy of the FTI return data and the completed\nForm 209 for two years. The destruction of completed Form 209s that were\nsubmitted to the state office will be the responsibility of the IEVS Coordinator.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-14" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00024 -->\n\n\n## II.E. ASSET VERIFICATION SYSTEM\n\nSection 1940 of Title VII requires Medicaid to implement an electronic Financial\nInstitution (FI) Verification system for verifying the assets of the aged, blind, and\ndisabled Medicaid applicants and recipients. The Asset Verification System (AVS),\nwill be used in determining financial eligibility. The AVS will search for open, closed,\nlocal, and out-of-state financial accounts from disclosed and non-disclosed FIs.\n\n\n### E.1. TRANSFER OF ASSETS\n\nWhen comparing the response to what the applicant has reported, if the response\nreveals an applicant gave away or sold assets for less than its fair market value, then\nit is presumed that they were transferred for the purpose of qualifying or remaining\neligible for Medicaid. However, all transferred assets can be returned to the\napplicant to negate the transfer (as if it never happened). If only a partial amount of\nthe assets is returned, credit should be given for the partial amount returned.\n\n\n#### E.2. REBUTTAL OF TRANSFER\n\nThe applicant or recipient has the burden to rebut the presumption that the transfer\nwas made for the purpose of qualifying or maintaining Medicaid eligibility. The\napplicant or recipient must be informed and provided the opportunity to rebut the\npresumption prior to the imposition of a transfer penalty. The applicant must\nprovide clear and convincing evidence that the transfer was exclusively for another\npurpose. Acceptable evidence that the transfer did not occur for the purposes of\nqualifying or maintaining Medicaid eligibility may be a submitted on a "Statement of\nClaimant or Other Person" (Form 234). See also Chapter 6 Section 0.1.d.\n\n\n#### E.3. INELIGIBILITY WORKS UNDUE HARDSHIP\n\nIf the applicant admits to transferring an asset for less than fair market value for the\npurpose of qualifying for Medicaid, an exemption from a transfer penalty may be\ngiven if the applicant can demonstrate by clear and convincing verification that the\nimposition of a transfer penalty will cause them to suffer an undue hardship. See\nalso Chapter 6 Section O.2.d.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="II-15" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00025 -->\n\n\n# Table of Content\n\n\n## List of Tables', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

For SSI eligibility, the worker should check the SDX (CARES) and/or SVES. For Medicare entitlement the worker should check the BENDEX, SVES, and/or TBQ. Any other documentation obtained from SSA that verifies SSI eligibility or Medicare entitlement is acceptable. The screen(s) used to verify SSI eligibility or Medicare entitlement should be printed and placed in the case file.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Tribal Documents</td>\n<td>Documents issued by a federally recognized Indian Tribe evidencing membership, enrollment in, or affiliation with such Tribe is satisfactory documentary proof of an individual\'s U.S. citizenship or nationality. The document must identify the federally recognized Indian Tribe which issued it, identify the individual\'s name, and confirm the individual\'s membership enrollment in or affiliation with that Tribe. Some examples include: Tribal enrollment/membership cards, Certificate of degree of Indian Blood issued by the Bureau of Indian Affairs, Tribal census document, or</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-i" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00028 -->\n\nDocument issued by a Tribe indicating an individual\'s affiliation with\nthe Tribe.\n\nNote: No additional identity documents are required.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

GENERAL CATEGORICAL ELIGIBILITY CRITERIA\n\nIn order to qualify for Medicaid in a medical institution, the claimant must meet\ngeneral categorical criteria of age, disability or blindness; residence, and citizenship\nfor the appropriate coverage group. There are certain other general categorical\nrequirements that must be met by all claimants.\n\n\n## III.B. AGE REQUIREMENT\n\nTo be eligible if not disabled, the claimant must be 65 years of age or older. This\nfactor is based on Social Security verification, birth certificate, passport or census\nreport.\n\nException: If the claimant alleges, he/she is 70 years old or older, his/her statement\nmay be accepted. The case may be certified when all other points have been\nverified. Date of birth should be verified at review or during the next action on the\ncase.\n\n\n## III.C. DISABILITY\n\nIf under age 65, a claimant must be disabled. Disability is defined as the inability to\nengage in\nany substantial gainful activity by reason of any medically determinable physical or\nmental impairment(s) which can be expected result in death, or which has lasted or\ncan be expected to last for a continuous period of not less than twelve (12) months.\n\nAn impairment is likely to result in death if:\n\n· Based on established medical knowledge, and under the particular\ncircumstances in the\ncase, it is found to be terminal; or\n\n. It actually results in death.\n\nConsider a claimant (child or adult) disabled when he/she is receiving RSDI or SSI\nbased on his/her disability or blindness. For all other persons under age 65, the Social\nSecurity Disability Determination Unit or Medicaid Medical Review Team will establish\ndisability determination upon submission of competent medical testimony. Medical\ntestimony may be in the form of written reports or copies of medical records from\nhospitals, clinics, VRS, VA, industrial organizations, etc., or written statements from\nlegally licensed physicians. Medical information based on previous examination is\nacceptable if it clearly establishes current disability. DHR Determination for\nDisability can be accepted if it is a permanent or presumptive finding. The\npresumptive finding can be accepted because disabilities that are presumed to be\napproved for SSI are normally approved for SSA.\n\nNOTE: Application for Medicaid is accepted for individuals with presumptive\ndisabilities who are not applying for SSI.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00035 -->\n\nSpecial information may be used as needed in conjunction with medical information\nto establish that a disability exists. This information must be from a knowledgeable\nsource willing to have the medical data made available to all parties during a\nhearing.\n\nNOTE: When current medical examination is needed to obtain information about\nthe disability, a determination that the claimant meets other eligibility requirements\nare made before referring the claimant for a medical examination. (A payment not\nto exceed $15 may be authorized to a physician for completion of medical\nexamination).\n\n\n## III.D. BLINDNESS\n\nA claimant meets blindness criteria when his/her central visual acuity is 20/200 or\nless (with glasses) or a limited visual field of 20 degrees or less in the better eye. A\nclaimant determined to be blind for purposes of SSI is also considered blind for\nMedicaid eligibility purposes.\n\n\n## III.E. UNITED STATES CITIZENSHIP\n\nFor purposes of qualifying as a United States citizen, the United States, as defined in\nthe Immigration and Nationality Act, includes the 50 states, the District of Columbia,\nPuerto Rico, Guam, Virgin Islands, and the Northern Mariana Islands. Nationals from\nAmerican Samoa or Swain\'s Island shall also be regarded as United States citizens for\npurposes of Medicaid.\n\nApplicants for Medicaid must:\n\n· Indicate that he/she is a citizen or national of the United States or an alien in\na satisfactory immigration status (henceforth qualified alien) as provided in\nF.1 by signing the application.\n\n· All non-citizen claimants must provide documents to establish immigration\nstatus as described in F.1 from the United States Citizenship and Immigration\nServices (USICS).\n\nUse the documents presented to verify immigration status in accordance with the\nprovisions of F.1. We are required to provide Medicaid eligibility pending verification\nof immigration status to an individual who claims to meet requirements in paragraph\nF.1. or F.6. and who meets all other non-immigration Medicaid eligibility\nrequirements.\n\nMedicaid applicants/recipients who declare under penalty of perjury to be a citizen\nor national of the United States must provide documentation of citizenship and\nidentity.\n\nThe Deficit Reduction Act (DRA) of 2005 requires states to document U.S. citizenship\nand identity. Medicaid applicants/recipients, who declare under penalty of perjury\nto be a citizen or national of the United States, must provide documentation of\ncitizenship and identity. Only family members applying for or receiving medical\nbenefits are required to provide documentation.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00036 -->\n\nThe law specifies certain forms of acceptable documentary evidence of citizenship\nand identity and provides for the use of additional forms of documentation as\nestablished by federal regulations.\n\n\n### E.1. EXEMPT INDIVIDUALS\n\nThe citizenship and identity documentation requirement will not apply to individuals\ndeclaring to be citizens or nationals of the United States who are eligible for\nMedicaid and:\n\n· are entitled to or enrolled for Medicare benefits,\n\n· are receiving Supplemental Security Income (SSI) benefits,\n\n· are receiving Social Security benefits because of their own disability (Social\nSecurity Disability Income (SSDI) only)\n\n· with respect to whom child welfare services are made available under Title IV-\nB of Social Security Act; or\n\n· with respect to whom adoption or foster care assistance is made available\nunder Title IV-E of the Social Security Act.\n\nSupplemental Security Income (SSI) recipients and Medicare beneficiaries requesting\nMedicaid are exempt from the citizenship/identity documentary requirement since\nproviding this documentation is a condition of eligibility for the SSI and Medicare\nprograms. When an applicant/recipient indicates current SSI eligibility or Medicare\nentitlement, the worker should conduct a database cross match.\n\nNote: For SSI eligibility, the worker should check the SDX (CARES) and SVES. For\nMedicare entitlement, the worker should check the BENDEX, SVES, and/or TBQ. Any\nother documentation obtained from SSA that verifies SSI eligibility or Medicare\nentitlement is acceptable. The screen(s) used to verify SSI eligibility or Medicare\nentitlement should be printed and placed in the case file.\n\n\n### E.2. ACCEPTABLE DOCUMENTATION\n\nThe law specifies certain forms of acceptable documentary evidence of citizenship\nand identity and provides for the use of additional forms of documentation as\nestablished by federal regulations.\n\nTo establish U.S. citizenship, documentary evidence must show:\n\n\\- A U.S. place of birth, or\n\n\\- That the person is a U.S. citizen.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

Therefore, citizenship and\nidentity documentation is not required at review or anytime thereafter\nfor deemed newborns.\n\n\n### E.3. PRESENTATION OF DOCUMENTS\n\nAll documents presented by the applicant/recipient must be either originals or copies\ncertified by the issuing agency. A face-to face interview is not required for\npresentation of documentation. Medicaid staff, Medicaid application assistors, other\nauthorized state agency staff, Medicaid Maternity Care staff, and staff at private\noutstation sites who have been authorized by the on-site coordinator may accept\noriginal documents in person, by mail, or by a person with legal authority to represent\nthe claimant.\n\n\\- If the applicant/recipient presents the original or a certified copy, the\nMedicaid worker should make a photocopy of the document(s) and return the\noriginal or certified copy to the applicant/recipient.\n\n\\- If the original or a certified copy of a document is received in the mail, the\nMedicaid worker should make a photocopy of the document and return the\noriginal or certified copy to the applicant/recipient by regular mail.\n\nAfter copying the original or certified copy the Medicaid worker should:\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-8" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00042 -->\n\n\\- Use an accepted agency stamp or notate in red on the photocopy that the\noriginal or a certified copy of the document was presented, date, and initial;\nand,\n\n\\- Make an entry in the Record of Contact indicating the type document received,\nand the fact that the original or a certified copy was presented; As a check against fraud, workers must\nconduct a SVES match of each applicant\'s/recipient\'s name against the corresponding\nsocial security number that was provided and place a copy of the SVES printout in the\ncase file.\n\nThe Medicaid worker must also update the computer system using the appropriate\ncitizenship code. Once documentation of citizenship and identity has been received\nand recorded in Medicaid\'s database, subsequent changes in eligibility will not require\nrepeating documentation unless later evidence raises a question of the individual\'s\ncitizenship or there is a gap of more than three years between the individual\'s last\nperiod of eligibility and subsequent application for Medicaid.\n\n\n### E.4. REASONABLE OPPORTUNITY\n\nThe primary responsibility for documenting citizenship and identity (C & I) is that of\nthe applicant/recipient. The worker must assist individuals to secure satisfactory\ndocumentary evidence of citizenship when, because of homelessness, incapacity of\nmind or body, the applicant/recipient would be unable to comply with the\nrequirement to present satisfactory documentary evidence of citizenship in a timely\nmanner and the applicant/recipient lacks a representative to assist him/her.\nProvisions in the Children\'s Health Insurance Program Reauthorization Act (CHIPRA) of\n2009 require states to provide Medicaid coverage to individuals declaring to be\ncitizens or nationals of the U.S. who are otherwise eligible, except for documentation\nof C & I, until they have been given a reasonable opportunity to document C & I.\nUnder CHIPRA provisions, the Alabama Medicaid Agency started using the State\nVerification Exchange System (SVES) to document citizenship for applications received\non or after that date. When a data match with SVES is used to document citizenship\nfor an individual who is otherwise eligible and SSA indicates the individual\'s\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-9" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00043 -->\n\ndeclaration of citizenship/nationality is inconsistent with SSA\'s files, the worker must\nmake a reasonable effort to identify and address the cause(s) of any inconsistencies,\nincluding the correction of a clerical error and continue Medicaid coverage. If\nattempts to resolve the discrepancies are unsuccessful, coverage must be continued\nand the individual must be notified in writing that s/he has 90 days from the date the\nnotice is received to either resolve the inconsistencies or provide C & I documents.\n\nIf the inconsistency is not resolved and C & I documents are not received by the end\nof the 90- day period, Medicaid coverage must be terminated within 30 days after the\nend of the 90-day period. Individuals who meet the eligibility requirements of Medicaid\nbut are not citizens of the United States are Medicaid eligible only if they meet\nspecific requirements. Those non-citizens who do not meet the specific requirements\nmay be eligible to receive treatment for an emergency medical condition as defined\nin F.9.\n\nMedicaid eligibility for aliens is based on whether the alien is a qualified or non-\nqualified alien and whether their date of arrival in the U.S. was before, on, or after\nAugust 22, 1996 (date of welfare reform law). QUALIFIED ALIENS\n\nA qualified alien is an alien who is a:\n\n-A lawful, permanent resident,\n-A refugee,\n-An asylee,\n\n-An alien who has had deportation withheld under section 243(H) of the Immigration\nand Nationality Act (INA)\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00045 -->\n\n-An alien granted parole for at least 1 year by the USCIS, or\n\n-An alien granted conditional entry under immigration law in effect before April 1,\n1980.\n\nThis definition eliminates most of the PRUCOL categories as well as PRUCOL as an\neligibility classification.\n\n\n## F.2. HOME REPLACEMENT FUNDS- (DESTRUCTION)\n\nIf a home is destroyed and funds are received for the replacement of a home, the\nclaimant has 9 months in which to replace the home. The funds are not counted as\nincome or resources for 9 months if the claimant or sponsor makes a statement on the\n"Statement of Claimant or Other Person" form that he/she will use the money to\nreplace his/her home within 9 months.\n\nExample: Mrs. Jones is a Medicaid claimant residing in a nursing home. She owns 1/2\ninterest in homestead property which is excluded as a resource because Mr. Jones\nresides there. The house burns and Mr. and Mrs. Jones receive a $50,000.00 insurance\nsettlement. They make a statement that they will replace their home. The\n$50,000.00 is not considered income the month in which it is received nor is it\nconsidered resources until 9 months after receipt.\n\n\n## M.2. INCOME PRODUCING PROPERTY DEFINITION\n\nProperty the claimant (or spouse, if any) has as interest in that generates income from\na third party that is received by the claimant (or spouse, if any) at least annually.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-19" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00106 -->\n\n\n## M.2.a. INSTITUTIONAL AND WAIVERS CASES\n\nIncome-producing property can have up to $6,000 of the equity value excluded if the\nproperty is producing income as of the first moment of the first day of the month;\notherwise, the full value of the property is a countable resource.\n\nIf the equity value of the property is in excess of $6,000, the amount in excess will be\ncounted toward the resource limit as long as the claimant remains eligible and the\nproperty produces income.\n\nIf at the time of the annual review the equity value of the income-producing property\nis in excess of $6,000 and the amount in excess exceeds the resource limit (or the\namount in excess combined with other resources exceeds the resource limit) the case\nshould be terminated.\n\nNOTE: Property does not have to be income-producing if it, in combination with other\nresources, does not exceed the resource limit. THE PROPERTY PRODUCES INCOME\n\nProperty that is excluded as income-producing property does not have to receive an\nannual net income of at least 6% of the equity to meet the income-producing exclusion\nas long as it is income-producing.\n\n\n# M.2.b.1. SSI-RELATED CASES\n\nSSI related cases [i.e., Pickle, Ping Pong, Retroactive, DAC, Widow(er)] must continue\nto receive an annual net income of at least 6% of the equity value to meet the\nincome-producing property exclusion.\n\n\n# M.2.b.2. REVIEW PROCEDURES\n\nIncome-producing property should be evaluated at each annual review. If income is\nnot received in a particular month or quarter during the year, the budget must be\nadjusted. The claimant is still considered eligible for Medicaid until the case is\nevaluated at the annual review. If, at that time, the property has received no income\nduring the year, it is a countable resource.\n\n\n# M.2.c. This category includes:\n\n\\- Property used in a trade or business;\n\n\\- Property that represents government authority to engage in an\nincome-producing activity (examples - commercial fishing permits granted by a\nState Commerce Commission and tobacco crop allotments issued by the U.S.\nDepartment of Agriculture);\n\n\\- Personal property used by an individual as an employee for work (tools, safety\nequipment, uniforms, etc.); and\n\n\\- Personal property required by an employer for work.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-20" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00107 -->\n\nNOTE: All liquid resources used in the operation of a trade or business are excluded as\nproperty essential for self support. BONA FIDE EFFORT TO SELL\n\nAny real property will be excluded from resources as long as reasonable efforts are\nbeing made to sell the property by someone with legal authority to sell. Should the\nclaimant be incompetent, the appointment of a person with legal authority to\nrepresent the claimant may be necessary. The property will be a countable resource\nuntil the first of the month following the date of filing a petition to appoint a person\nwith legal authority to represent the claimant to sell the property. These individuals\nwill be eligible on the first day of the month in which the petition is filed if all other\npoints of eligibility are met. This period shall not exceed 120 days for Medicaid\neligibility. The property will again be a countable resource on the first of the month\nfollowing the appointment until the person with legal authority to represent the\nclaimant makes a bona fide effort to sell the property. Reasonable effort to sell\nmeans that a claimant must attempt to sell the property by listing it with a real estate\nagent or by attempting to sell it himself/herself. The effort must be continuous with\nno gaps that exceed one week in duration. Evidence must show the date of the\nactions to sell the property to verify that the property was actually on the market as\nof the first moment of the month.\n\nThe bona fide effort to sell exclusion may be applied only for the month in which all\nrequirements for the exclusion have been met as of the first moment of the month.\nThe claimant must complete Form 226, Agreement to Sell, (claimant or someone who\nhas legal authority to sell must sign) and provide evidence of 1 of the following:\n\no That the property is listed with an agent; or\n\no That the property is being privately sold. The claimant must furnish\nverification:\n\n\\- that the property is continuously being advertised in at least one\nnewspaper or other local media and\n\n\\- that a "For Sale" sign has been placed on the property, and\n\n\\- that the claimant has begun to conduct "Open House," or otherwise show\nthe property to interested parties on a continuous basis,\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-21" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00108 -->\n\no The claimant should provide evidence of attempts of any other appropriate\nmethods of sale.\n\nFor property to qualify for the bona fide effort to sell exclusion in a month, the above\nevidence of sale must show that the property was on the market as of the first\nmoment of that month. Medicaid coverage is\nrequested beginning August 1. No information regarding citizenship will be provided. To be\nawarded Medicaid and then receive the 90-day reasonable opportunity period, the\nindividual must meet all other points of eligibility including verification of SSN.\nEligibility cannot be determined if the individual\'s SSN is not verified as enumeration\nis a point of eligibility that must be established.\n\nIf the data submitted by Medicaid matches, the SSN will be verified and SVES will\ndisplay a one-byte SSN verification code of "V", "F", "M", "P", "R", "X", or "Z". The\nSVES page will display a code to indicate whether the declaration of citizenship is\nconsistent with SSA data.\n\nWhen the declaration of citizenship is inconsistent with data in SSA files, a SVES\ncitizenship code of "UB" or "UD" will be displayed to indicate the declaration of\ncitizenship is inconsistent with data in the SSA files. The "UB" code denotes a person\nwho is not known to be a citizen and citizenship has not been verified by SSA. The\n"UD" code denotes a person who is not known to be a citizen and is now deceased.\n\nWhen the declaration of citizenship is consistent with data in SSA files, a copy of the\nSVES page showing citizenship code "DA" should be printed, placed in the file, and\nthe record of contact updated. (Code "DA" indicates the person is a citizen and\ncitizenship has been verified by SSA. Documentation of both citizenship and identity\nare considered met when a "DA" is indicated.) SVES citizenship code "DC" will\nrequire further investigation as this indicates the person was a citizen and the SSN\nbelongs to a deceased citizen.\n\nThe usual processing procedures should be followed when eligibility cannot be\ndetermined because the individual failed to provide C & I documentation and other\nnecessary information (i.e. income verification, insurance information, etc.) in\nresponse to a 30-day checklist. If the information is not received within the 30-day\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-10" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00044 -->\n\ntimeframe and additional time has not been requested, the case should be denied or\nterminated.\n\nNOTE:\n\nWhen a SVES data match is used to document citizenship for an individual who is\notherwise eligible and the data indicates the individual\'s declaration of citizenship or\nnationality is consistent with SSA\'s files, documentation of C & I is considered to have\nbeen met.\n\nWhen a SVES data match is not used to document citizenship, the individual should be\ngiven a 90-day reasonable opportunity period to provide satisfactory C & I documents.\nCD Table 1 through CD Table 4 should be used sequentially to obtain the most reliable\nform of citizenship documentation available. ID Table should be used for acceptable\ndocumentation of identity.\n\nPolicy regarding the groups of individuals who are exempt from the C & I\ndocumentation requirement still applies (Medicare beneficiaries, SSI recipients, SSDI\nrecipients, children receiving child welfare and foster care assistance under Title IV-\nB, children receiving adoption or foster care assistance under Title IV-E of the Social\nSecurity Act, and deemed newborns).\n\nIII.F.\nALIENS\n\nThe Personal Responsibility and Work Opportunity Reconciliation Act of 1996 (P.L.\n104-193) significantly changed Medicaid eligibility for individuals who are not citizens\nof the United States. Individuals who meet the eligibility requirements of Medicaid\nbut are not citizens of the United States are Medicaid eligible only if they meet\nspecific requirements. Those non-citizens who do not meet the specific requirements\nmay be eligible to receive treatment for an emergency medical condition as defined\nin F.9.\n\nMedicaid eligibility for aliens is based on whether the alien is a qualified or non-\nqualified alien and whether their date of arrival in the U.S. was before, on, or after\nAugust 22, 1996 (date of welfare reform law). A qualified alien may or may not be\neligible. A non-qualified alien or an ineligible alien is only eligible for emergency\nservices. The previous categories of lawful permanent residents and aliens\npermanently residing in the United States under color of law (PRUCOL) no longer\napply.\n\n\n### F.1. QUALIFIED ALIENS\n\nA qualified alien is an alien who is a:\n\n-A lawful, permanent resident,\n-A refugee,\n-An asylee,\n\n-An alien who has had deportation withheld under section 243(H) of the Immigration\nand Nationality Act (INA)\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-11" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00045 -->\n\n-An alien granted parole for at least 1 year by the USCIS, or\n\n-An alien granted conditional entry under immigration law in effect before April 1,\n1980.\n\nThis definition eliminates most of the PRUCOL categories as well as PRUCOL as an\neligibility classification.\n\n\n## F.2. QUALIFIED ALIENS IN THE U.S. BEFORE 8/22/96\n\nAliens who are members of the following groups and who otherwise meet Medicaid\neligibility requirements are eligible for Medicaid if they were living in the United\nStates before 8/22/96:\n\n-A lawful permanent resident,\n\n-American Indian Born in Canada. An American Indian born in Canada may freely\nenter and reside in the U. S. and is considered to be lawfully admitted for permanent\nresidence if he/she is of at least one-half American Indian blood. As such, he/she is a\nqualified alien. This does not include a spouse or child of such an Indian nor a non-\ncitizen whose membership in an Indian tribe or family is created by adoption, unless\nsuch person is of at least 50 percent or more Indian blood. Such aliens are to be\nconsidered qualified aliens because they are considered lawful permanent residents.\nNOTE: Examples of acceptable types of documentation are birth or\nbaptismal certificate issued on a reservation, Tribal records, letter from\nthe Canadian Department of Indian Affairs, or school records.\n\n-A refugee (207 of INA),\n\n-An asylee (208 of INA),\n\n-An alien who has had deportation withheld under 243(h) of the Immigration and\nNationality Act (INA),\n\n-An alien granted parole for at least 1 year by the CIS (21(d)(5) of INA),\n\n-An alien granted conditional entry under 203(a)(7) of the immigration law in effect\nbefore April 1, 1980,\n\n-A battered immigrant who meets certain requirements,\n\n-Honorably discharged veterans and aliens on active duty in the United States armed\nforces and the spouse or unmarried dependent child(ren) of such individuals,\n\n-Cuban and Haitian (208) entrants; and\n\n-Amerasian (209) immigrants.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-12" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00046 -->\n\n\n## F.3. HOME REPLACEMENT FUNDS- (DESTRUCTION)\n\nIf a home is destroyed and funds are received for the replacement of a home, the\nclaimant has 9 months in which to replace the home. The funds are not counted as\nincome or resources for 9 months if the claimant or sponsor makes a statement on the\n"Statement of Claimant or Other Person" form that he/she will use the money to\nreplace his/her home within 9 months.\n\nExample: Mrs. Jones is a Medicaid claimant residing in a nursing home. She owns 1/2\ninterest in homestead property which is excluded as a resource because Mr. Jones\nresides there. The house burns and Mr. and Mrs. Jones receive a $50,000.00 insurance\nsettlement. They make a statement that they will replace their home. The\n$50,000.00 is not considered income the month in which it is received nor is it\nconsidered resources until 9 months after receipt.\n\n\n## M.2. INCOME PRODUCING PROPERTY DEFINITION\n\nProperty the claimant (or spouse, if any) has as interest in that generates income from\na third party that is received by the claimant (or spouse, if any) at least annually.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-19" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00106 -->\n\n\n## M.2.a. INSTITUTIONAL AND WAIVERS CASES\n\nIncome-producing property can have up to $6,000 of the equity value excluded if the\nproperty is producing income as of the first moment of the first day of the month;\notherwise, the full value of the property is a countable resource.\n\nIf the equity value of the property is in excess of $6,000, the amount in excess will be\ncounted toward the resource limit as long as the claimant remains eligible and the\nproperty produces income.\n\nIf at the time of the annual review the equity value of the income-producing property\nis in excess of $6,000 and the amount in excess exceeds the resource limit (or the\namount in excess combined with other resources exceeds the resource limit) the case\nshould be terminated.\n\nNOTE: Property does not have to be income-producing if it, in combination with other\nresources, does not exceed the resource limit. However, a lien may be required. (See\nVI.P. for policy regarding liens).\n\n\n# M.2.b. THE PROPERTY PRODUCES INCOME\n\nProperty that is excluded as income-producing property does not have to receive an\nannual net income of at least 6% of the equity to meet the income-producing exclusion\nas long as it is income-producing.\n\n\n# M.2.b.1. SSI-RELATED CASES\n\nSSI related cases [i.e., Pickle, Ping Pong, Retroactive, DAC, Widow(er)] must continue\nto receive an annual net income of at least 6% of the equity value to meet the\nincome-producing property exclusion.\n\n\n# M.2.b.2. REVIEW PROCEDURES\n\nIncome-producing property should be evaluated at each annual review. If income is\nnot received in a particular month or quarter during the year, the budget must be\nadjusted. The claimant is still considered eligible for Medicaid until the case is\nevaluated at the annual review. If, at that time, the property has received no income\nduring the year, it is a countable resource.\n\n\n# M.2.c. PROPERTY ESSENTIAL TO SELF-SUPPORT\n\nProperty essential to self-support may be excluded regardless of its value or rate of\nreturn. This category includes:\n\n\\- Property used in a trade or business;\n\n\\- Property that represents government authority to engage in an\nincome-producing activity (examples - commercial fishing permits granted by a\nState Commerce Commission and tobacco crop allotments issued by the U.S.\nDepartment of Agriculture);\n\n\\- Personal property used by an individual as an employee for work (tools, safety\nequipment, uniforms, etc.); and\n\n\\- Personal property required by an employer for work.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-20" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00107 -->\n\nNOTE: All liquid resources used in the operation of a trade or business are excluded as\nproperty essential for self support. The resource must be in current use. If it is not in\ncurrent use, for reasons beyond the claimant\'s control, there must be a reasonable\nexpectation that the required use will resume.\n\nWhen a claimant alleges owning trade or business property which is not already\nexcluded, consider if a valid trade or business exists and if the property is in current\nuse. Obtain a statement (Statement of Claimant or Other Person form) which includes\nthe following information:\n\n\\- A description of the trade or business;\n\n\\- A description of the assets of the trade or business;\n\n\\- The number of years it has been operating;\n\n\\- The identity of any co-owners;\n\n\\- Estimated gross and net earnings of the trade or business for the current tax\nyear.\n\nRequest a copy of the latest tax return. NOTE: This provision applies only to the\nexclusion as a resource. Income generated by the trade or business is not exempt.\n\n\n# M.3. BONA FIDE EFFORT TO SELL\n\nAny real property will be excluded from resources as long as reasonable efforts are\nbeing made to sell the property by someone with legal authority to sell. Should the\nclaimant be incompetent, the appointment of a person with legal authority to\nrepresent the claimant may be necessary. The property will be a countable resource\nuntil the first of the month following the date of filing a petition to appoint a person\nwith legal authority to represent the claimant to sell the property. These individuals\nwill be eligible on the first day of the month in which the petition is filed if all other\npoints of eligibility are met. This period shall not exceed 120 days for Medicaid\neligibility. The property will again be a countable resource on the first of the month\nfollowing the appointment until the person with legal authority to represent the\nclaimant makes a bona fide effort to sell the property. Reasonable effort to sell\nmeans that a claimant must attempt to sell the property by listing it with a real estate\nagent or by attempting to sell it himself/herself. The effort must be continuous with\nno gaps that exceed one week in duration. Evidence must show the date of the\nactions to sell the property to verify that the property was actually on the market as\nof the first moment of the month.\n\nThe bona fide effort to sell exclusion may be applied only for the month in which all\nrequirements for the exclusion have been met as of the first moment of the month.\nThe claimant must complete Form 226, Agreement to Sell, (claimant or someone who\nhas legal authority to sell must sign) and provide evidence of 1 of the following:\n\no That the property is listed with an agent; or\n\no That the property is being privately sold. The claimant must furnish\nverification:\n\n\\- that the property is continuously being advertised in at least one\nnewspaper or other local media and\n\n\\- that a "For Sale" sign has been placed on the property, and\n\n\\- that the claimant has begun to conduct "Open House," or otherwise show\nthe property to interested parties on a continuous basis,\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-21" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00108 -->\n\no The claimant should provide evidence of attempts of any other appropriate\nmethods of sale.\n\nFor property to qualify for the bona fide effort to sell exclusion in a month, the above\nevidence of sale must show that the property was on the market as of the first\nmoment of that month. When all conditions and requirements are met and bona fide\neffort to sell is demonstrated prior to signing the agreement, the lack of a signed\nagreement will not preclude the application of the bona fide effort to sell property\nexclusion.\n\nExample 1: Claimant enters the nursing home on August 15, and files an application\nfor Medicaid benefits on September 10. Proof is furnished (in form of letter from\nrealtor) that a continuous effort has been made to sell the property since April 1.\nAgreement to Sell form (Form 226) is signed on September 27. Medicaid coverage is\nrequested beginning August 1. Since the claimant has proven that "bona fide effort to\nsell" existed as of the first moment of the month in which the coverage is requested,\nthe absence of a signed agreement on that date would not preclude eligibility. The\nconditions of the "bona fide effort to sell" policy were met on August 1.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

However,\nclaimant is not competent and person with legal authority to represent the claimant\nmust be appointed before property can be sold. The court is petitioned and on\nOctober 12, a person with legal authority to represent the claimant is appointed.\nSubsequently, the Agreement to Sell form is signed. Therefore, the property cannot\nbe excluded on the "bona fide effort to sell" provision until November 1st, if the\nrequirements are all met as of that date.\n\nThe claimant must accept any reasonable offer to buy (tax appraised value) and has\nthe burden of demonstrating that an offer was rejected because it was not reasonable.\nThe claimant must present evidence to establish that the rejected offer was\nunreasonable. The bona fide effort to sell should be redeveloped every six months or\nat the end of the contract.\n\nExample 3: A claimant\'s property is appraised at the Tax Assessor\'s Office for\n$15,000.00. The property is listed for $25,000.00 and an offer of $15,000.00 is made.\nThe claimant must furnish evidence showing why the offer was rejected (i.e.,\nestimates from other realtors, timber companies, or other knowledgeable sources).\n\n\n## M.3.a. CASE FILE, INTERIM ACTION\n\nA reevaluation of the bona-fide effort to sell will be conducted every six months or at\nthe end of the contract using Agency Form-254 (Bona Fide Re-evaluation). The\nclaimant must demonstrate continued efforts to sell in order to maintain the\nexclusion. The evidence will be kept in the case file and a summary included in the\nRecord of Contact.\n\nNOTE: Even though the value of the property is excluded as a resource, a lien may be\nrequired.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-22" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00109 -->\n\n\n## M.3.b. SALE FOR LESS THAN FAIR MARKET VALUE\n\nProperty which is for sale or is sold for less than Current Market Value (CMV) must be\nevaluated to determine if there is or will be uncompensated value. The claimant must\nsubmit evidence to establish that the offer is reasonable to support claims on the Fair\nMarket Value Assessment form.\n\nThe following procedure will place the responsibility for the verification of the sale for\nless than fair market value on the realtor. This procedure is for situations involving\nsale offers and not ordinary valuation of property. When the district office is\ncontacted by a realtor, with an offer which is less than fair market value, the worker\nshould immediately fax a copy of the attached Fair Market Value Assessment form to\nthe realtor. If the Liens Officer receives the call from the realtor, he/she will fax the\nform to the realtor with instructions to return the form to the district office handling\nthe case. In both situations the name, address, etc. of the district office should be\nshown at the top of the form. Enter the realtor\'s name and address and/or fax number\nin the space provided. Complete the claimant\'s information (name and Social Security\nnumber) and enter the address of the property in the space provided. The burden of\ngathering the information and submitting it will be on the realtor.\n\nOnce the district office receives the fax and documentation from the realtor, the\ndistrict office manager/assistant manager will evaluate the documentation and use the\nfollowing criteria to determine whether the sale of the property for less than fair\nmarket value would incur a penalty:\n\na. If the property has been on the market less than one (1) year, evaluate the\nreason(s) for the offered lower value. If the realtor can provide verification\nthat the cost of repairs meets or exceeds the difference between the tax\nappraised value and the offered lower value, approve the sale with no\npenalty.\n\nb. If the same conditions apply in the above paragraph except that the cost of\nrepairs does not meet or exceed the difference between the tax appraised\nvalue and the offered lower value, verify, by declaration from the sponsor\nthat the value of the property is under appeal with the Board of\nEqualization. If the Board of Equalization has not lowered the value, send\nthe fax and documentation attached on an Interpretation Request form\ninclude any private appraisals provided. This will facilitate the legal staff in\nprocessing the Interpretation Request.\n\nc. If the property has been on the market more than one (1) year and the\ndifference between the tax appraised value and the offered lower value is\nattributed to repair cost. Evaluate the cost of repairs to get the difference\nin the value of the property and the cost to repair it. If the difference\nbetween the tax appraised value and the offered price plus the estimated\ncost to repair is equal to zero (0), approve the sale. If it is greater than zero\n(0), consider the value of comparable property in the area. If comparable\nproperty in the area is selling for the offered price, approve the sale and do\nnot assign a penalty.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-23" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00110 -->\n\nd. If the district office is unable to make a decision based on the information\n(not just confirmation of whether or not their decision is correct), submit\nthe information on an Interpretation Request to the area\'s regional\nsupervisor.\n\nOnce the district office manager/assistant manager has made the decision and\ncompleted the section of the Fair Market Value Assessment form indicating the\ndecision, fax the form to the Liens Officer. The district office manager/assistant\nmanager should verbally confirm the approval or disapproval to the realtor, but may\nnot fax a copy of the Fair Market Assessment form to the realtor, after it is completed.\nShould the sale for less than fair market value be disapproved, explain the reason for\ndisapproval. For example, the realtor did not provide adequate information regarding\nthe needed repairs or there was not information provided regarding comparable\nproperty values in the area. Re-evaluate the request when the realtor provides\nadditional information.\n\nOnce the Liens Officer receives the approval for the sale for less than fair market\nvalue, he/she will proceed with releasing the lien when the sale is complete. A copy\nof the settlement statement will be provided to the district office.\n\n\n# M.4. LOSS OF HOUSING FOR JOINT OWNER\n\nReal property will be excluded from resources as long as:\n\n\\- It is jointly owned, and\n\n\\- Sale of the property would cause the other owner undue hardship due to loss\nof housing.\n\nNOTE: Undue hardship would result when:\n\n\\- Property serves as the principal place of residence for one of the other\nowners.\n\n\\- Sale of property would result in loss of that residence, and\n\n\\- No other housing would be readily available for the displaced other owner\n(e.g., the other owner does not own another house that is legally available for\noccupancy).\n\nIf undue hardship ceases to exist, the value of the property will become a countable\nresource. This exclusion has to be redeveloped every six months. The Joint Owner\nStatement form is used to develop and/or redevelop this exclusion.\n\nNOTE: A lien may be required unless the joint owner is a sibling with an equity\ninterest.\n\nNOTE: In joint tenancy each of two or more joint tenants has an equal interest in the\nwhole property for the duration of the tenancy. A life estate grants the life tenant\nthe right to the use and enjoyment of a piece of property for the term of his natural\nlife. Therefore, a life estate is not considered joint tenancy.\n\n\n# M.5. DEFINITIONS LIFE INSURANCE\n\nA life insurance policy is a contract. Its purchaser (the owner) pays premiums to the\ncompany that provides the insurance (the insurer). In return, the insurer agrees to\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-24" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00111 -->\n\npay a specific sum to a designated beneficiary upon the death of the insured (the\nperson on whom, or on whose life, the policy exists).\n\n\n# M.5.a. FACE VALUE\n\nFace Value (FV) is the amount of basic death benefit contracted for at the time the\npolicy is purchased. The face page of the policy may show it as such, or as the\n"amount of insurance," "the amount of this policy," "the sum insured," etc. A policy\'s\nFV does not include:\n\n\\- The FV of any dividend addition, which is added after the policy, is issued;\n\n\\- Additional sums payable in the event of accidental death or because of other\nspecial provisions; or\n\n\\- The amount(s) of term insurance, when a policy provides whole life coverage for\none family member and term coverage for the other(s).\n\n\n# M.5.b. CASH SURRENDER VALUE\n\nA policy\'s cash surrender value (CSV) is a form of equity value that it acquires over\ntime. The owner of a policy can obtain its CSV only by turning in the policy for\ncancellation before it matures or the insured dies. A loan against a policy reduces its\nCSV.\n\n\n# M.5.c. DIVIDENDS', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

CASH SURRENDER VALUE\n\nA policy\'s cash surrender value (CSV) is a form of equity value that it acquires over\ntime. The owner of a policy can obtain its CSV only by turning in the policy for\ncancellation before it matures or the insured dies. A loan against a policy reduces its\nCSV.\n\n\n# M.5.c. DIVIDENDS\n\nPeriodically (annually, as a rule), the insurer may pay a share of any surplus company\nearnings to the policy owner as a dividend.\n\nDepending on the Life Insurance Company and type of policy involved, dividends can\nbe applied to premiums due or paid by check or by an addition or accumulation to an\nexisting policy.\n\n\n# M.5.d. DIVIDEND ADDITIONS\n\nDividend additions are amounts of insurance purchased with dividends that are added\nto the policy to increase its death benefit and CSV. The table which comes with the\npolicy does not reflect the added CSV of any dividend additions. They are not added\nto the FV of the original policy for the purpose of determining whether the policy is a\ncountable resource.\n\n\n# M.5.e. DIVIDEND ACCUMULATIONS\n\nDividend accumulations are dividends that the policy owner has constructively\nreceived but left in the custody of the insurer to accumulate at interest, like money in\na bank account. They are not a value of the policy per se; the owner can obtain them\nat any time without affecting the policy\'s FV or CSV.\n\nDividend accumulations are a countable resource and cannot be excluded from\nresources under the life insurance exclusion, even if the policy that pays the\naccumulation is excluded from resources. In order for the accumulations to be\nexcluded under the burial fund provision, they must be separately designated for\nburial (i.e., as set aside for burial).\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-25" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00112 -->\n\n\n# M.5.f. PROCEEDS\n\nProceeds of a life insurance policy are the FV of the policy plus any additions payable\nat maturity or death. Proceeds do not include dividends or interest left to accumulate\nin the policy. Also, proceeds do not include a policy\'s CSV.\n\n\n# M.6. INSURANCE POLICY EXCLUSIONS\n\nFor institutional Medicaid cases the cash surrender value of life insurance is excluded\nif the total face value of all policies, owned by the individual, does not exceed $5,000.\nFor HCBS waiver cases that use the institutional income limit, the cash surrender value\nof life insurance is excluded if the total of all policies owned by the individual does\nnot exceed $5,000.\n\nFor SSI-related Medicaid programs certified by the Medicaid district offices the cash\nsurrender value of life insurance with a face value of up to $1,500.00 is excluded.\nCash surrender value of insurance policies with a face value over $1,500.00 must be\ndesignated for burial or counted as a countable resource.\n\nIf the owner is not listed on the policy, the insured is assumed to be the owner. Three\ntypes of life insurance are (1) whole life (or ordinary); (2) burial; (3) term (group).\n\n\n# M.6.a. WHOLE LIFE INSURANCE\n\nWhole life (or ordinary, limited payment life, or endowment) policies are considered\nin determining the $1,500/$5,000 limit. This type of insurance is a contract for which\nthe insurer pays the face amount to the beneficiary upon the death of the insured.\n\nThese policies usually have a cash surrender value after the second year. The\nclaimant must own the policy in order for it to be counted as a resource. Ownership is\nassumed unless proven otherwise.\n\nDo not include the face value of dividend additions in determining whether a policy is\na countable or an excludable resource. If the policy is a countable resource, the CSV\nof dividend additions are included in determining the resource value of the policy.\n\n\n# M.6.b. BURIAL INSURANCE\n\nBurial insurance is defined as "insurance whose terms specifically provide that the\nproceeds can be used only to pay the burial expenses of the insured." For Medicaid\npurposes, a burial insurance policy with CSV is not considered burial insurance. Even\nthough the policy may be labeled "burial insurance" and names a funeral home as\nbeneficiary, it should be treated as a whole life insurance policy with CSV. The face\nvalue of a burial insurance policy that does not generate CSV will not be taken into\naccount when determining the total face value of life insurance owned by an\nindividual. However, the policy will be treated as an irrevocable arrangement and will\nreduce the burial fund exclusion by the policy\'s face value.\n\n\n# M.6.c. TERM INSURANCE\n\nTerm (group) insurance is a contract of temporary protection. The insured pays a\npremium for a specific number of years and the company pays the face value if the\ninsured dies within the time specified in the policy. The face value does not count\ntoward the $1,500/ $5,000 face value limit for life insurance exclusion if the policy\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-26" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00113 -->\n\nnever will have cash surrender value. Term (group) insurance does not affect the\nburial fund exclusion when it has no cash surrender value.\n\n\n## M.6.d. BURIAL FUNDS, SPACES, AND PLOTS\n\nBurial funds can be excluded under certain conditions whereas burial spaces are\nexcluded regardless of value.\n\n\n### M.6.d. 1. BURIAL FUNDS\n\nBurial funds are defined as:\n\n\\- revocable burial contracts;\n\n\\- revocable burial trusts;\n\n\\- other revocable burial arrangements;\n\n\\- cash;\n\n\\- financial accounts (e.g., savings or checking accounts); or\n\n\\- other financial instruments with a definite cash value (e.g. stocks, bonds, CDs,\netc.)\n\nProperty other than that listed in the definition above will not be considered burial\nfunds and may not be excluded under the burial funds provision.\n\n\n### M.6.d.2. BURIAL FUND LIMIT\n\nFor institutional cases, funds designated for burial are limited to $5,000 for a claimant\nand an additional $5,000 for the spouse. The burial fund limit is the same for HCBS\nwaiver cases governed by institutional eligibility rules.\n\nThe limit for other Medicaid programs (i.e., SSI Related) certified by the Medicaid\ndistrict office is $1,500 for a claimant and an additional $1,500 for a spouse unless\notherwise notified.\n\nException: The Medicare Savings Programs are not subject to the resource test.\nTherefore, burial fund development is not required for these programs.\n\nThe $1,500/$5,000 maximum allowance is reduced by the face value of a life\ninsurance policy owned by the claimant on his/her life. If the cash surrender value\nwas excluded when computing countable resources (i.e., the face value of the policy is\n$1,500/$5,000 or less), the burial fund is not considered full. If the face value\nexceeds the $1,500/$5,000 limit, the cash surrender value may be set aside as a burial\nfund. If the cash surrender value is set aside as a part of the burial fund, it counts\ntoward the $1,500/$5,000 limit. In addition, the $1,500/$5,000 maximum burial fund\nexclusion is further reduced by the amount of any irrevocable burial arrangement for\nthe purpose of meeting the claimant\'s or spouse burial expenses. Excludable burial\nspace items included in irrevocable agreements should be deducted prior to it\naffecting the $1,500/$5,000 maximum burial fund limit.\n\nPrepaid revocable burial contracts, owned by someone other than the claimant or\nspouse, are not considered as the claimant\'s resource and are not considered in the\nburial exclusion development. Ownership of the burial contract will be determined by\nwhose money was used to purchase the contract. A burial contract will be considered\nas being owned by the claimant if his/her money was used to purchase the contract,\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-27" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00114 -->\n\nregardless of who is indicated as "purchaser" or "owner". Ownership by someone\nother than the claimant or spouse must be verified and fully documented in the record\nof contact. The establishment of the contract\'s ownership should begin at the initial\ninterview, with the question as to whose funds were used to purchase the contract. If\nthe sponsor or anyone other than the claimant/spouse claims to have purchased the\ncontract with their money, for the purpose of meeting the claimant\'s/spouse\'s burial\nneeds, then verification should be requested. In the absence of verification, the\nclaimant will be considered the owner and the burial fund exclusion applied\naccordingly.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

UKRAINIAN HUMANITARIAN PAROLEES\n\nUkrainians and other non-Ukrainian individuals paroled into the United States\nbetween February 24, 2022 and September 30, 2023 are eligible to receive\nmainstream benefits (e.g., TANF, Medicaid, SNAP, SSI), resettlement assistance, and\nother benefits available to refugees. A spouse, child, parent, legal guardian, or\nprimary caregiver of any Ukrainian Humanitarian Parolee or non-Ukrainian individual\nwho is paroled into the United States after September 30, 2023 is entitled to the same\nbenefits and assistance.\n\n\n## F.8.a. DETERMINING MEDICAID ELIGIBILITYG\n\nApplication for Medicaid coverage should be process using the usual procedures.\nThere is no requirement to expedite the processing of applications for Ukrainian\nHumanitarian Parolees. When processing applications for these immigrants, please\nnote the following:\n\n· Ukrainian parolees/refugees must meet financial and all other non-financial\neligibility requirements for an eligibility category in order to qualify for\nMedicaid.\n\n· Ukrainian parolees/refugees are not subject to the 5-year waiting period\n\n\n## F.8.b. VERIFYING UKRAINIAN HUMANITARIAN PAROLEES\' STATUS\n\nUkrainians and non-Ukrainians who claim to be parolees or refugees must provide\nverification of admittance of parolee/refugee status. Documents issued by the\nDepartment of Homeland Security (DHS) verify both parolee/refugee status and date\nof entry.\n\n\n## F.8.c. PERIOD OF ELIGIBILITY\n\n\n<table>\n<tr>\n<th>Category of Applicant</th>\n<th>Period of Eligibility</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ukrainian Humanitarian Parolee and other non-Ukrainian individuals</td>\n<td>Initial date of eligibility is May 21, 2022 Or The individual\'s date of humanitarian parole, whichever is later.</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ukrainian and other non-Ukrainian individuals paroled and entered the U.S between February 24, 2022 and May 21, 2022</td>\n<td>Date of eligibility is May 21, 2022</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ukrainian and other non-Ukrainian individuals entered the United States after May 21, 2022</td>\n<td>Date of eligibility is their date of humanitarian parole.</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-20" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00054 -->\n\n\n## F.8.d. DOCUMENTATION OF UKRAINIAN PAROLEE STATUS AND DATE OF ENTRY\n\n\n<table>\n<tr>\n<th>Immigration Status or Category of Applicant</th>\n<th>Acceptable Documentation</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Ukrainian citizen or national who received humanitarian parole (known as a Ukrainian Humanitarian Parolee, or UHP</td>\n<td>Form I-94 noting humanitarian parole (per INA section 212(d)(5) or 8 U.S.C. § 1182(d)(5)) Or Foreign passport with DHS/CBP admission stamp noting "DT" Or Foreign passport with DHS/CBP admission stamp noting Uniting for Ukraine or "U4U" Or Foreign passport with DHS/CBP admission stamp noting Ukrainian Humanitarian Parolee or "UHP" Or Form I-765 Employment Authorization Document (EAD) receipt notice with code C11 Or Form I-766 Employment Authorization Document (EAD) with the code C11</td>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>A non-Ukrainian individual who last habitually resided in Ukraine and received humanitarian parole</td>\n<td>Any one of the forms or stamps listed above resided in Ukraine and received humanitarian for UHPs And Documentation of last habitual residence in Ukraine</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\n### F.9. ELIGIBILITY OF NON- QUALIFIED ALIENS\n\nAliens who do not meet the qualified alien requirements are Non-Qualified Aliens. If\notherwise eligible, a non-qualified alien is eligible for Medicaid only for treatment of\nemergency medical conditions. Non-qualified aliens do not include legal non-\nimmigrants. Treatment of emergency medical condition is defined and explained in\nF.9. These aliens do not have to make a declaration of immigration status. It is not\nnecessary to verify the immigration status of non-qualified aliens. However, such\naliens do have to provide their SSN(s) if one is available or apply for an SSN if the\napplicant does not have one.\n\nNon-Qualified aliens also include illegal aliens. These aliens either were never legally\nadmitted to the United States for any period of time or were admitted for a limited\nperiod of time and did not leave the Unites States when the period of time expired.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-21" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00055 -->\n\nThese individuals, if they are otherwise eligible for Medicaid, may be eligible for\nMedicaid for treatment of an emergency medical condition.
