PROCEDURE TO DESIGNATE FUNDS\n\nThe exclusion is applied only when the assets have been designated as having been set\naside for burial; e.g., by the title of an account or by a signed statement over the\npenalty clause as to the purpose of the asset (declarative designation). A "Statement\nof Claimant or Other Person" designation form may be used to exclude the current CSV\nvalue of funds for burial retroactively provided there is no evidence that the funds\nwere used and replaced after that date. The "Statement of Claimant or Other Person"\nform must show:\n\n\\- the current value and owner of the resources;\n\n\\- for whose burial the resources are set aside;\n\n\\- the form(s) in which the resources are held (burial contract, bank account,\netc.); These may also include accounts with commingled funds where some of\nthe funds held are designated for burial. For example, an applicant, with a\nsavings account, may choose to designate half of the funds in the account for\nburial and;\n\n\\- the date the claimant or spouse first considered the funds to be set aside for\nthe burial of the person specified.\n\nOnce the date the burial funds are considered set aside for burial has been\nestablished, the first month for which the exclusion affects the first of the month\nresources determination is the latest of:\n\n\\- the month following the month in which the funds were considered to have\nbeen set aside; or\n\n\\- the actual or effective month of application, if the funds were considered set\naside before that month. DEVELOPMENT OF THE EXCLUSION\n\nIf the claimant alleges owning a burial fund, develop the possibility of the burial fund\nexclusion by gathering the following information: life insurance, burial insurance,\nvault insurance, burial contracts, and any other assets which the claimant alleges are\nto be used for burial expenses. If the face value exceeds\n$1,500/$5,000, the cash surrender value of the policies may be set aside as all\nor a part of the burial fund;\n\n\\- The total value of any irrevocable burial arrangement on the claimant (or\nspouse, if applicable) (except to the extent that it includes burial spaces which\nare excludable, if the burial agreement is not paid for, you should deduct the\nprice of burial items up to the amount paid on the contract.).\n\nThe remainder is the net burial fund exclusion allowed for Medicaid purposes.\n\nDetermine total countable toward the maximum burial fund exclusion.\n\n\\- Determine total value of revocable burial arrangements (owned by the\nclaimant) less any burial space exclusions and any insurance which is a part of\nthe contract.\n\n\\- The remainder is the net countable amount of the prepaid burial contract.\n\n\\- Add CSV of countable life insurance (FV exceeds $1,500/$5,000) if that life\ninsurance policy is designated for burial.\n\nNOTE: When designating life insurance as a burial fund, the claimant can also\ndesignate any dividend accumulations on the life insurance policy as a burial fund.\nDividend accumulations are a separate resource (i.e., not to be considered as an\nincrease in the value of the CSV) and must be designated as burial funds separate from\nthe life insurance policy itself.\n\n\\- Add other funds designated for burial.\n\n\\- The total is the total amount designated for burial.\n\nCompare the amount designated for burial with the net allowable burial fund\nexclusion. If the amount designated for burial is less than the amount allowed for\nburial exclusion, additional designation is permitted not to exceed the allowable limit.\n\nAny burial funds in excess of the exclusion limit applicable to the claimant (or spouse)\nare countable resources. If excess burial, due to the cash surrender value of insurance\npolicies, causes the claimant to be ineligible, and the claimant sends a letter\nrequesting termination of the policy or a loan against the policy, the cash value of the\ninsurance will be excluded. This exclusion applies as of the date he or she sends a\nletter requesting the termination of the policy or a loan against the CSV of the policy\nuntil the actual receipt of the cash value of the policy or the money from the loan. A\ncopy of the letter or a sworn statement (only used if cannot provide a copy of the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-29" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00116 -->\n\nletter) is sufficient proof that the claimant has taken action to retrieve the cash value\nof the policy.\n\n\n## M.6.g. INCREASES IN VALUE OF BURIAL FUNDS\n\nMedicaid will exclude from resources up to $1500/$5000 when it is designated for\nburial. Once the amount of designated burial funds equals $1,500/$5,000, the only\nadditions to that amount that can be excluded, under the burial funds provision, is\nappreciation and/or interest.\n\nAny appreciation in the value of excluded burial funds is excluded from resources (and\nfrom income) even if the total of the burial funds thus excluded exceeds\n$1,500/$5,000. This includes interest earned by burial funds provided the interest is\nleft to accumulate as part of the funds.\n\n\n## M.6.h. DEVELOPMENT OF INTEREST EARNED ON BURIAL FUNDS\n\nIf the amount designated is the $1500/$5000 limit, then that amount will be excluded.\nAny excess/overage of the designated amount will count. When only a portion of the\nburial fund is excluded, use the following procedure to determine countable interest\nto be charged:\n\nStep 1\nDetermine total interest paid on the commingled account.\n\nStep 2\nDetermine the ratio of the non-excluded portion of the fund to the\nexcluded portion by dividing the value of the non-excluded portion of the\nfund by the total value of the fund. Carry the quotient to 3 decimal\nplaces.\n\nStep 3\nMultiply the decimal obtained in step 2 representing the non-excluded\nportion by the total amount of interest earned on the fund. The result is\nthe amount of interest paid on the non-excluded portion of the fund for\nthe period in question.\n\nNOTE: The same ratio (in step 2) may be used every month as long as there are no\ndeposits or withdrawals from the total fund.\n\n\n## M.6.i. INTEREST AND APPRECIATION\n\nIn order for the exclusion to apply, any excluded burial fund(s) must be identifiable\nfrom other resources. Interest retained and appreciations in value of the excluded\nfunds are also excluded and must be identifiable.\n\n\n### M.6.j. MISUSE OF BURIAL FUNDS\n\nIf excluded burial funds or excluded interest or appreciation on excluded burial funds\nis used for a purpose other than for burial expenses, future Medicaid benefits could be\naffected. Count the misused funds as unearned income in the month following the\nmonth these burial funds have been determined to have been misused. This policy is\nonly applicable when counting the funds would cause the claimant to have excess\nresources.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-30" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00117 -->\n\n\n#### M.6.k. BURIAL SPACES AND PLOTS', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

ASSIGNMENT OF THIRD-PARTY PAYMENTS\n\nThird party benefits are any benefits for which an entity is or may be liable to pay all\nor part of the medical costs of a claimant of Medicaid. To be eligible for Medicaid, an\napplicant/recipient must assign all third-party benefits to the state.\n\nAn applicant/recipient must cooperate in identifying third party coverage as well as\nprovide information and assist the Medicaid Agency in pursuing those benefits. an applicant/recipient has a valid reason for not participating in\na program requirement.\n\nExamples of good cause are:\n\n\\- if the applicant states that it is against the child\'s best interest or\n\n\\- there is a threat of physical or mental harm if they cooperate with identifying third\nparty coverage or obtaining payments for medical care.\n\nIf an applicant thinks they are in danger of 2026-04-02T20:26:35.084218097Z [2026-04-02 20:26:35,083] [DEBUG] openai._base_client: Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/deployments/text-embedding/embeddings', 'headers': {'Authorization': '<redacted>'}, 'files': None, 'post_parser': <function Embeddings.create.<locals>.parser at 0x7da1e3741e40>, 'json_data': {'input': '<!-- PageNumber="VI-30" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00117 -->\n\n\n#### M.6.k. BURIAL SPACES AND PLOTS\n\nBurial spaces and plots are excluded as countable resources. There is no limit on the\nvalue of any burial space or plot (conventional grave site, crypt, mausoleum, vault,\ncasket, urn, or other repository held for the purposes of providing a place for the\nburial of the claimant, his/her spouse, or any other member of his/her immediate\nfamily). The opening and closing of the grave as well as headstones are considered\nburial space items. If the burial agreement includes both burial spaces and funeral\nservices purchases, the value of the burial space can only be excluded under the burial\nspace exclusion if the item and its value are specifically identified in the agreement.\nIf not, then the entire agreement is considered to be funds set aside for burial which\nmay result in excess burial funds. If the burial agreement has not been fully paid for,\nburial items may be excluded up to the amount paid on the contract.\n\n\n##### M.6.l. DEVELOPMENT OF THE BURIAL SPACE EXCLUSION\n\nThe burial space exclusion is in addition to, and has no effect on, the burial funds\nexclusion discussed elsewhere in this manual.\n\nA burial space or agreement which represents the purchase of a burial space held for\nthe burial of the claimant, his or her spouse, or any other member of his or her\nimmediate family, is an excluded resource, regardless of value.\n\nThe claimant\'s immediate family includes his or her parents, including adoptive\nparents; minor or adult children, including adoptive or stepchildren; siblings (brothers\nand sisters), including adoptive and step siblings; spouse of the above relatives. If the\nrelative\'s relationship to the claimant is by marriage only, the marriage must be in\neffect in order for the burial space exclusion to continue to apply. For example, a\nburial space held for a sister-in-law is no longer excludable if she and the claimant\'s\nbrother divorce.\n\nA burial space is:\n\n\\- burial plot;\n\n\\- grave site;\n\n\\- crypt;\n\n\\- mausoleum;\n\n\\- casket;\n\n\\- urn (cremation cost is not excluded);\n\n\\- niche; or\n\n\\- other repository customarily and traditionally used for the deceased\'s bodily\nremains.\n\nThe term also includes necessary and reasonable improvements or additions to such\nspaces, including but not limited to:\n\n\\- vaults;\n\n\\- headstones, markers or plaques;\n\n\\- burial containers (e.g., for caskets); and\n\n\\- arrangements for the opening and closing of the gravesite;\n\n\\- a contract for care and maintenance of the gravesite\n\n\\- (endowment for perpetual care), can be excluded as a burial space.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-31" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00118 -->\n\n\n# M.6.m. LIFE INSURANCE FUNDED BURIAL CONTRACTS M.6.m. 1. DEFINITION\n\nA life insurance funded burial contract involves a claimant purchasing a life insurance\npolicy on his/her life and then, assigning, revocable or irrevocably, either the\nproceeds or ownership of the policy to a third party, generally a funeral provider. The\npurpose of the assignment is to fund a burial contract. Life insurance funded burial\ncontracts are not burial insurance.\n\n\n# M.6.m.2. FUNERAL HOME AS A BENEFICIARY\n\nIf the ownership of a life insurance policy has not been changed or assigned to a\nfuneral home, and the funeral home is the beneficiary, it is a life insurance policy.\nThe face value and cash value are countable as any other life insurance policy. No\nburial items/spaces may be deducted, since none have been purchased. The burial\nitem/space exclusion does not apply. This is because the funeral provider has not\nreceived any payment, and there has been no purchase of burial items/spaces made.\nThe provider has no obligation to provide any burial items/spaces until the claimant\ndies, and therefore no items/spaces are being held for the claimant.\n\n\n# M.6.m.3. CHANGE OR ASSIGNMENT OF OWNERSHIP TO A FUNERAL HOME\n\nIf the ownership has been changed or assigned to a funeral home or a trust, a transfer\nhas occurred. Complete the burial worksheet and deduct the burial space items as\nnormally done in prepaid burial cases. There is no uncompensated value for burial\nspace items. If the amount is in excess of the burial fund limit, count the excess\namount as a countable resource. If the burial fund has been changed or assigned to an\nirrevocable trust no transfer penalty should be imposed.\n\n\n# M.6.m.4. OWNERSHIP TRANSFERRED TO A TRUST\n\nIf ownership has been assigned to a trust determine if the trust is revocable or\nirrevocable. If the trust is revocable the funds in the trust are a countable resource.\nThese funds can be designated as a burial fund up to the maximum excludable\namount. Complete the burial worksheet and deduct the burial space items as\nnormally done in prepaid burial cases. The amount in excess of the burial fund limit\nshould be reflected in the budget as excess burial. If the trust is irrevocable these\nfunds are not a countable resource. Complete the burial worksheet and deduct the\nburial space items as normally done in prepaid burial cases. No transfer penalty\nshould be imposed on the amount in excess of the burial fund limit.\n\n\n# M.6.m.5. DOCUMENTS NEEDED FOR PREPAID FUNERAL ARRANGEMENT\n\nWhen a prepaid funeral arrangement is funded by a life insurance policy, the following\ndocuments should be obtained:\n\n\\- A copy of the life insurance policy,\n\n\\- A copy of the change of ownership or assignment of the policy,\n\n\\- A copy of the prepaid funeral contract, and\n\n\\- A copy of the trust, if the policy was transferred to a trust.\n\nDocumentation: Document the file with a copy of:\n\n\\- the life insurance policy;\n\n\\- the assignment; and\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-32" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00119 -->\n\n\\- any other related documents.\n\n\\- Burial contract. Examine the burial contract and determine what items and/or\narrangements have been contracted. Document the file with a copy of the\nburial contract.\n\n\n## M.7. PERSONAL PROPERTY\n\nPersonal property is any property that is not real property. The term encompasses\nsuch things as cash, jewelry, household goods, tools, life insurance policies,\nautomobiles, and mobile homes.\n\n\n## M.7.a. HOUSEHOLD GOODS AND PERSONAL EFFECTS\n\nHousehold goods and personal effects are excluded as a resource.\n\n\n### M.7.a.1. HOUSEHOLD GOODS\n\nHousehold goods are items of personal property found in or near the home that are\nused on a regular basis and items needed by the householder for maintenance, use,\nand occupancy of the premises of the home. Household goods include, but are not\nlimited to, furniture, appliances, electronic equipment such as a personal computers\nand television, carpets, cooking and eating utensils and dishes. Items that are\nacquired or held because of their value or as an investment (such as coin, stamp, and\nantique collections) are not considered household goods. (See M.7.d for information\nregarding other personal property.)\n\n\n### M.7.a.2. PERSONAL EFFECTS\n\nPersonal effects are items of personal property ordinarily worn or carried by the\nindividual and articles otherwise having an intimate relation to the individual.\nPersonal effects include, but are not limited to, personal jewelry including wedding\nand engagement rings, personal care items, and educational or recreational items such\nas books or musical instruments. Items of cultural or religious significance to an\nindividual, such as ceremonial attire and items of an individual\'s physical or mental\nimpairment such as prosthetic devices or wheelchairs are personal effects. Items that\nare acquired or held because of their value or as an investment (such as coin, stamp,\nand antique collections) are not considered personal effects. (See M.7.d for\ninformation regarding other personal property.)\n\n\n### M.7.b. MOTOR VEHICLES\n\nMotor vehicles include automobiles as well as other vehicles used to provide necessary\ntransportation for employment, medical treatments, are specially equipped for the\nhandicapped or to perform essential daily activities. A vehicle that does not meet the\ndefinition of an automobile is a countable resource using its equity value.\n\n\n### M.7.b.1. TREATMENT OF AUTOMOBILES FOR MEDICAID CERTIFIED PROGRAMS OTHER THAN MEDICARE SAVINGS PROGRAMS\n\nOne motor vehicle is totally excluded regardless of its value for the claimant or a\nmember of the claimant\'s household.\n\n\n# M.7.b.2. OTHER AUTOMOBILES\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-33" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00120 -->', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

The current market value of a vehicle is\nthe average price for which a vehicle of that particular year, make, model, and\ncondition will sell on the open market.\n\nTo determine the current market value of a vehicle:\n\n\\- Refer to the National Automobile Dealers Association, N.A.D.A., Official Used\nCar Guide or Red Book;\n\n\\- Obtain estimates of the current market value from a knowledgeable source\nsuch as a car dealer or used car dealer; or\n\n\\- Use the Kelly Blue Book\'s trade-in value (http: //www.kbb.com/)\n\n\n# M.7.c. MOBILE HOMES\n\nA mobile home is an item of personal property that is counted against the resource\nlimit, when it cannot be excluded. When it is verified that a claimant owns an\ninterest in a mobile home and it is situated on property in which the claimant has no\nownership interest (a rented lot, a space on land owned by a relative, etc.), the\nmobile home is considered personal property. Any encumbrances owed on\nthe mobile home should be verified and deducted from the current market value of\nthe mobile home prior to establishing the equity value of this resource. It is not\nnecessary to complete a lien agreement in this situation.\n\nIf the mobile home is excluded under the bona fide effort to sell exclusion, the Form\n226 (Agreement to Sell) must be signed so the Agency can recover the money from the\nsale of the mobile home when it is sold.\n\nNOTE: The cash received for the conversion of the excluded resource is treated as any\nother countable resource in the following month and spend down must be verified.\nRefer to Chapter VI.F.2.\n\nNOTE: Refer to Chapter VI.P.5 for information regarding when a lien is required on\nmobile homes.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-34" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00121 -->\n\n\n# M.7.d. RETIREMENT FUNDS\n\nRetirement funds are annuities or work-related plans for providing income when\nemployment ends or funds held in Individual Retirement Accounts (IRA\'s).\n\nNOTE: Section N.20 of this chapter specifies when an IRA is considered a countable\nresource.\n\n\n## M.8.a. GENERAL POLICY PRINCIPLES\n\nIf a claimant owns a retirement fund and is eligible for periodic retirement benefits,\nthe claimant must apply for those benefits. If a claimant owns a retirement fund and\nis not eligible for periodic payments, but has the option of withdrawing the funds, the\nretirement fund is counted as a resource.\n\n\n## M.8.b. OPERATING GUIDELINES\n\nIf a claimant must terminate employment in order to file for a retirement fund, the\nfund is not a resource. If the retirement fund is available for withdrawal even if the\nwithdrawal is delayed for reasons beyond the claimant\'s control, the value of the funds\ncounts as a resource from the month the funds became available for withdrawal. If a\nclaim for periodic payment is denied and the claimant can withdraw the funds from\nhis/her retirement fund, the value of the retirement fund is counted as a resource\neffective the first moment of the month following the month of the denial notice.\n\nNOTE: An ineligible spouse\'s retirement funds are not deemed to the eligible\nclaimant.\n\n\n# M.9. ADULT PROTECTIVE SERVICES\n\nThe Adult Protective Services includes both Adults in Need of Protection Services\nProgram and Elderly and Disabled Adult Victim Services (EDAVS) Program. Both\nprograms are managed by the Alabama Department of Human Resources (ADHR). ADHR\nprotect elderly and disabled adults from abuse, neglect, and exploitation and prevent\nunnecessary institutionalization.\n\nAn "adult in need of protection services" or "elderly and disabled adult victim services"\nis defined as a person 18 years of age or older whose behavior indicates that he or she\nis mentally incapable of adequately caring for himself or herself and his or her\ninterests without serious consequences to himself or herself or others, or who,\nbecause of physical or mental impairment, is unable to protect himself or herself from\nabuse, neglect, exploitation, sexual abuse, or emotional abuse by others, and who has\nno guardian, relative, or other appropriate person able, willing, and available to\nassume the kind and degree of protection and supervision required under the\ncircumstances.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-35" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00122 -->\n\nResources of claimants defined as "adults in need of protection" under the Alabama\nAdult Protective Services Law will not be counted for the purposes of Medicaid\neligibility during the period beginning the date a petition to protect the person is filed\nin the circuit court, until the appointment of a person with legal authority to\nrepresent the claimant. (Alabama Medicaid does not award partial month eligibility.\nTherefore, these individuals will be eligible on the first day of the month in which the\npetition is filed if all other points of eligibility are met.). This period shall not exceed\n120 days for Medicaid eligibility. A copy of the petition is required in the record.\n\nNOTE: Elderly and Disabled Adult Victim Services (EDAVS) must be treated the same\nas Adults in Need of Protection Services.\n\n\n# M. 10. LUMP SUM SSI/SSA PAYMENTS\n\nThe unspent portion of lump sum SSI payments will be excluded from resources for\nnine (9) months following the month of receipt. The unspent portion of lump sum SSA\npayments will be excluded from resources for nine (9) months following the month of\nreceipt. Verification of the amount of the payment and the date it was received must\nbe in the case file.\n\nNOTE: SSA lump sum payments are income in the month received. SSI lump sum\npayments are not income in the month received.\n\n\n# M. 11. SOCIAL SECURITY BENEFITS (SS) DEBIT CARD\n\nThe Social Security Administration issues benefits using a debit card. Benefits retained\non a debit card that is not connected to an account will not count as a resource.\nHowever, benefits retained on a debit card connected to an account will count as a\nresource as of the first day of the following month.\n\n\n# M. 12. CASH AND IN-KIND ITEMS RECEIVED FOR THE REPAIR OR REPLACEMENT OF LOST, DAMAGED, OR STOLEN EXCLUDED RESOURCES\n\nCash and in-kind receipts from any source for the replacement or repair of lost,\ndamaged, or stolen excluded resources are themselves excluded resources for 9\nmonths from the date of their receipt. For cash receipts, the exclusion can be\nextended for up to an additional 9 month\'s if, for the first 9 months, circumstances\nbeyond the claimant\'s control:\n\n\\- prevent repair or replacement of the lost, damaged, or stolen property;\n\n\\- keep the claimant from contracting for such repair or replacement; and\n\n\\- the claimant intends to use the funds for their designated purpose.\n\nNOTE: Interest earned by funds excluded under this provision is excluded from\nincome and resources for the period during which the funds themselves are\nexcluded from resources.\n\n\n# M. 13. INSTITUTIONALIZED 21 OR OLDER INCAPABLE OF INDICATING INTENT\n\nFor any institutionalized claimant who became incapable of indicating intent at or\nafter age 18, the state of residence is the state in which the claimant is physically\npresent, except where another state makes a placement.\n\n\n## G.6. OTHERS\n\nFor any other institutionalized claimant, the state of residence is the state where the\nclaimant is living with the intention to remain there.\n\n\n## G.7. INTERSTATE RESIDENCY AGREEMENTS\n\nThe only time the above residence requirements would not be in effect would be\nwhen the state entered into a residency agreement with another state. When this has\noccurred, the state where the claimant physically resides is his residence for Medicaid\npurposes.\n\nStates with which Alabama has entered into agreement are:\n\n\n<table>\n<tr>\n<th>California</th>\n<th>Georgia</th>\n<th>Iowa</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Pennsylvania</td>\n<td>Texas</td>\n<td>Wisconsin</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\nWhere two or more states cannot resolve which state is the state of residence, the\nstate where the claimant is physically located is the state of residence.\n\n\n## G.8. APPLICATION\n\nWhen it is determined the claimant is a resident of another state, for the period for\nwhich he/she is applying for assistance, the application will be denied unless there is\nan interstate residency agreement. The notice of denial should:\n\n(1) contain an explanation to the guardian or responsible relative as to the\nactual state of residence and how this was determined, and\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-26" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00060 -->\n\n(2) advise the family to apply for Medicaid in the other state.\n\n\n## III.H. MEDICAL APPROVAL\n\nThe nursing facility claimant must be certified financially and as needing the level of\ncare received. Medicaid district offices determine and certify claimants for financial\neligibility. Alabama Medicaid Agency will not authorize payment when it has been\ndetermined that the claimant does not meet the medical need requirements for the\nlevel of care required.\n\n\n## III.I. ELIGIBILITY FOR OTHER PROGRAM BENEFITS\n\nAs a condition of eligibility, a claimant is required to apply for any payments or\nbenefits from other sources for which he/she may be eligible. A claimant ceases to\nbe eligible if he fails to take appropriate steps to apply for and, if eligible, to obtain\nsuch benefits. See Income, Chapter VII, A. 3rd paragraph, for details.\n\n\n## III.J. ASSIGNMENT OF THIRD-PARTY PAYMENTS\n\nThird party benefits are any benefits for which an entity is or may be liable to pay all\nor part of the medical costs of a claimant of Medicaid. To be eligible for Medicaid, an\napplicant/recipient must assign all third-party benefits to the state.\n\nAn applicant/recipient must cooperate in identifying third party coverage as well as\nprovide information and assist the Medicaid Agency in pursuing those benefits. If an\napplicant/recipient refuse to provide information, he/she must be advised by the\nworker of his/her rights to request a good cause exemption to cooperate with\nMedicaid.\n\nGood cause is where an applicant/recipient has a valid reason for not participating in\na program requirement.\n\nExamples of good cause are:\n\n\\- if the applicant states that it is against the child\'s best interest or\n\n\\- there is a threat of physical or mental harm if they cooperate with identifying third\nparty coverage or obtaining payments for medical care.\n\nIf an applicant thinks they are in danger of bodily harm or emotional damage, then\nevidence must be submitted to substantiate this claim.\n\nWhen a request for a good cause exemption is made due to circumstances that may\nbe against the child\'s best interest or physical or emotional harm, the factors listed\nbelow should be considered.\n\n\\- Cooperation is reasonably anticipated to result in physical or emotional harm\nof a serious nature to the child for whom support is sought or to the adult with\nwhom the child is living, to the point of impairing the adult capacity to care\nfor the child adequately, or\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-27" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00061 -->\n\n\\- At least one of the following circumstances exists and proceeding with Third\nParty assignment activities would, in a particular case, be detrimental to the\nindividual for whom Third Party assignment is sought.\n\n\\- The child was conceived because of incest or forcible rape.\n\n\\- Legal proceedings for adoption of the child are pending before a court of\ncompetent jurisdiction.\n\n\\- The applicant/recipient is currently being assisted by a public or licensed\nprivate social agency to resolve the issue of whether to keep the child or\nrelinquish for adoption, and the discussions have not gone for more than three\nmonths.\n\nExemptions for claim of emotional harm factors:\n\n\\- The present emotional state of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- The emotional health history of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- Intensity and probable duration of the emotional impairment;\n\n\\- The degree of cooperation to be required; an\n\n\\- The extent of involvement of the child in the paternity establishment or\nsupport enforcement activity to be undertaken.\n\nExemptions for claim of physical harm factors:\n\n\\- Based on either actual or anticipated physical abuse to the client and/or child which\nwould result if cooperation were required.\n\n\\- An investigation will be conducted only when the applicant/recipient states there is\nno documentary evidence to substantiate the good cause claim, such as in the case of\n"battered wives" who have not told anyone about physical harm to which they have\nbeen subjected, or when the application is not credible without supportive evidence.\n\nThe following is a list of documentary evidence that is necessary to support the good\ncause claim:\n\n\\- A birth certificate, medical or law enforcement documentation.\n\n\\- Court documents or other court recordings.\n\n\\- Written statement from a government service unit or licensed private agency.\n\n\\- Medical documentation that indicate the current/past emotional or physical health\nhistory of the adult or the child for whom support would be sought. Or written\nstatements from a mental health professional indicating a diagnosis or prognosis\nconcerning the emotional health of the adult or the child for whom the Third-Party\nassignment would be sought.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-28" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00062 -->\n\n\\- Court, medical, criminal, social services, psychological, law enforcement records or\nservice case records in the Department of Human Resources.\n\n\\- Sworn statement(s) from individual(s), other than the applicant/recipient, with\npersonal knowledge of the circumstance(s) which provide the basis for the good cause\nclaim. The individual(s) must state their personal knowledge of the situation and how\nthey were involved. The Statement of Claimant, Form 234, should be used for this\npurpose.\n\nNOTE: If the applicant/recipient statement of good cause claim along with the\nsupportive evidence do not provide sufficient basis for making a determination, an\ninvestigation may be requested to determine that a good cause does or does not\nexist.\n\nNOTE: If the client expresses and substantiates a reason for not cooperating with the\nidentification and pursuit of other health insurance benefits, Third Party Division\nshould be contacted for a determination of good cause that can be notated on the\nMMIS\' TPL resource file.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

DETERMINING MARKET VALUE\n\nOther automobiles are treated as resources and counted against the resource limit to\nthe extent of the claimant\'s equity interest. The current market value of a vehicle is\nthe average price for which a vehicle of that particular year, make, model, and\ncondition will sell on the open market.\n\nTo determine the current market value of a vehicle:\n\n\\- Refer to the National Automobile Dealers Association, N.A.D.A., Official Used\nCar Guide or Red Book;\n\n\\- Obtain estimates of the current market value from a knowledgeable source\nsuch as a car dealer or used car dealer; or\n\n\\- Use the Kelly Blue Book\'s trade-in value (http: //www.kbb.com/)\n\n\n# M.7.c. MOBILE HOMES\n\nA mobile home is an item of personal property that is counted against the resource\nlimit, when it cannot be excluded. GENERAL POLICY PRINCIPLES\n\nIf a claimant owns a retirement fund and is eligible for periodic retirement benefits,\nthe claimant must apply for those benefits. OPERATING GUIDELINES\n\nIf a claimant must terminate employment in order to file for a retirement fund, the\nfund is not a resource. INTERSTATE RESIDENCY AGREEMENTS\n\nThe only time the above residence requirements would not be in effect would be\nwhen the state entered into a residency agreement with another state. When this has\noccurred, the state where the claimant physically resides is his residence for Medicaid\npurposes.\n\nStates with which Alabama has entered into agreement are:\n\n\n<table>\n<tr>\n<th>California</th>\n<th>Georgia</th>\n<th>Iowa</th>\n</tr>\n<tr>\n<td>Pennsylvania</td>\n<td>Texas</td>\n<td>Wisconsin</td>\n</tr>\n</table>\n\n\nWhere two or more states cannot resolve which state is the state of residence, the\nstate where the claimant is physically located is the state of residence.\n\n\n## G.8. APPLICATION\n\nWhen it is determined the claimant is a resident of another state, for the period for\nwhich he/she is applying for assistance, the application will be denied unless there is\nan interstate residency agreement. The notice of denial should:\n\n(1) contain an explanation to the guardian or responsible relative as to the\nactual state of residence and how this was determined, and\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-26" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00060 -->\n\n(2) advise the family to apply for Medicaid in the other state.\n\n\n## III.H. MEDICAL APPROVAL\n\nThe nursing facility claimant must be certified financially and as needing the level of\ncare received. Medicaid district offices determine and certify claimants for financial\neligibility. Alabama Medicaid Agency will not authorize payment when it has been\ndetermined that the claimant does not meet the medical need requirements for the\nlevel of care required.\n\n\n## III.I. ELIGIBILITY FOR OTHER PROGRAM BENEFITS\n\nAs a condition of eligibility, a claimant is required to apply for any payments or\nbenefits from other sources for which he/she may be eligible. A claimant ceases to\nbe eligible if he fails to take appropriate steps to apply for and, if eligible, to obtain\nsuch benefits. See Income, Chapter VII, A. 3rd paragraph, for details.\n\n\n## III.J. ASSIGNMENT OF THIRD-PARTY PAYMENTS\n\nThird party benefits are any benefits for which an entity is or may be liable to pay all\nor part of the medical costs of a claimant of Medicaid. To be eligible for Medicaid, an\napplicant/recipient must assign all third-party benefits to the state.\n\nAn applicant/recipient must cooperate in identifying third party coverage as well as\nprovide information and assist the Medicaid Agency in pursuing those benefits. If an\napplicant/recipient refuse to provide information, he/she must be advised by the\nworker of his/her rights to request a good cause exemption to cooperate with\nMedicaid.\n\nGood cause is where an applicant/recipient has a valid reason for not participating in\na program requirement.\n\nExamples of good cause are:\n\n\\- if the applicant states that it is against the child\'s best interest or\n\n\\- there is a threat of physical or mental harm if they cooperate with identifying third\nparty coverage or obtaining payments for medical care.\n\nIf an applicant thinks they are in danger of bodily harm or emotional damage, then\nevidence must be submitted to substantiate this claim.\n\nWhen a request for a good cause exemption is made due to circumstances that may\nbe against the child\'s best interest or physical or emotional harm, the factors listed\nbelow should be considered.\n\n\\- Cooperation is reasonably anticipated to result in physical or emotional harm\nof a serious nature to the child for whom support is sought or to the adult with\nwhom the child is living, to the point of impairing the adult capacity to care\nfor the child adequately, or\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-27" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00061 -->\n\n\\- At least one of the following circumstances exists and proceeding with Third\nParty assignment activities would, in a particular case, be detrimental to the\nindividual for whom Third Party assignment is sought.\n\n\\- The child was conceived because of incest or forcible rape.\n\n\\- Legal proceedings for adoption of the child are pending before a court of\ncompetent jurisdiction.\n\n\\- The applicant/recipient is currently being assisted by a public or licensed\nprivate social agency to resolve the issue of whether to keep the child or\nrelinquish for adoption, and the discussions have not gone for more than three\nmonths.\n\nExemptions for claim of emotional harm factors:\n\n\\- The present emotional state of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- The emotional health history of the individual subject to emotional harm;\n\n\\- Intensity and probable duration of the emotional impairment;\n\n\\- The degree of cooperation to be required; an\n\n\\- The extent of involvement of the child in the paternity establishment or\nsupport enforcement activity to be undertaken.\n\nExemptions for claim of physical harm factors:\n\n\\- Based on either actual or anticipated physical abuse to the client and/or child which\nwould result if cooperation were required.\n\n\\- An investigation will be conducted only when the applicant/recipient states there is\nno documentary evidence to substantiate the good cause claim, such as in the case of\n"battered wives" who have not told anyone about physical harm to which they have\nbeen subjected, or when the application is not credible without supportive evidence.\n\nThe following is a list of documentary evidence that is necessary to support the good\ncause claim:\n\n\\- A birth certificate, medical or law enforcement documentation.\n\n\\- Court documents or other court recordings.\n\n\\- Written statement from a government service unit or licensed private agency.\n\n\\- Medical documentation that indicate the current/past emotional or physical health\nhistory of the adult or the child for whom support would be sought. Or written\nstatements from a mental health professional indicating a diagnosis or prognosis\nconcerning the emotional health of the adult or the child for whom the Third-Party\nassignment would be sought.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-28" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00062 -->\n\n\\- Court, medical, criminal, social services, psychological, law enforcement records or\nservice case records in the Department of Human Resources.\n\n\\- Sworn statement(s) from individual(s), other than the applicant/recipient, with\npersonal knowledge of the circumstance(s) which provide the basis for the good cause\nclaim. The individual(s) must state their personal knowledge of the situation and how\nthey were involved. The Statement of Claimant, Form 234, should be used for this\npurpose.\n\nNOTE: If the applicant/recipient statement of good cause claim along with the\nsupportive evidence do not provide sufficient basis for making a determination, an\ninvestigation may be requested to determine that a good cause does or does not\nexist.\n\nNOTE: If the client expresses and substantiates a reason for not cooperating with the\nidentification and pursuit of other health insurance benefits, Third Party Division\nshould be contacted for a determination of good cause that can be notated on the\nMMIS\' TPL resource file.', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

VICTIMS\' COMPENSATION PAYMENTS\n\nAny payments received from a fund established by a state to aid victims of crime are\nexcluded from income. Interest on unspent victims\' compensation payments is not\nexcluded from income or resources.\n\nThe payments can also be excluded from resources for the number of months that\nremain in the fund for payment following the month of receipt.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-37" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00124 -->\n\n\n# M.18. TAX ADVANCES AND REFUNDS RELATED TO EARNED INCOME\n\nAn unspent federal tax refund or payment made by an employer related to Earned\nIncome Tax Credits (EITCs) is excluded 9 months following the month the refund or\npayment is received. Interest earned on unspent tax refunds related to EITCs is not\nexcluded from income or resources by this provision.\n\nAny tax credit refund or advance payment received on January 1, 2010 through\nDecember 31, 2012 is not considered a resource for 12 months following the receipt of\nthe payment. However, any unspent portion retained after the 12-month period has\nexpired is a countable resource.\n\n\n# M. 19. CASH RECEIVED FOR MEDICAL OR SOCIAL SERVICES\n\nCash received for medical or social services that is not counted as income is not a\nresource for the calendar month following the month it is received. However, cash\nretained until the first moment of the second calendar month, following its receipt, is\na resource at that time. This rule applies only to the unspent portion of those cash\npayments.\n\nNote: The rule above does not apply to cash received as repayment for medical or\nsocial services bills an individual has already paid. Even though not income, such cash\nis a resource and, if retained, is subject to resource counting rules as of the first\nmoment of the month following receipt.\n\nSee Chapter VII for treatment as income.\n\n\n# M.20. LONG TERM CARE INSURANCE ON OR AFTER MARCH 1, 2009\n\nInsurance companies in Alabama issues long-term care (LTC) insurance policies known\nas Long-Term Care Insurance Partnership Policies. The Long-Term Care Insurance\nPartnership Policy (Partnership Policy) meets specific requirements outlined in the\nDeficit Reduction Act of 2005 in that it contains consumer protection and inflation\nprovisions. In determining eligibility, Medicaid will allow the insured individual to\ndisregard resources in an amount equal to LTC insurance benefits paid by his or her\nPartnership Policy for LTC services received. To qualify for this resource exclusion,\nthe insurance benefit payments may have been made for any LTC services received\n(including those not usually covered by Medicaid) for any length of time in any long-\nterm care setting. Resources excluded in determining Medicaid eligibility will be\nexempt from estate recovery.\n\nIndividuals with a LTC insurance policy issued before March 1, 2009 (a non-Partnership\nPolicy) may exchange the policy for a Partnership Policy; however, only insurance\nbenefits paid by the new Partnership Policy will be used to determine the amount of\nthe resource exclusion. Partnership Policies issued in other states will be accepted,\nand the insured will qualify for the resource exclusion to the same as extent as\nindividuals with Partnership policies issued in Alabama.\n\nLTC insurance policies that have been certified as Partnership policies by the Alabama\nDepartment of Insurance will be issued with a "Partnership Status Disclosure Notice".\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-38" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00125 -->\n\nAn interactive list of all state insurance departments located on the website of the\nNational Association of Insurance Commissioners at\nhttp://www.naic.org/state_web_map.htm may be used to obtain additional\ninformation about out-of-state insurance policies.\n\n\n## M.20.a. VERIFICATION OF INSURANCE PAYMENTS\n\nThe total amount of insurance benefits paid will be verified as of the month of\napplication in order to establish the amount that can be disregarded under the\nPartnership policy exclusion. In cases involving policies that continue to pay benefits\nafter the month of application, total insurance benefits paid will be re-verified at\nannual review. It will not be necessary to verify the exclusion amount each month.\n\nWhen the LTC insurance payment is made directly to the applicant/recipient, the\npayment will be considered income. The monthly insurance payment will be reflected\nin the eligibility and post-eligibility steps in the budgeting process. Financial eligibility\nand the liability amount will be calculated using the usual procedures.\n\nWhen the LTC insurance payment is made directly to the nursing home, the payment\nwill be treated as a third-party payment and will not be considered income to the\napplicant/recipient.\n\n\n## M.20.b. RESOURCE ASSESSMENT IN SPOUSAL CASES\n\nExisting spousal impoverishment rules will apply when determining the spousal share\nof a couple\'s total combined resources. The institutionalized (IS) will be allowed to\ndisregard resources assessed to him/her in an amount equal to LTC insurance benefits\npaid by his/her Partnership Policy. The amount of resources disregarded or protected\nunder the Partnership Policy exclusion should be documented in the case. Resources\nthat cannot be excluded or disregarded must be spent down.\n\nExample: A claimant with a long term care insurance Partnership Policy enters the\nnursing home March 2009. From March 2009 to February 2010, the policy pays a total\nof $54,000 for nursing home care. The claimant applies for Medicaid in March 2010.\nAt the beginning of the continuous period of institutionalization, the claimant and his\nspouse had combined resources totaling $157,000. When computing the spousal share,\ntotal resources are equally divided. Of the $78,500 assessed to the IS, Medicaid\ndisregards $54,000 under the Partnership Policy exclusion. The remaining $24,500\nmust be spent down to the $2000 resource the IS is allowed.\n\nDuring the continuous period of institutionalization after the IS has been determined\neligible, no resources of the CS shall be deemed to the IS. The IS may transfer\nresources that have been protected for him/her under the Partnership policy exclusion\nto the CS without penalty. However, if the IS or CS transfers protected resources to\nothers, the amount of resources that can be disregarded under the Partnership policy\nexclusion will be reduced by the amount transferred. A transfer penalty will be\napplied to a transferred amount greater than the amount that can be disregarded\nunder the Partnership policy exclusion.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-39" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00126 -->\n\nIf the IS is discharged from the nursing home and later readmitted, a new assessment\nshould be completed based on total combined resources at the time of the most\nrecent continuous period of\n\ninstitutionalization. The IS will be permitted to disregard resources assessed to him or\nher up to the amount still available under the Partnership policy exclusion.\n\n\n## M.21. MEDICAID APPOINTEES/CONTRACTORS\n\nMonies received as stipends or reimbursements made to applicants or recipients\nappointed by Medicaid or Medicaid contractors for participation in Medicaid related\nbusiness shall not be considered as income or resources to the applicant/recipient.\nThis includes but is not limited to: money received for participation in citizen advisory\npanels, Medicaid focus groups, or other Medicaid meetings.\n\n\n## M.22. MEDICARE SET ASIDE ARRANGEMENT\n\nMonies received from a Workers\' Compensation award may be designated for future\nmedical expenses. Future medical expenses when they reflect a reasonable estimate\nof future expenses may be place in a Medicare Set-Aside Arrangement (MSA). These\naccounts are excludable resources.\n\n\n### VI.N. COUNTABLE RESOURCES\n\nThe countable value of a resource is the equity a claimant has in the asset. The fact\nthat the asset has no cash value at the time of determination does not mean that it is\nnot a resource.\n\n\n#### N.1. REAL PROPERTY\n\nThe countable value of real property is equity the claimant has in the property.\nEquity is defined as the amount that the property can be expected to sell for on the\nopen market in the particular geographic area involved less any legal encumbrance on\nthe property.\n\n\n##### N. 1.a. DETERMINATION OF OWNERSHIP INTEREST\n\nA copy of all verification documents should be in the case record. These documents\nmust include:\n\nDeeds: A copy of the filed deed that verifies present ownership interest. Validity must\nbe established by the following requirements:\n\n\\- Must be written or printed or partly written and partly printed.\n\n\\- Must be on parchment or paper.\n\n\\- Must be signed by the contracting party or his agent having written authority. If\nthe grantor cannot sign his name, it must be written for him with the words "his\nmark" written against the same or over it.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-40" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00127 -->\n\n\\- The execution of the deed must be attested by one witness; or, if the grantor\ncannot write, by two witnesses; or if he can write but does not do so and\nanother writes his name for him, by two witnesses. In both latter cases, the\ntwo witnesses must be able to and do write their names as witnesses.\n\nNOTE: Instead of witnesses, appropriate state officers, e.g., notary public can\nacknowledge the deed and such acknowledgment will have the same effect.\n\nAdditionally, deeds, as required by Alabama case law, should have:\n\n\\- Positive identification or description of the grantor(s) of the land and the\ngrantee(s).\n\n\\- Description of the property - this does not have to be exact. Courts allow\nparole evidence, i.e., oral testimony, as to the precise property conveyed.\n\n\\- Delivery - the deed must have been delivered to the grantee, to someone for his\nuse, or the proper recording office. This is often a question of intention. In an\nambiguous situation, determine whether the grantor intended to part with\ncontrol of the deed.\n\nIf the date the deed was signed differs significantly from the date the deed is\nrecorded, a Statement of Claimant form should be completed providing an\nexplanation as to the reason for the delay in recording the deed. The deed is\nnot valid until it has been delivered.\n\nNOTE: In cases where one or more deeds are presented on the same property,\nthe deed that was recorded first should be considered as the correct deed in\ndetermining property ownership (for Medicaid eligibility purposes).\n\nWills: In order for a will to be effective, it must be offered for probate. Evidence\nthat it has been offered for probate must be obtained. In order for a will to be held\nvalid by the probate court it must meet the following requirements:\n\n\\- Must be in writing.\n\n\\- Must be signed by the testator (the person making the will) or by another person\nin the presence of the testator and by his/her direction (e.g., the testator may\nbe too ill to sign himself/herself).\n\n\\- Must be attested (witnessed) by at least two witnesses who must sign their\nnames to the will in the presence of the testator.\n\nOther Documents: The following documents may also be considered in determining\nownership interest:\n\n\\- Assessment notice\n\n\\- Recent tax file\n\n\\- Current mortgage statement\n\n\\- Report of title search\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-41" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00128 -->\n\n\n##### N.1.b. TYPES OF OWNERSHIP\n\nThere are ownership interests that arise from Alabama laws. Ownership of real\nproperty can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use of the property\nwithout title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is generally the\nperson possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the property as he sees\nfit.\n\n\n###### Homestead Allowance\n\nState law provides for a $15,000.00 homestead allowance for the surviving spouse from\nthe decedent\'s estate. This allowance has priority over any other distribution made\nfrom the decedent\'s estate, and is in addition to any other share passing to surviving\nspouse under the will, by intestate succession, or by elective share. (Prior to June 15,\n2015, it was $6,000.)\n\n\n###### Exempt Property Allowance\n\nState law provides for $7,500.00 exempt property allowance for the surviving spouse\nfrom the decedent\'s estate. If there is not $7,500.00 worth of personal property in\nthe estate then the spouse is entitled to other assets of the estate to make up the\ndifference. This allowance has priority over any other distributions made from the\nestate, except homestead and family allowances, and is in addition to any other share\nor benefit passing to the spouse by the will of the decedent, by intestate succession,\nor by way of elective share. (Prior to June 15, 2015, it was $3,500.)\n\n\n###### Family Allowance\n\nState law provides for an allowance in money out of the decedent\'s estate for the\nsurviving spouse. The family allowance may be issued as a lump sum not to exceed\n$15,000.00 or in periodic installments not to exceed $500 per month. This allowance\nhas priority over any other distribution made from the estate, except homestead, and\nis in addition to any other share or benefit passing to the spouse by the will of the\ndecedent, by intestate succession, or by way of elective share. (Prior to June 15,\n2015, the allowance was up to one year and equal to $500.00 per month or $6,000 in a\nlump sum.)\n\n\n###### Elective Share\n\nState law provides for a right of election for the surviving spouse to take an elective\nshare of the decedent\'s estate. The elective share is an option available to a surviving\nspouse who can then choose to take outside the decedent\'s will. It is an alternative to\nwhat is provided for the spouse in the decedent\'s will. The elective share is the\nlesser of:\n\n1\\. All the estate of the decedent reduced by the value of the surviving spouse\'s\nseparate estate, or\n\n2\\. One third of the estate of the decedent.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-42" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00129 -->\n\nThe elective share is available only after the debts of the estate are paid but takes\npriority over specific devises in the will.\n\nNOTE: Homestead, family allowance, exempt property, and elective share rights may\nnot be waived except by a written contract, agreement, or waiver signed by the party\nwaiving these rights after fair disclosure of their existence. If these rights are waived,\nthe consideration received by the party waiving these rights must be carefully\nexamined for the possibility of a transfer for less than fair market value.\n\nNOTE: If the will does not provide for a surviving spouse who married the testator\nafter the execution of the will, the omitted spouse shall receive their intestate share\nof the estate, unless they were intentionally left out of the will, or received value by a\ntransfer outside the will.\n\nOwnership of real property can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use\nof the property without title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is\ngenerally the person possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the\nproperty as he sees fit.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.1. ELIGIBILITY THROUGH ANOTHER AGENCY\n\nWhen a claimant becomes eligible for Medicaid through another agency, Medicaid\nterminates the case. District offices should send a Notice of Action to claimants\nadvising them of their termination due to coverage through another agency.\n\n\n## III.L. REQUIREMENTS FOR ELIGIBILITY UNDER A SPECIAL INCOME LEVEL\n\nEligibility under a special income level is not effective until the claimant has been in\na medical institution for a period of thirty continuous days. A "period of 30\ncontinuous days" is defined as being from the day of admission to the medical\ninstitution and ending no earlier than 12:00 midnight of the thirtieth day following the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-29" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00063 -->\n\nbeginning of the period. Once a claimant has been in a medical institution for a 30-\ncontinuous day period, eligibility begins with the first day of the 30-continuous day\nperiod and lasts, assuming all other eligibility criteria are met, until the claimant is\ndischarged from the institution.\n\nNOTE: Exceptions to the fulfillment of the 30-continuous day requirement are for\napplicants who: (1) are already on a full benefit Medicaid program, (2) are potentially\nSSI eligible, and (3) dies within the 30-day period while in a medical facility.\n\n\n## III.M. SOCIAL SECURITY NUMBER\n\nAll claimants must furnish a Social Security Number or verification that they have\napplied for a number. They must also be advised that the Social Security number will\nbe used to verify income and eligibility from other sources including SSA, IRS, and the\nState Wage Information Collection Agency.\n\nEXCEPTION - Emergency Medical Services for qualified aliens who are ineligible\nbecause of alienage and non-qualified aliens.\n\n\n## III.N. REDETERMINATIONS OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nThere must be a review of conditions of eligibility at least annually to make sure that\na claimant is still eligible.\n\n\n## III.O. QUALITY CONTROL\n\nA claimant must provide information within his capacity which is needed to establish\neligibility. This applies to the initial eligibility determination as well as subsequent\ndeterminations. A claimant must agree to cooperate fully with state and federal\npersonnel in a Quality Control review.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-30" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00064 -->\n\n\n## III.T. THIRTY (30) CONTINUOUS DAYS\n\nThe special income test (institutional income standard) is effective with the\ncompletion of 30 continuous days of institutionalization in a medical institution.\nMedicaid eligible claimants (those receiving SSI and/or Optional State\nSupplementation or those potentially eligible for SSI or State Supplementation),\nliability amount should be zero ("0") during the partial month with liability being\nshown the following month. With all other claimants, the liability amount should be\nshown when institutionalized residence is established (30 continuous days\nrequirement is met).\n\nNOTE: Premature or newborn infants applying for disability through SSI who are born\nin a hospital are treated as individuals. There is no deeming from parents while\ninfant remains in hospital.\n\n\n## III.U. PERIODS OF ENTITLEMENT\n\nAccrual rights are those rights of claimants to have their eligibility for Medicaid\ndetermined for certain minimum periods.\n\nAny child under age 19 will have continuous Medicaid eligibility for 12 months,\nregardless of any changes in circumstances, after the initial award or a\nredetermination, unless the child moves out of state, becomes 19 years of age, or\ndies. Children under the age of 19 will have their Medicaid eligibility continued for up\nto 12 months, or until their 19th birthday, from the time of award or redetermination\nof eligibility. Redeterminations are conducted at least once per year. Medicaid\nrecipients are still required to report changes in their situation.\n\nContinued SSI cases will have a DC Tracking Code of "CE" if they have been terminated\nfrom SSI and will continue Medicaid. The D.O. Review date will be the date the case\nwill terminate. Department of Youth Services cases will have DC Tracking Code of\n"DY" while are in custody of DYS.\n\n\n## III.V. RETROACTIVE COVERAGE\n\nThe accrual rights for Medicaid in an institution is the three (3) month period\nimmediately preceding the month in which the application was received provided:\n\n\n## V.1. ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS MET\n\nAll eligibility requirements are met for each month. The criteria of eligibility used in\nmaking this determination will be dependent on claimant\'s living arrangement during\nthat period of time.\n\n\n## V.2. INCURRED MEDICAL EXPENSE\n\nClaimant has incurred medical expenses for those months.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-31" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00065 -->\n\nNOTE: The Record of Contact must indicate if the claimant is eligible for Medicaid\nthree (3) months prior to the month of application or any subsequent month prior to\ncertification. If the claimant has incurred medical expenses during the\nretroactive period, the award notice should cite the specific Administrative Code for\nineligibility if the claimant is determined to be ineligible for retroactive coverage in\nany of the months considered.\n\n\n## III.W. PARTIAL MONTH\n\nIn determining Medicaid eligibility during the partial month in a medical institution,\nthe 30 continuous days requirement applies unless one of the following situations\nexists:\n\n\n## W.1. AT ADMISSION CLAIMANT RECEIVED SSI/FULL MEDICAID\n\nClaimant received SSI payment at the time of admission to medical facility or is on a\nMedicaid full benefit program at the time of admission.\n\n\n## W.2. AT ADMISSION CLAIMANT RECEIVED OPTIONAL STATE SUPPLEMENT\n\nClaimant received Optional State Supplementation payment through the County\nDepartment of Human Resources at the time of admission to the medical facility, or\n\n\n## W.3. MEDICAID APPOINTEES/CONTRACTORS\n\nMonies received as stipends or reimbursements made to applicants or recipients\nappointed by Medicaid or Medicaid contractors for participation in Medicaid related\nbusiness shall not be considered as income or resources to the applicant/recipient.\nThis includes but is not limited to: money received for participation in citizen advisory\npanels, Medicaid focus groups, or other Medicaid meetings.\n\n\n## M.22. MEDICARE SET ASIDE ARRANGEMENT\n\nMonies received from a Workers\' Compensation award may be designated for future\nmedical expenses. Future medical expenses when they reflect a reasonable estimate\nof future expenses may be place in a Medicare Set-Aside Arrangement (MSA). These\naccounts are excludable resources.\n\n\n### VI.N. COUNTABLE RESOURCES\n\nThe countable value of a resource is the equity a claimant has in the asset. The fact\nthat the asset has no cash value at the time of determination does not mean that it is\nnot a resource.\n\n\n#### N.1. REAL PROPERTY\n\nThe countable value of real property is equity the claimant has in the property.\nEquity is defined as the amount that the property can be expected to sell for on the\nopen market in the particular geographic area involved less any legal encumbrance on\nthe property.\n\n\n##### N. 1.a. DETERMINATION OF OWNERSHIP INTEREST\n\nA copy of all verification documents should be in the case record. These documents\nmust include:\n\nDeeds: A copy of the filed deed that verifies present ownership interest. Validity must\nbe established by the following requirements:\n\n\\- Must be written or printed or partly written and partly printed.\n\n\\- Must be on parchment or paper.\n\n\\- Must be signed by the contracting party or his agent having written authority. If\nthe grantor cannot sign his name, it must be written for him with the words "his\nmark" written against the same or over it.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-40" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00127 -->\n\n\\- The execution of the deed must be attested by one witness; or, if the grantor\ncannot write, by two witnesses; or if he can write but does not do so and\nanother writes his name for him, by two witnesses. In both latter cases, the\ntwo witnesses must be able to and do write their names as witnesses.\n\nNOTE: Instead of witnesses, appropriate state officers, e.g., notary public can\nacknowledge the deed and such acknowledgment will have the same effect.\n\nAdditionally, deeds, as required by Alabama case law, should have:\n\n\\- Positive identification or description of the grantor(s) of the land and the\ngrantee(s).\n\n\\- Description of the property - this does not have to be exact. Courts allow\nparole evidence, i.e., oral testimony, as to the precise property conveyed.\n\n\\- Delivery - the deed must have been delivered to the grantee, to someone for his\nuse, or the proper recording office. This is often a question of intention. In an\nambiguous situation, determine whether the grantor intended to part with\ncontrol of the deed.\n\nIf the date the deed was signed differs significantly from the date the deed is\nrecorded, a Statement of Claimant form should be completed providing an\nexplanation as to the reason for the delay in recording the deed. The deed is\nnot valid until it has been delivered.\n\nNOTE: In cases where one or more deeds are presented on the same property,\nthe deed that was recorded first should be considered as the correct deed in\ndetermining property ownership (for Medicaid eligibility purposes).\n\nWills: In order for a will to be effective, it must be offered for probate. Evidence\nthat it has been offered for probate must be obtained. In order for a will to be held\nvalid by the probate court it must meet the following requirements:\n\n\\- Must be in writing.\n\n\\- Must be signed by the testator (the person making the will) or by another person\nin the presence of the testator and by his/her direction (e.g., the testator may\nbe too ill to sign himself/herself).\n\n\\- Must be attested (witnessed) by at least two witnesses who must sign their\nnames to the will in the presence of the testator.\n\nOther Documents: The following documents may also be considered in determining\nownership interest:\n\n\\- Assessment notice\n\n\\- Recent tax file\n\n\\- Current mortgage statement\n\n\\- Report of title search\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-41" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00128 -->\n\n\n##### N.1.b. TYPES OF OWNERSHIP\n\nThere are ownership interests that arise from Alabama laws. Ownership of real\nproperty can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use of the property\nwithout title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is generally the\nperson possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the property as he sees\nfit.\n\n\n###### Homestead Allowance\n\nState law provides for a $15,000.00 homestead allowance for the surviving spouse from\nthe decedent\'s estate. This allowance has priority over any other distribution made\nfrom the decedent\'s estate, and is in addition to any other share passing to surviving\nspouse under the will, by intestate succession, or by elective share. (Prior to June 15,\n2015, it was $6,000.)\n\n\n###### Exempt Property Allowance\n\nState law provides for $7,500.00 exempt property allowance for the surviving spouse\nfrom the decedent\'s estate. If there is not $7,500.00 worth of personal property in\nthe estate then the spouse is entitled to other assets of the estate to make up the\ndifference. This allowance has priority over any other distributions made from the\nestate, except homestead and family allowances, and is in addition to any other share\nor benefit passing to the spouse by the will of the decedent, by intestate succession,\nor by way of elective share. (Prior to June 15, 2015, it was $3,500.)\n\n\n###### Family Allowance\n\nState law provides for an allowance in money out of the decedent\'s estate for the\nsurviving spouse. The family allowance may be issued as a lump sum not to exceed\n$15,000.00 or in periodic installments not to exceed $500 per month. This allowance\nhas priority over any other distribution made from the estate, except homestead, and\nis in addition to any other share or benefit passing to the spouse by the will of the\ndecedent, by intestate succession, or by way of elective share. (Prior to June 15,\n2015, the allowance was up to one year and equal to $500.00 per month or $6,000 in a\nlump sum.)\n\n\n###### Elective Share\n\nState law provides for a right of election for the surviving spouse to take an elective\nshare of the decedent\'s estate. The elective share is an option available to a surviving\nspouse who can then choose to take outside the decedent\'s will. It is an alternative to\nwhat is provided for the spouse in the decedent\'s will. The elective share is the\nlesser of:\n\n1\\. All the estate of the decedent reduced by the value of the surviving spouse\'s\nseparate estate, or\n\n2\\. One third of the estate of the decedent.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-42" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00129 -->\n\nThe elective share is available only after the debts of the estate are paid but takes\npriority over specific devises in the will.\n\nNOTE: Homestead, family allowance, exempt property, and elective share rights may\nnot be waived except by a written contract, agreement, or waiver signed by the party\nwaiving these rights after fair disclosure of their existence. If these rights are waived,\nthe consideration received by the party waiving these rights must be carefully\nexamined for the possibility of a transfer for less than fair market value.\n\nNOTE: If the will does not provide for a surviving spouse who married the testator\nafter the execution of the will, the omitted spouse shall receive their intestate share\nof the estate, unless they were intentionally left out of the will, or received value by a\ntransfer outside the will.\n\nOwnership of real property can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use\nof the property without title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is\ngenerally the person possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the\nproperty as he sees fit.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.1. ELIGIBILITY THROUGH ANOTHER AGENCY\n\nWhen a claimant becomes eligible for Medicaid through another agency, Medicaid\nterminates the case. District offices should send a Notice of Action to claimants\nadvising them of their termination due to coverage through another agency.\n\n\n## III.L. REQUIREMENTS FOR ELIGIBILITY UNDER A SPECIAL INCOME LEVEL\n\nEligibility under a special income level is not effective until the claimant has been in\na medical institution for a period of thirty continuous days. A "period of 30\ncontinuous days" is defined as being from the day of admission to the medical\ninstitution and ending no earlier than 12:00 midnight of the thirtieth day following the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-29" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00063 -->\n\nbeginning of the period. SOCIAL SECURITY NUMBER\n\nAll claimants must furnish a Social Security Number or verification that they have\napplied for a number. This applies to the initial eligibility determination as well as subsequent\ndeterminations. THIRTY (30) CONTINUOUS DAYS\n\nThe special income test (institutional income standard) is effective with the\ncompletion of 30 continuous days of institutionalization in a medical institution.\nMedicaid eligible claimants (those receiving SSI and/or Optional State\nSupplementation or those potentially eligible for SSI or State Supplementation),\nliability amount should be zero ("0") during the partial month with liability being\nshown the following month. With all other claimants, the liability amount should be\nshown when institutionalized residence is established (30 continuous days\nrequirement is met).\n\nNOTE: Premature or newborn infants applying for disability through SSI who are born\nin a hospital are treated as individuals. There is no deeming from parents while\ninfant remains in hospital.\n\n\n## III.U. PERIODS OF ENTITLEMENT\n\nAccrual rights are those rights of claimants to have their eligibility for Medicaid\ndetermined for certain minimum periods.\n\nAny child under age 19 will have continuous Medicaid eligibility for 12 months,\nregardless of any changes in circumstances, after the initial award or a\nredetermination, unless the child moves out of state, becomes 19 years of age, or\ndies. Children under the age of 19 will have their Medicaid eligibility continued for up\nto 12 months, or until their 19th birthday, from the time of award or redetermination\nof eligibility. Redeterminations are conducted at least once per year. Medicaid\nrecipients are still required to report changes in their situation.\n\nContinued SSI cases will have a DC Tracking Code of "CE" if they have been terminated\nfrom SSI and will continue Medicaid. The D.O. Review date will be the date the case\nwill terminate. Department of Youth Services cases will have DC Tracking Code of\n"DY" while are in custody of DYS.\n\n\n## III.V. RETROACTIVE COVERAGE\n\nThe accrual rights for Medicaid in an institution is the three (3) month period\nimmediately preceding the month in which the application was received provided:\n\n\n## V.1. ELIGIBILITY REQUIREMENTS MET\n\nAll eligibility requirements are met for each month. INCURRED MEDICAL EXPENSE\n\nClaimant has incurred medical expenses for those months.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="III-31" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00065 -->\n\nNOTE: The Record of Contact must indicate if the claimant is eligible for Medicaid\nthree (3) months prior to the month of application or any subsequent month prior to\ncertification. PARTIAL MONTH\n\nIn determining Medicaid eligibility during the partial month in a medical institution,\nthe 30 continuous days requirement applies unless one of the following situations\nexists:\n\n\n## W.1.
