2026-04-02T20:27:11.595759803Z [2026-04-02 20:27:11,595] [DEBUG] openai._base_client: Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/deployments/text-embedding/embeddings', 'headers': {'Authorization': '<redacted>'}, 'files': None, 'post_parser': <function Embeddings.create.<locals>.parser at 0x7da1e3741e40>, 'json_data': {'input': 'Ownership of real property can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use\nof the property without title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is\ngenerally the person possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the\nproperty as he sees fit.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.1. FEE SIMPLE OWNERSHIP\n\nWhen property is held in fee simple, this means that the owner has the sole ownership\ninterest. He/she alone (or if he/she is mentally incompetent, his/her person with\nauthority) may sell or transfer the ownership interest without conditions imposed by\nothers. This is commonly referred to as simple ownership.\n\nIf the claimant is the sole owner of the property and has the right to dispose of the\nproperty, the equity value of the property is countable as a resource.\n\nThe claimant owns all the property (has a full interest) if:\n\n\\- The claimant acquired the property as the sole owner.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly with the\nright of survivorship.\n\n\\- The deceased spouse willed the entire sole ownership interest to the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse willed his interest to the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse died intestate leaving no surviving children\nor dependents, no parents, no brothers, no sisters, nor their descendants.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.2. JOINT OWNERSHIP\n\nJoint ownership means that ownership interest is vested in more than one person. In\ntenancy-in-common, two or more persons have an undivided fractional interest in the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-43" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00130 -->\n\nwhole property for the duration of the tenancy. There is no right of survivorship to a\ntenancy-in-common.\n\nIn Alabama, persons may own property as tenants in common or as joint tenants with\nright of survivorship. For the latter to be the case, the deed must specify that there is\na right of survivorship.\n\nWhen a right of survivorship is present, the party who survives automatically gets the\ndeceased spouse\'s entire interest, so that he or she then has outright title (fee simple)\nto the whole property. It makes no difference whether the husband or wife survives.\nIt operates the same way in either case.\n\nThe usual case, however, will be where the married couple jointly owns the property\nas tenants-in-common with no right of survivorship. While both are alive, each has an\nundivided one-half absolute interest in the property. When one\'s spouse dies, his or\nher interest is treated by the same rules as if he or she owned the whole property.\nThe rules of descent and distribution apply to the undivided one-half absolute interest\nof the dead husband or wife.\n\nIf two or more persons jointly own the property, the value of the property is charged\nto the claimant in proportion to his/her ownership interest. Assume each owner has\nan equal share unless evidence is established to the contrary. Assume each owner is\nfree to dispose of his/her share of the property. The claimant\'s interest is an\nundivided one-half interest (half of property) if:\n\n\\- The claimant and spouse are both alive and they acquired the property jointly\nwith or without right of survivorship.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse willed his/her interest to someone other\nthan the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired property jointly without right\nof survivorship and the claimant\'s spouse died intestate leaving: surviving\nchildren or their descendants, and/or a parent or parents, and/or brothers and\nsisters, or their descendants.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.3. LIFE ESTATE - LIFE INTEREST\n\nBlack\'s Law Dictionary defines a life estate as "a legal arrangement whereby the\nbeneficiary (i.e., the life tenant) is entitled to the income from the property for his or\nher life." This definition has two essential components: (1) the right to use and\nenjoyment, or income from the property, and (2) for the term of his/her natural life.\n\nLife estates may be established by deed, will, or under state intestacy laws. An\nexample of the type of language necessary to establish a life estate is shown below.\n\nEXAMPLE: "The Grantor and Grantees stipulate, covenant, and agree that the Grantor\nshall have the right to live in the residence located on said lands for the remainder of\nhis/her life, rent free, tax free, and free of all other charges."\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-44" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00131 -->\n\nThe owner of a life estate has the right to occupy the property, the right to use the\nproperty and receive income from it, and the right to sell his/her life estate interest.\nHe/she does not have the right to sell the property in fee simple.\n\nWhen a life estate is sold, it conveys to the purchaser certain rights as long as the\nowner of the life estate is alive. When the owner of the life estate dies, the purchaser\nof the life estate loses all rights to the property.\n\nAn attempt by the record owner of a piece of real property to reserve a life estate on\nbehalf of a person with no legal right to the property is ineffective and does not\ncreate any interest in the property on behalf of the stranger.\n\nExample: Grantor grants, bargains, sells, and conveys his/her interest in real property\nsubject to the life estate on behalf of his/her spouse.\n\nIf the record owner of real property conveys a life estate to his/her spouse and\nconveys the remainder interest to another person such a conveyance creates a valid\ninterest in the property on behalf of his/her spouse.\n\nExample: Grantor grants, bargains, sells, and conveys a life estate in real property to\nhis/her spouse and grants, bargains, sells, and conveys the remainder interest to a\nthird party.\n\nA life estate interest in real property is assigned a zero value for Institutional cases.\nThe Life Estate and Remainder Interest Table will be used to assign a value to SSI\nRelated cases. A lien is not required.\n\nThe purchase of a life estate interest in another individual\'s home is considered a\ntransfer of resources for less than fair market value unless:\n\n\\- The purchase price was for fair market value; and,\n\n\\- The purchaser continuously resided in the home for a period of at least\ntwelve full months after the date of purchase; and\n\n\\- The purchase price must be actuarially sound.\n\nNote: Any excess purchase price will be treated as a transfer of assets for less than\nfair market value. Refer to the Life Estate values in the section N.1.c.1.\n\n\n####### N. 1.b.4. REMAINDER INTEREST\n\nWhen an owner conveys his/her property to another person for life (life estate holder)\nwith the remainder going to a second person (the remainder man) upon the death of\nthe life estate holder, both a life estate interest and a remainder interest have been\ncreated in the property. Upon the death of the life estate holder, the "remainder\nman" (person who has the remainder interest in the property) will hold full title in fee\nsimple. Another way the two interests can be created is when the owner conveys\nproperty to another for life and reserves in himself/herself the remainder interest.\n\nIf there is no contractual restriction that prevents the "remainder man" from disposing\nof his/her remainder interest during the life estate holder\'s tenancy, the value of the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-45" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00132 -->\n\nremainder interest is a countable resource unless it meets the criteria for exclusion.\nThe resource value is calculated using the tax appraised value of the property and the\nLife Estate and Remainder Interest Table for all programs. A lien is required. [See\nVI.N.1.c.1. for instructions regarding valuation of remainder interest.]\n\nNOTE: The full tax appraised value will be used for determination of uncompensated\ntransfers of a remainder interest in property.\n\n\n####### N.1.b.5. FORECLOSURE', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

2026-04-02T20:27:16.607179368Z [2026-04-02 20:27:16,606] [DEBUG] openai._base_client: Request options: {'method': 'post', 'url': '/deployments/text-embedding/embeddings', 'headers': {'Authorization': '<redacted>'}, 'files': None, 'post_parser': <function Embeddings.create.<locals>.parser at 0x7da1e3741e40>, 'json_data': {'input': 'Ownership of real property can consist of an interest in the title or a right to the use\nof the property without title to the property. The owner of a real property resource is\ngenerally the person possessing legal title, and who has the right to control the\nproperty as he sees fit.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.1. FEE SIMPLE OWNERSHIP\n\nWhen property is held in fee simple, this means that the owner has the sole ownership\ninterest. He/she alone (or if he/she is mentally incompetent, his/her person with\nauthority) may sell or transfer the ownership interest without conditions imposed by\nothers. This is commonly referred to as simple ownership.\n\nIf the claimant is the sole owner of the property and has the right to dispose of the\nproperty, the equity value of the property is countable as a resource.\n\nThe claimant owns all the property (has a full interest) if:\n\n\\- The claimant acquired the property as the sole owner.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly with the\nright of survivorship.\n\n\\- The deceased spouse willed the entire sole ownership interest to the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse willed his interest to the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse died intestate leaving no surviving children\nor dependents, no parents, no brothers, no sisters, nor their descendants.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.2. JOINT OWNERSHIP\n\nJoint ownership means that ownership interest is vested in more than one person. In\ntenancy-in-common, two or more persons have an undivided fractional interest in the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-43" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00130 -->\n\nwhole property for the duration of the tenancy. There is no right of survivorship to a\ntenancy-in-common.\n\nIn Alabama, persons may own property as tenants in common or as joint tenants with\nright of survivorship. For the latter to be the case, the deed must specify that there is\na right of survivorship.\n\nWhen a right of survivorship is present, the party who survives automatically gets the\ndeceased spouse\'s entire interest, so that he or she then has outright title (fee simple)\nto the whole property. It makes no difference whether the husband or wife survives.\nIt operates the same way in either case.\n\nThe usual case, however, will be where the married couple jointly owns the property\nas tenants-in-common with no right of survivorship. While both are alive, each has an\nundivided one-half absolute interest in the property. When one\'s spouse dies, his or\nher interest is treated by the same rules as if he or she owned the whole property.\nThe rules of descent and distribution apply to the undivided one-half absolute interest\nof the dead husband or wife.\n\nIf two or more persons jointly own the property, the value of the property is charged\nto the claimant in proportion to his/her ownership interest. Assume each owner has\nan equal share unless evidence is established to the contrary. Assume each owner is\nfree to dispose of his/her share of the property. The claimant\'s interest is an\nundivided one-half interest (half of property) if:\n\n\\- The claimant and spouse are both alive and they acquired the property jointly\nwith or without right of survivorship.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired the property jointly without\nright of survivorship and the spouse willed his/her interest to someone other\nthan the claimant.\n\n\\- The claimant and the deceased spouse acquired property jointly without right\nof survivorship and the claimant\'s spouse died intestate leaving: surviving\nchildren or their descendants, and/or a parent or parents, and/or brothers and\nsisters, or their descendants.\n\n\n###### N. 1.b.3. LIFE ESTATE - LIFE INTEREST\n\nBlack\'s Law Dictionary defines a life estate as "a legal arrangement whereby the\nbeneficiary (i.e., the life tenant) is entitled to the income from the property for his or\nher life." This definition has two essential components: (1) the right to use and\nenjoyment, or income from the property, and (2) for the term of his/her natural life.\n\nLife estates may be established by deed, will, or under state intestacy laws. An\nexample of the type of language necessary to establish a life estate is shown below.\n\nEXAMPLE: "The Grantor and Grantees stipulate, covenant, and agree that the Grantor\nshall have the right to live in the residence located on said lands for the remainder of\nhis/her life, rent free, tax free, and free of all other charges."\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-44" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00131 -->\n\nThe owner of a life estate has the right to occupy the property, the right to use the\nproperty and receive income from it, and the right to sell his/her life estate interest.\nHe/she does not have the right to sell the property in fee simple.\n\nWhen a life estate is sold, it conveys to the purchaser certain rights as long as the\nowner of the life estate is alive. When the owner of the life estate dies, the purchaser\nof the life estate loses all rights to the property.\n\nAn attempt by the record owner of a piece of real property to reserve a life estate on\nbehalf of a person with no legal right to the property is ineffective and does not\ncreate any interest in the property on behalf of the stranger.\n\nExample: Grantor grants, bargains, sells, and conveys his/her interest in real property\nsubject to the life estate on behalf of his/her spouse.\n\nIf the record owner of real property conveys a life estate to his/her spouse and\nconveys the remainder interest to another person such a conveyance creates a valid\ninterest in the property on behalf of his/her spouse.\n\nExample: Grantor grants, bargains, sells, and conveys a life estate in real property to\nhis/her spouse and grants, bargains, sells, and conveys the remainder interest to a\nthird party.\n\nA life estate interest in real property is assigned a zero value for Institutional cases.\nThe Life Estate and Remainder Interest Table will be used to assign a value to SSI\nRelated cases. A lien is not required.\n\nThe purchase of a life estate interest in another individual\'s home is considered a\ntransfer of resources for less than fair market value unless:\n\n\\- The purchase price was for fair market value; and,\n\n\\- The purchaser continuously resided in the home for a period of at least\ntwelve full months after the date of purchase; and\n\n\\- The purchase price must be actuarially sound.\n\nNote: Any excess purchase price will be treated as a transfer of assets for less than\nfair market value. Refer to the Life Estate values in the section N.1.c.1.\n\n\n####### N. 1.b.4. REMAINDER INTEREST\n\nWhen an owner conveys his/her property to another person for life (life estate holder)\nwith the remainder going to a second person (the remainder man) upon the death of\nthe life estate holder, both a life estate interest and a remainder interest have been\ncreated in the property. Upon the death of the life estate holder, the "remainder\nman" (person who has the remainder interest in the property) will hold full title in fee\nsimple. Another way the two interests can be created is when the owner conveys\nproperty to another for life and reserves in himself/herself the remainder interest.\n\nIf there is no contractual restriction that prevents the "remainder man" from disposing\nof his/her remainder interest during the life estate holder\'s tenancy, the value of the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-45" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00132 -->\n\nremainder interest is a countable resource unless it meets the criteria for exclusion.\nThe resource value is calculated using the tax appraised value of the property and the\nLife Estate and Remainder Interest Table for all programs. A lien is required. [See\nVI.N.1.c.1. for instructions regarding valuation of remainder interest.]\n\nNOTE: The full tax appraised value will be used for determination of uncompensated\ntransfers of a remainder interest in property.\n\n\n####### N.1.b.5. FORECLOSURE', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

FORECLOSURE\n\nIn a foreclosure, if there is no equity interest, there is no transfer. In other\nforeclosures, where there is an equity interest, we must ensure that it is not a\n"friendly" foreclosure (a situation where a family member or friend attempts to profit\nfrom buying the property after foreclosure for less than the tax appraised value) or an\nattempt to transfer resources. The case should be fully documented. Statements\nshould be made by the claimant as to the attempts made to retrieve his/her equity,\nand the circumstances surrounding the foreclosure, and if the claimant made any\nattempt to sell the property prior to the foreclosure. If you have determined, to your\nsatisfaction that it was not a friendly foreclosure and the claimant had no choice in\nthe matter, no transfer penalty should be imposed.\n\nUnless there is some document restricting the claimant from selling the property or\nrestricting the access or use of the property, or some other litigation action in process,\nthe property is a countable resource until the foreclosure is complete (date of offer\nfor public sale).\n\nWhen an outstanding mortgage exists, the sponsor/applicant should be advised that if\nthe property is foreclosed and there is an equity value, a transfer penalty may be\nimposed. Efforts should be made to encourage the sponsor/applicant to exclude the\nproperty using the bona-fide effort to sale exclusion. See Section M.3. (Bona-fide\nEffort to Sale) i.e., the property should be listed continuously with a realtor.\n\n\n###### N.1.b.6. PROPERTY SOLD FOR TAXES\n\nProperty sold for taxes is the sale of a real estate property that results when a\ntaxpayer reaches a certain point of delinquency in their owned property tax payments.\n\n\n###### STATE ENTERS A TAX LIEN ON PROPERTY\n\nWhen the State enters a lien on property due to delinquent ad valorem taxes, the\nproperty is still a resource to the claimant. The claimant is legally the owner of the\nproperty. The property should be handled as any other property owned by the\nclaimant (meet property exclusion, etc.) until it is sold by the State to a third party.\n\n\n###### STATE SELLS PROPERTY TO A THIRD PARTY\n\nOnce the property is sold by the State to a third party, the claimant still has a right of\nredemption interest in the property. The claimant has up to three years, from the\ndate of sale to redeem property sold for delinquent ad valorem taxes. The value of the\nright of redemption is the tax appraised value less the amount of back taxes owed.\nThe resource amount from the right of redemption will need to have a bona fide effort\nto sell exclusion, otherwise the resource amount would be considered a countable\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-46" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00133 -->\n\nresource. If the property is not redeemed within the three-year period, the claimant\nno longer has any interest in the property and therefore no other action is warranted.\n\n\n####### N. 1.b.7. OTHER PROPERTY INTEREST\n\nA claimant may also have ownership of the right to the use of the real property such as\nmineral rights, water rights, easements, and leaseholds (use only).\n\n\n######## Mineral Rights\n\nA mineral right is an ownership interest in certain natural resources such as coal,\nsulfur, petroleum, sand, natural gas, etc., which are usually obtained from the\nground.\n\n\n######## Timber Rights\n\nTimber rights permit a person to cut and remove free standing trees from property\nowned by another as designated by contract with the person holding title to the land\non which the timber stands.\n\nAs the life estate holder has the right to use, enjoy, and receive income from the\nproperty for the duration of his/her life, the value of the timber is a countable\nresource when it is sold (In spousal cases reevaluate the original assessment of\nresources and include the proceeds from the sale of the timber in the assessment to\ndetermine how much to attribute to the institutional spouse). Treat the timber rights\nin accordance with the most recent changes to the Federal Register. The one time\nsale of timber off property is a conversion of resources and not income in the month of\nreceipt. If there is a contract for periodic cutting, then the receipts would be income\nin the month received.\n\n\n######## Easements\n\nAn easement is a property right whereby one has the right to use of land of another\nperson for a special purpose.\n\nLeasehold\n\nLeasehold does not designate rights of ownership but conveys to an individual the\ncontrol of property for a definite period or duration, at the owner\'s will and usually for\nan agreed rent. However, under the common law, life estates could be created by\nlease. A photocopy of a lease for life should be submitted on an Interpretation\nRequest to the Medicaid Office of General Counsel through the area\'s regional\nsupervisor to determine if it creates a life estate under state law.\n\n\n# N.1.c. VALUATION OF REAL PROPERTY\n\nThe following factors must be considered:\n\n\\- Ownership Interest: The type of ownership, number of owners, and the\nclaimant and/or spouse\'s actual ownership must be developed.\n\n\\- Availability. Resources which are not available are not considered. A\nresource may be unavailable due to legal restrictions.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-47" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00134 -->\n\nReal property must be convertible to cash that can be used for his/her support/\nmaintenance. If the claimant or spouse has the right, authority, or power to liquidate\nthe property or his/her interest in the property, it is a countable resource.\n\n\\- Current Market Value (CMV) - Current market value is the amount the property\ncan be expected to sell for on the open market in the geographic area. The\nvalue of real property is determined by:\n\n\\- Tax Assessment - Send Tax Appraisal form to the local County Tax Assessor\'s\noffice to verify the current market value. In the event the assessed value is\ngiven instead of the current market value, the CMV can be determined by\ndividing the tax assessed value by the assessment ratio.\n\nExample: Tax Assessed Value - $10,000.00 Assessment Ratio divided by 50% =\n$20,000.00 Current Market Value.\n\n\\- If the CMV based on the Tax Assessor\'s statement exceeds the Agency\'s resource\nlimit and the value cannot be otherwise excluded, eligibility can be denied/\nterminated.\n\n\\- The Tax Assessor\'s statement is always used to determine CMV unless one of the\nfollowing conditions applies:\n\n\\- The assessment is more than one (1) year old unless it is the most current\nassessment.\n\n\\- It is a special purpose assessment.\n\n\\- It is based on a fixed rate per acre method. This term applies only to standard\ndollar per acre assessments for land based on land usage without regard to\nmarket prices. Examples: Agricultural, Industrial, and Residential\n\nThe fixed rate method does not apply when certain conditions require that types of\nland be taxed similarly regardless of location. Examples: Swamp, desert, and\nlandfills.\n\nIn these cases the Tax Assessor\'s statement would not be used.\n\n\\- The taxing authority does not provide the current market value, does not\nprovide an assessment ratio, or provides only a range; e.g., between 50% and\n75%. Exception: If the claimant would be ineligible using the lower limit of the\nrange, eligibility can be denied/terminated.\n\n\\- It is under appeal before the Board of Equalization. Verification of this must be\nin the case file. NOTE: When the Tax Assessor changes property values, secure\na copy of the updated Tax Assessment sheet.\n\nIf such conditions as they appear above are present, document the file and\nfollow procedures outlined below.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-48" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00135 -->\n\n\\- Estimate of CMV from a knowledgeable source:\nAsk the claimant or sponsor to obtain the estimate. APPLICATION PROCESS\n\n\n## A.1. OPPORTUNITY TO APPLY\n\nAll individuals wishing to make application for Medicaid must have the\nopportunity to do so, without delay. An application may be submitted by mail, in\nperson, fax, on-line, telephone, and other commonly acceptable forms of\ntechnology. Fax, electronic, and phone signatures are acceptable.\n\n\n## A.2. COVERAGE GROUPS DETERMINATION\n\nA claimant who applies for Medicaid under any category is entitled to have eligibility\ndetermined under all possible eligibility groups.\n\nWhen determining eligibility for a coverage group that provides limited Medicaid\nservices (i.e., QMB, SLMB, QI 1) or when an individual has been determined ineligible\nfor any coverage group, the worker should determine if the claimant may be eligible\nunder another coverage group [i.e., widow(er), DAC, Pickle, etc.]. The case should\nbe awarded under the coverage group that provides the most Medicaid services.\n\nNote: A fleeing felon who loses Medicaid because he is no longer actually receiving\nan SSI benefit could still be eligible for Medicaid provided, he meets the\nrequirements for some other group covered under the state\'s Medicaid plan.\nTherefore, benefits can be awarded under the limited program if the client meets all\nthe eligibility requirements.\n\n\n## A.3. EX PARTE\n\nWhen a claimant\'s Medicaid is terminated, the worker should determine if the\nclaimant might be eligible under another coverage group.\n\n\n## A.4. APPLICATION FORM\n\nThe official application form for institutional Medicaid programs is Agency Form\n204/205. The official application form for the limited coverage programs i.e. QMB,\nSLMB, and QI 1 and for the Qualified Disabled Working Individual Program (QDWI) is\nAgency Form 211. Agency Form 204/205 is used to redetermine eligibility for\ncontinued Medicaid benefits for former SSI recipients. These serve as sources of\nbasic data to obtain information necessary to clear eligibility for Medicaid programs.\n\nApplications for all programs are sent to the district office serving the county in\nwhich the claimant is residing at time of application.\n\n\n## A.5. REQUIREMENTS FOR APPLICATION\n\nAn application will be considered as a claim for Medicaid benefits if the following\nrequirements are met:\n\nA.5.a. An acceptable Medicaid Agency application must be\nsigned. Xerox or facsimile copies are also acceptable. The application process will\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00069 -->\n\nbegin when the Alabama Medicaid Agency receives a signed application. Support\npersonnel should inspect applications to ensure applications are signed. Unsigned\napplications should be returned and should not be entered into the Medicaid system,\nas they are not considered to be an acceptable application.\n\nThe date received will be the date the signed application is opened, and date\nstamped by Medicaid or Medicaid\'s representative or the date it is received online.\nThe date received is used as the date that protects the accrual rights. Exception:\nUpon receipt of an application that is mailed, use the date shown by the U.S.\npostmark as the filing date if using the date, the application is received will result in\na loss of benefits. If the postmark is unreadable or there is no postmark, use the\ndate the application is signed (if dated) or 5 days before receipt of the application,\nwhichever date is later.\n\nA.5.b. It must be filed at a Medicaid district office.\nEXCEPTION: Applications for waiver services programs must be filed through the\nappropriate provider agency (i.e., Department of Senior Services {E & D and TA\nWaivers}, Department of Rehabilitation Services {State of Alabama Independent\nLiving Waiver}, or Department of Mental Health {ID and LAH Waivers}).\n\nA.5.c. The claimant, or someone authorized to act on\nclaimant\'s behalf, must sign the application. A person who signs an application for\nsomeone else will be required to provide evidence of his or her authority to sign the\napplication for the person claiming benefits. This information must be verified prior\nto approving an application. A Xerox or faxed signature is acceptable.\n\nIf the claimant signs the application form using an "X" for his or her signature, the\nsignature must have two (2) witnesses or be notarized.\n\nAn Alabama Medicaid Agency employee may serve as an appointed representative for\nan individual applying for Medicaid. A worker cannot determine eligibility for a\nfamily member.\n\n\n## IV.B. REAPPLICATION\n\nIf an application has been denied and the claimant wishes to submit a new\napplication, the previously denied application may be used as a reapplication if the\nreapplication is made within 60 days of the previously denied application. The actual\napplication form that was denied may be used for the reapplication process.\n\nIf the reapplication is not made within 60 days, a new application form must be\ncompleted.\n\nExample: Application denied on October 10 for failure to provide information.\nSponsor visits district office in November to bring needed verification and make\nanother application. Because sponsor wishes to reapply and comply with agency\npolicy within 60 days, a new application form will not be necessary. In this case, use\nthe following procedure.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00070 -->\n\n\n## B.1. REAPPLICATION PROCEDURE\n\nUse the following steps when reapplication is applicable:\n\nB. 1.a. Mark through "Application" on page one of the denied\napplication and write "Reapplication" in red.\n\nB. 1.b. Have the claimant or sponsor sign and date the\n"Reapplication" form. This signature should be under the section explaining the\nresponsibilities of the person signing the form.\n\nB. 1.c. Indicate in the Record of Contact Form that the\nreapplication has been received.\n\n\n## B.2. ACCRUAL RIGHTS - REAPPLICATION\n\nAccrual rights for Medicaid will start with the date of the reapplication.\n\n\n# IV.C. REACTIVATIONS\n\nIn situations where a claimant has been terminated based on an annual review and\nthe claimant subsequently complies with policy, the Medicaid Worker may reactivate\neligibility without a new application form if the evidence is presented within 90 days\nof the termination notice. This same procedure would be applicable also when a\ncase is terminated for failure to provide information/verification due to a change in\nthe claimant\'s income or resources. If the requested verification is received within\n90 days of the termination notice, eligibility should be reinstated for previous months\nas long as all eligibility points are met in those months, this includes QMB cases.\n\n\n# C.1. REACTIVATION PROCEDURE\n\nIndicate in red in the Record of Contact the date of the reactivation. Document or\nexplain the receipt of verification or necessary information that reestablishes\neligibility.\n\n\n# IV.D. CLAIMANT\'S/ REPRESENTATIVE\'S RESPONSIBILITIES\n\nIt is the responsibility of the claimant or person with legal authority of the claimant\nfor Medicaid to provide information sufficient to establish the eligibility of each\nindividual for whom assistance is requested, including, but not limited to, the\nfurnishing of a social security number, and the establishment of age, residence,\ncitizenship, blindness, disability, and financial eligibility (income and resources) in\neach of the months in which Medicaid benefits are sought.\n\n\n# D.1. APPOINTMENT OF REPRESENTATIVE', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

COVERAGE GROUPS DETERMINATION\n\nA claimant who applies for Medicaid under any category is entitled to have eligibility\ndetermined under all possible eligibility groups.\n\nWhen determining eligibility for a coverage group that provides limited Medicaid\nservices (i.e., QMB, SLMB, QI 1) or when an individual has been determined ineligible\nfor any coverage group, the worker should determine if the claimant may be eligible\nunder another coverage group [i.e., widow(er), DAC, Pickle, etc.]. The case should\nbe awarded under the coverage group that provides the most Medicaid services.\n\nNote: A fleeing felon who loses Medicaid because he is no longer actually receiving\nan SSI benefit could still be eligible for Medicaid provided, he meets the\nrequirements for some other group covered under the state\'s Medicaid plan.\nTherefore, benefits can be awarded under the limited program if the client meets all\nthe eligibility requirements.\n\n\n## A.3. EX PARTE\n\nWhen a claimant\'s Medicaid is terminated, the worker should determine if the\nclaimant might be eligible under another coverage group.\n\n\n## A.4. APPLICATION FORM\n\nThe official application form for institutional Medicaid programs is Agency Form\n204/205. The official application form for the limited coverage programs i.e. QMB,\nSLMB, and QI 1 and for the Qualified Disabled Working Individual Program (QDWI) is\nAgency Form 211. Agency Form 204/205 is used to redetermine eligibility for\ncontinued Medicaid benefits for former SSI recipients. These serve as sources of\nbasic data to obtain information necessary to clear eligibility for Medicaid programs.\n\nApplications for all programs are sent to the district office serving the county in\nwhich the claimant is residing at time of application.\n\n\n## A.5. The application process will\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00069 -->\n\nbegin when the Alabama Medicaid Agency receives a signed application. Support\npersonnel should inspect applications to ensure applications are signed. Unsigned\napplications should be returned and should not be entered into the Medicaid system,\nas they are not considered to be an acceptable application.\n\nThe date received will be the date the signed application is opened, and date\nstamped by Medicaid or Medicaid\'s representative or the date it is received online.\nThe date received is used as the date that protects the accrual rights. Exception:\nUpon receipt of an application that is mailed, use the date shown by the U.S.\npostmark as the filing date if using the date, the application is received will result in\na loss of benefits. If the postmark is unreadable or there is no postmark, use the\ndate the application is signed (if dated) or 5 days before receipt of the application,\nwhichever date is later.\n\nA.5.b. It must be filed at a Medicaid district office.\nEXCEPTION: Applications for waiver services programs must be filed through the\nappropriate provider agency (i.e., Department of Senior Services {E & D and TA\nWaivers}, Department of Rehabilitation Services {State of Alabama Independent\nLiving Waiver}, or Department of Mental Health {ID and LAH Waivers}).\n\nA.5.c. A person who signs an application for\nsomeone else will be required to provide evidence of his or her authority to sign the\napplication for the person claiming benefits. This information must be verified prior\nto approving an application. A Xerox or faxed signature is acceptable.\n\nIf the claimant signs the application form using an "X" for his or her signature, the\nsignature must have two (2) witnesses or be notarized.\n\nAn Alabama Medicaid Agency employee may serve as an appointed representative for\nan individual applying for Medicaid. A worker cannot determine eligibility for a\nfamily member.\n\n\n## IV.B. REAPPLICATION\n\nIf an application has been denied and the claimant wishes to submit a new\napplication, the previously denied application may be used as a reapplication if the\nreapplication is made within 60 days of the previously denied application. The actual\napplication form that was denied may be used for the reapplication process.\n\nIf the reapplication is not made within 60 days, a new application form must be\ncompleted.\n\nExample: Application denied on October 10 for failure to provide information.\nSponsor visits district office in November to bring needed verification and make\nanother application. Because sponsor wishes to reapply and comply with agency\npolicy within 60 days, a new application form will not be necessary. APPLICATION PROCESS\n\n\n## A.1. OPPORTUNITY TO APPLY\n\nAll individuals wishing to make application for Medicaid must have the\nopportunity to do so, without delay. An application may be submitted by mail, in\nperson, fax, on-line, telephone, and other commonly acceptable forms of\ntechnology. Fax, electronic, and phone signatures are acceptable.\n\n\n## A.2. COVERAGE GROUPS DETERMINATION\n\nA claimant who applies for Medicaid under any category is entitled to have eligibility\ndetermined under all possible eligibility groups.\n\nWhen determining eligibility for a coverage group that provides limited Medicaid\nservices (i.e., QMB, SLMB, QI 1) or when an individual has been determined ineligible\nfor any coverage group, the worker should determine if the claimant may be eligible\nunder another coverage group [i.e., widow(er), DAC, Pickle, etc.]. The case should\nbe awarded under the coverage group that provides the most Medicaid services.\n\nNote: A fleeing felon who loses Medicaid because he is no longer actually receiving\nan SSI benefit could still be eligible for Medicaid provided, he meets the\nrequirements for some other group covered under the state\'s Medicaid plan.\nTherefore, benefits can be awarded under the limited program if the client meets all\nthe eligibility requirements.\n\n\n## A.3. EX PARTE\n\nWhen a claimant\'s Medicaid is terminated, the worker should determine if the\nclaimant might be eligible under another coverage group.\n\n\n## A.4. APPLICATION FORM\n\nThe official application form for institutional Medicaid programs is Agency Form\n204/205. The official application form for the limited coverage programs i.e. QMB,\nSLMB, and QI 1 and for the Qualified Disabled Working Individual Program (QDWI) is\nAgency Form 211. Agency Form 204/205 is used to redetermine eligibility for\ncontinued Medicaid benefits for former SSI recipients. These serve as sources of\nbasic data to obtain information necessary to clear eligibility for Medicaid programs.\n\nApplications for all programs are sent to the district office serving the county in\nwhich the claimant is residing at time of application.\n\n\n## A.5. REQUIREMENTS FOR APPLICATION\n\nAn application will be considered as a claim for Medicaid benefits if the following\nrequirements are met:\n\nA.5.a. An acceptable Medicaid Agency application must be\nsigned. Xerox or facsimile copies are also acceptable. The application process will\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-1" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00069 -->\n\nbegin when the Alabama Medicaid Agency receives a signed application. Support\npersonnel should inspect applications to ensure applications are signed. Unsigned\napplications should be returned and should not be entered into the Medicaid system,\nas they are not considered to be an acceptable application.\n\nThe date received will be the date the signed application is opened, and date\nstamped by Medicaid or Medicaid\'s representative or the date it is received online.\nThe date received is used as the date that protects the accrual rights. Exception:\nUpon receipt of an application that is mailed, use the date shown by the U.S.\npostmark as the filing date if using the date, the application is received will result in\na loss of benefits. If the postmark is unreadable or there is no postmark, use the\ndate the application is signed (if dated) or 5 days before receipt of the application,\nwhichever date is later.\n\nA.5.b. It must be filed at a Medicaid district office.\nEXCEPTION: Applications for waiver services programs must be filed through the\nappropriate provider agency (i.e., Department of Senior Services {E & D and TA\nWaivers}, Department of Rehabilitation Services {State of Alabama Independent\nLiving Waiver}, or Department of Mental Health {ID and LAH Waivers}).\n\nA.5.c. The claimant, or someone authorized to act on\nclaimant\'s behalf, must sign the application. A person who signs an application for\nsomeone else will be required to provide evidence of his or her authority to sign the\napplication for the person claiming benefits. This information must be verified prior\nto approving an application. A Xerox or faxed signature is acceptable.\n\nIf the claimant signs the application form using an "X" for his or her signature, the\nsignature must have two (2) witnesses or be notarized.\n\nAn Alabama Medicaid Agency employee may serve as an appointed representative for\nan individual applying for Medicaid. A worker cannot determine eligibility for a\nfamily member.\n\n\n## IV.B. REAPPLICATION\n\nIf an application has been denied and the claimant wishes to submit a new\napplication, the previously denied application may be used as a reapplication if the\nreapplication is made within 60 days of the previously denied application. The actual\napplication form that was denied may be used for the reapplication process.\n\nIf the reapplication is not made within 60 days, a new application form must be\ncompleted.\n\nExample: Application denied on October 10 for failure to provide information.\nSponsor visits district office in November to bring needed verification and make\nanother application. Because sponsor wishes to reapply and comply with agency\npolicy within 60 days, a new application form will not be necessary. In this case, use\nthe following procedure.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-2" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00070 -->\n\n\n## B.1. REAPPLICATION PROCEDURE\n\nUse the following steps when reapplication is applicable:\n\nB. 1.a. Mark through "Application" on page one of the denied\napplication and write "Reapplication" in red.\n\nB. 1.b. Have the claimant or sponsor sign and date the\n"Reapplication" form. This signature should be under the section explaining the\nresponsibilities of the person signing the form.\n\nB. 1.c. Indicate in the Record of Contact Form that the\nreapplication has been received.\n\n\n## B.2. ACCRUAL RIGHTS - REAPPLICATION\n\nAccrual rights for Medicaid will start with the date of the reapplication.\n\n\n# IV.C. REACTIVATIONS\n\nIn situations where a claimant has been terminated based on an annual review and\nthe claimant subsequently complies with policy, the Medicaid Worker may reactivate\neligibility without a new application form if the evidence is presented within 90 days\nof the termination notice. This same procedure would be applicable also when a\ncase is terminated for failure to provide information/verification due to a change in\nthe claimant\'s income or resources. If the requested verification is received within\n90 days of the termination notice, eligibility should be reinstated for previous months\nas long as all eligibility points are met in those months, this includes QMB cases.\n\n\n# C.1. REACTIVATION PROCEDURE\n\nIndicate in red in the Record of Contact the date of the reactivation. Document or\nexplain the receipt of verification or necessary information that reestablishes\neligibility.\n\n\n# IV.D. CLAIMANT\'S/ REPRESENTATIVE\'S RESPONSIBILITIES\n\nIt is the responsibility of the claimant or person with legal authority of the claimant\nfor Medicaid to provide information sufficient to establish the eligibility of each\nindividual for whom assistance is requested, including, but not limited to, the\nfurnishing of a social security number, and the establishment of age, residence,\ncitizenship, blindness, disability, and financial eligibility (income and resources) in\neach of the months in which Medicaid benefits are sought.\n\n\n# D.1. REDETERMINATIONS OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nAn eligibility redetermination (annual review) must be made for every claimant at\nleast once every 12 months. A 30-day checklist will be sent when requesting\nadditional information at annual review. If additional time is requested to gather the\ninformation, the case record should be documented to include the date of contact,\nwhy the information requested is not submitted timely and additional time given.\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency determines continuing eligibility and, if necessary,\nadjusts claimant\'s record to indicate available income and reflect change(s) in\nhis/her circumstances. CHANGES BETWEEN REVIEWS\n\nA change may be reported to the district office in many ways.\n\nAmong them are:\n\nF.1.a. Orally - Changes may come to the attention of the\nworker from the claimant, provider, or references in person or by telephone. Oral\nreports must be detailed in the Record of Contact.\n\nF.1.b. Correspondence - A letter from the claimant, sponsor,\nor notice from provider may be received.\n\nF.1.c. Intra-Agency - Other workers in the agency may notify\nAlabama Medicaid Office of a change in a claimant\'s situation.\n\n\n# IV.G. INQUIRY\n\nIf a case record is not established on an inquiry, complete a Record of Contact\nregarding what was discussed. This often proves very helpful when an application is\nlater filed - especially when references to income and resources are drastically\ndifferent from date of inquiry to date of application.\n\n\n# IV.H. ELIGIBILITY DETERMINATION PROCESS - INTRODUCTION\n\nAlabama Medicaid Agency is responsible for determining initial and continuing\neligibility. In determining eligibility, the Medicaid caseworker must show that every\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00073 -->\n\neligibility requirement is met. Each point of eligibility must be substantiated,\nverified, and documented in the case record.\n\n\n## H.1. PERSONAL INTERVIEW\n\nA personal interview is required for every application received by Alabama Medicaid\nAgency except for the Limited Medicaid programs.\n\nIt is not required, prior to denial, to have a personal contact i.e. face to face or\ntelephone with claimants who are active SSI recipients, Limited Medicaid programs\nwhere applicants do not have Medicare Part A coverage, and applicants who\nobviously have excess liquid resources.\n\nInterviews may be held with the applicant/authorized representative by telephone or\nin the district office. When a claimant cannot be interviewed personally, his\nrepresentative may be interviewed and may complete the form and sign for him/her.\nAn Appointment of Representative form is applicable.\n\nA personal contact (face-to-face or telephone) with the claimant or\nsponsor/authorized representative is necessary when an application is received with\ninsufficient information before negative action (denial) is taken unless multiple\nattempts have failed, or no telephone number is provided, and no response is\nreceived to written correspondence.\n\n\n## H.2. INVESTIGATION\n\nIn preparation for the investigation, clear the files for available records on the\nclaimant and study the material in the record to become familiar with the facts at\nhand.\n\n\n## H.3. DATA SOURCE\n\nThe claimant is the most important source of information in the eligibility\ndetermination or the redetermination of his/her eligibility. Expect him/her to\nprovide all and complete information as well as verification about his/her situation.\nIf he/she cannot provide sufficient evidence as needed, the eligibility worker should\noffer help in obtaining and securing verification. If the claimant does not respond to\nthe first written request for information within 30 days, the application will be\ndenied.\n\nIf the applicant/sponsor request additional time to gather the requested information,\ndocument the case record to include the date of contact, why the information\nrequested not submitted timely and, additional time given.\n\n\n## H.4. This will not be income\nin the month received; it will only be the new value of the resource and should be\nbudgeted appropriately.\n\n\n## N. 1.c.2. VALUATION OF OTHER INTERESTS\n\nOther property interests (mineral rights, timber rights, easements, and leaseholds)\nmay be countable resources if they have a cash value available to the claimant or\nspouse.\n\nMineral Rights\n\nA knowledgeable source (other than the Tax Assessor) should be used to determine the\nvalue. Recommended sources:\n\n\\- Gas and Oil Board 205-349-2852\n\nIf no other value can be provided, the current market value of mineral rights can be\ndetermined by multiplying the yearly income received by four (4).\n\nTimber Rights, Easements, and Leaseholds\n\nTo determine value of these property interests, the following information should be\nobtained:\n\n\\- Proof of ownership interest.\n\n\\- Proof from a knowledgeable source giving the estimated value.\n\n\\- Verification of any income received as a result of ownership interest, if\napplicable.\n\nEXAMPLE: A timber company may be paying a claimant\'s monthly fee for the right to\ncut timber.\n\nNOTE: Liens are applicable on mineral and timber rights.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-50" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00137 -->\n\n\n# N.2. CHECKING AND SAVINGS ACCOUNTS\n\nFunds maintained in checking or savings accounts are countable resources. The\nclaimant\'s interest in the funds must be determined.\n\n\n# N.2.a. DETERMINATION OF OWNERSHIP\n\nWhen a claimant is a joint account holder who has unrestricted access to the funds, all\nof the funds are presumed to belong to the claimant. The claimant will be offered the\nopportunity to rebut this presumption. A successful rebuttal will result in a finding\nthat the funds in the joint account are not owned by the claimant and therefore, are\nnot resources.\n\n\n# N.2.a.1. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - One Account Holder is a Claimant\n\nWhen only one holder of a joint account is a claimant who has unrestricted access to\nthe funds in the account, all the funds in the account are presumed to be the\nclaimant\'s. This presumption is made regardless of the source of the funds.\n\n\n# N.2.a.2. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - Two or More Account Holders are Eligible Individuals or Claimants\n\nWhen two or more eligible claimants are holders of a joint account and each has\nunrestricted access to the funds in the account, the presumption is that each owns an\nequal share of the funds in the account. This presumption is made regardless of the\nsource of the funds.\n\n\n## N.2.a.3. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - Deemors are Joint Account Holders\n\nWhen a deemor is a joint account holder with a claimant and each has unrestricted\naccess to the funds, presume that all the funds in the account are the claimant\'s.\n\nIf two or more claimants are joint account holders with a deemor, presume the funds\nbelong to the claimants.\n\nIf two deemors, who are not considered parents, hold a joint account, divide the funds\nequally between them for deeming purposes.\n\n\n## N.2.b. UNRESTRICTED ACCESS TO FUNDS\n\nThe determination of accessibility depends upon the legal structure of the account.\nWhen a claimant is a joint holder of an account and is legally able to withdraw funds\nfrom the account, he/she is considered to have unrestricted access to the funds.\n\nIt is possible to have ownership interest in a bank account but have restricted access\nto the funds. If unrestricted access is an issue which cannot be resolved with the\nevidence on hand, request the financial institution to provide additional information,\ne.g., the exact language used in the document which established the account, a\ndescription of any legal restrictions on the claimant\'s access to the funds, etc.\n\n\n## N.2.c. EVIDENCE NECESSARY FOR A SUCCESSFUL REBUTTAL\n\nWhen the claimant has unrestricted access to the funds, but does not consider himself\nor herself as an owner of the funds (either fully or partially) and objects to the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-51" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00138 -->\n\npresumption of ownership, it is the claimant\'s responsibility to submit all of the\nfollowing evidence:\n\n\\- A statement by the claimant, sponsor or deemor on "Statement of claimant or\nOther Person" form, containing the penalty clause, giving:\n\n\\- His/her allegation regarding no ownership or partial ownership of the funds,\ndocumented by:\n\n\\- A statement from the financial institution, the bank book, account book,\ncertificate of deposit, etc., verifying the language of the account (photo\ncopies are necessary for the case record); and\n\n\\- Signature card;\n\n\\- The reason for establishing the joint account.\n\n\\- Who made deposits to and withdrawals from the account, how withdrawals\nwere spent, etc. This must be documented with evidence (e.g., canceled\nchecks, wage statements, deposit slips, employer\'s statements verifying\nsource of deposits, etc.).\n\n\\- Corroborating statements on "Statement of Claimant or Other Person" form\nfrom the other account holder(s), including all of the verification required\nabove.\n\nIf the co-holder of the joint account is incompetent or a minor, obtain corroborating\nstatement from a third party that has knowledge of the circumstances surrounding the\nestablishment of the joint account. If there is no third party, document in the case\nrecord why no corroborating statement was obtained. Using the information provided,\nthe District Office manager/assistant should make a determination of the success or\nfailure of the rebuttal. If a determination cannot be made after discussion with the\nregional supervisor, send rebuttal evidence to the area\'s regional supervisor on an\nInterpretation Request form. The regional supervisor will forward the Interpretation\nRequest and attachments (including their comments on their stance) to the Policy\nUnit.\n\nIf rebuttal evidence is submitted, determine who owns the funds in the joint account\nand document the findings in the case record. Proceed with any further development\nor make the determination of eligibility, as applicable.\n\nIf the rebuttal of presumption is successful; it is determined that the funds in the\naccount do not belong to the claimant; and should be excluded as a countable\nresource; a change in the account designation removing the claimant\'s or deemor\'s\nname from the account or restricting access to the account must be executed.\nVerification of the date and manner of the change should be submitted as evidence\nbefore the eligibility process can be completed. If this change is not made and the\nclaimant does not submit evidence within ten (10) days following notification,\npresumption of ownership will apply.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-52" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00139 -->', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

This will not be income\nin the month received; it will only be the new value of the resource and should be\nbudgeted appropriately.\n\n\n## N. 1.c.2. VALUATION OF OTHER INTERESTS\n\nOther property interests (mineral rights, timber rights, easements, and leaseholds)\nmay be countable resources if they have a cash value available to the claimant or\nspouse.\n\nMineral Rights\n\nA knowledgeable source (other than the Tax Assessor) should be used to determine the\nvalue. Recommended sources:\n\n\\- Gas and Oil Board 205-349-2852\n\nIf no other value can be provided, the current market value of mineral rights can be\ndetermined by multiplying the yearly income received by four (4).\n\nTimber Rights, Easements, and Leaseholds\n\nTo determine value of these property interests, the following information should be\nobtained:\n\n\\- Proof of ownership interest.\n\n\\- Proof from a knowledgeable source giving the estimated value.\n\n\\- Verification of any income received as a result of ownership interest, if\napplicable.\n\nEXAMPLE: A timber company may be paying a claimant\'s monthly fee for the right to\ncut timber.\n\nNOTE: Liens are applicable on mineral and timber rights.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-50" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00137 -->\n\n\n# N.2. CHECKING AND SAVINGS ACCOUNTS\n\nFunds maintained in checking or savings accounts are countable resources. The\nclaimant\'s interest in the funds must be determined.\n\n\n# N.2.a. DETERMINATION OF OWNERSHIP\n\nWhen a claimant is a joint account holder who has unrestricted access to the funds, all\nof the funds are presumed to belong to the claimant. The claimant will be offered the\nopportunity to rebut this presumption. A successful rebuttal will result in a finding\nthat the funds in the joint account are not owned by the claimant and therefore, are\nnot resources.\n\n\n# N.2.a.1. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - One Account Holder is a Claimant\n\nWhen only one holder of a joint account is a claimant who has unrestricted access to\nthe funds in the account, all the funds in the account are presumed to be the\nclaimant\'s. This presumption is made regardless of the source of the funds.\n\n\n# N.2.a.2. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - Two or More Account Holders are Eligible Individuals or Claimants\n\nWhen two or more eligible claimants are holders of a joint account and each has\nunrestricted access to the funds in the account, the presumption is that each owns an\nequal share of the funds in the account. This presumption is made regardless of the\nsource of the funds.\n\n\n## N.2.a.3. PRESUMPTION OF OWNERSHIP - Deemors are Joint Account Holders\n\nWhen a deemor is a joint account holder with a claimant and each has unrestricted\naccess to the funds, presume that all the funds in the account are the claimant\'s.\n\nIf two or more claimants are joint account holders with a deemor, presume the funds\nbelong to the claimants.\n\nIf two deemors, who are not considered parents, hold a joint account, divide the funds\nequally between them for deeming purposes.\n\n\n## N.2.b. UNRESTRICTED ACCESS TO FUNDS\n\nThe determination of accessibility depends upon the legal structure of the account.\nWhen a claimant is a joint holder of an account and is legally able to withdraw funds\nfrom the account, he/she is considered to have unrestricted access to the funds.\n\nIt is possible to have ownership interest in a bank account but have restricted access\nto the funds. If unrestricted access is an issue which cannot be resolved with the\nevidence on hand, request the financial institution to provide additional information,\ne.g., the exact language used in the document which established the account, a\ndescription of any legal restrictions on the claimant\'s access to the funds, etc.\n\n\n## N.2.c. EVIDENCE NECESSARY FOR A SUCCESSFUL REBUTTAL\n\nWhen the claimant has unrestricted access to the funds, but does not consider himself\nor herself as an owner of the funds (either fully or partially) and objects to the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-51" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00138 -->\n\npresumption of ownership, it is the claimant\'s responsibility to submit all of the\nfollowing evidence:\n\n\\- A statement by the claimant, sponsor or deemor on "Statement of claimant or\nOther Person" form, containing the penalty clause, giving:\n\n\\- His/her allegation regarding no ownership or partial ownership of the funds,\ndocumented by:\n\n\\- A statement from the financial institution, the bank book, account book,\ncertificate of deposit, etc., verifying the language of the account (photo\ncopies are necessary for the case record); and\n\n\\- Signature card;\n\n\\- The reason for establishing the joint account.\n\n\\- Who made deposits to and withdrawals from the account, how withdrawals\nwere spent, etc. This must be documented with evidence (e.g., canceled\nchecks, wage statements, deposit slips, employer\'s statements verifying\nsource of deposits, etc.).\n\n\\- Corroborating statements on "Statement of Claimant or Other Person" form\nfrom the other account holder(s), including all of the verification required\nabove.\n\nIf the co-holder of the joint account is incompetent or a minor, obtain corroborating\nstatement from a third party that has knowledge of the circumstances surrounding the\nestablishment of the joint account. If there is no third party, document in the case\nrecord why no corroborating statement was obtained. Using the information provided,\nthe District Office manager/assistant should make a determination of the success or\nfailure of the rebuttal. If a determination cannot be made after discussion with the\nregional supervisor, send rebuttal evidence to the area\'s regional supervisor on an\nInterpretation Request form. The regional supervisor will forward the Interpretation\nRequest and attachments (including their comments on their stance) to the Policy\nUnit.\n\nIf rebuttal evidence is submitted, determine who owns the funds in the joint account\nand document the findings in the case record. Proceed with any further development\nor make the determination of eligibility, as applicable.\n\nIf the rebuttal of presumption is successful; it is determined that the funds in the\naccount do not belong to the claimant; and should be excluded as a countable\nresource; a change in the account designation removing the claimant\'s or deemor\'s\nname from the account or restricting access to the account must be executed.\nVerification of the date and manner of the change should be submitted as evidence\nbefore the eligibility process can be completed. If this change is not made and the\nclaimant does not submit evidence within ten (10) days following notification,\npresumption of ownership will apply.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-52" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00139 -->', 'model': 'text-embedding'}}

LIFE INSURANCE\n\nA life insurance policy is considered only to the extent of its cash surrender value. If\nthe face values of all life insurance policies on the claimant or spouse total\n$1,500/$5,000 or less, the cash surrender value of such policies will not be taken into\naccount. Insurance which has no cash surrender value (e.g. term insurance, burial\ninsurance) is not considered in determining the face value of insurance.\n\nWhen the total face values of all life insurance (excluding term, (group), and burial\ninsurance) policies on the claimant or spouse exceed $1,500/$5,000, the cash\nsurrender values of all such policies must be counted as a resource.\n\n\n### N.3.a. DETERMINING OWNERSHIP\n\nIt is necessary that the life insurance policy be owned by the claimant or person whose\nresources are deemed to him/her. If the consent of another person is needed to\nsurrender a policy for its full cash surrender value, and the consent is obtained, the\nlife insurance policy is considered to be a resource. If the consent cannot be\nobtained, the policy is not included. Resolve any doubt about the policy\'s status in\nthis regard by contacting the insurance company. Assignment of a life insurance\npolicy to another person means consent of that person is required before it can be\ncashed. Most policies require that an assignment be reported to the company to be\neffective.\n\n\n### N.3.b. DETERMINING CASH SURRENDER VALUE\n\nIn computing the cash surrender value of a life insurance policy, it is necessary to\nknow if the premiums are up-to-date or if they are in default (have not been paid),\nand to read the conditions in the policy affecting cash surrender. If the policy is not in\ndefault and a loan has not been obtained based on the cash value of the policy, the\ncash surrender value is calculated by using the table of value in the policy as of the\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-53" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00140 -->\n\nlast anniversary date (the anniversary date of a policy is the same as the date of\nissue).\n\nMany companies show a table of the cash or loan value as part of the life insurance\npolicy itself. Most of these tables show the cash surrender value at 5-year intervals\nfrom the date the policy was issued based on a dollar amount (per $1,000 of life\ninsurance) and 1 to 5-year intervals for the age of the policy (the number of years the\npolicy has been in effect). It is possible to ascertain the approximate cash value of a\npolicy by consulting these tables.\n\nHave the claimant submit all life insurance policies he/she or his/her spouse own.\nDocument the file with copies all life insurance policies. The claimant\'s statements\ncan usually be verified through examination of the policies. Where this is not possible\n(e.g., cash surrender value cannot be determined because policy is in default or\nbecause dividends have accrued on the policy or because the age of the policy is not\ncovered in the table) contact the insurance company or local agent for the\nnecessary verification. This will be done for all cases in which the pertinent\ninformation cannot be obtained from examination of the policies. Record the\nfollowing information for each life insurance policy owned:\n\n\\- Name of insured.\n\n\\- Name of owner.\n\n\\- Type of insurance (ordinary life or term).\n\n\\- Face value. Face value is the amount of basic death benefit contracted for at\nthe time the policy is purchased.\n\n\\- For ordinary life, cash surrender value.\n\n\\- For ordinary life, amount of loans outstanding against the policy.\n\n\\- Name of company.\n\n\\- Policy number.\n\nNOTE: Dividends on life insurance are a partial return of premiums paid and, as such,\nare not income for Medicaid purposes. However, any interest earned by the dividends\nis unearned income.\n\n\n### N.4. TRUSTS\n\n\n#### N.4.a. DEFINITION OF TRUST\n\nA trust is a right of property held by one party for the benefit of another. The person\nwho holds the legal title to property for the benefit or use of another is the "trustee."\nThe person for whose benefit the trust is created is the "beneficiary." While the\nbeneficiary of a trust does not hold legal title, he does have an equitable ownership\ninterest in the property. According to the terms of the particular trust involved, the\nbeneficiary may be the person designated to receive income from the trust either\ndirectly or through the trustee.\n\nNOTE: All trust documents must be submitted to the Agency\'s Legal Division for\nevaluation on an Interpretation Request form through the area\'s regional supervisor.\nException: Alabama Family Trusts and Qualified Income Trusts are not required to be\nevaluated by the Legal Division.\n\n<!-- PageNumber="VI-54" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00141 -->\n\n\n#### N.4.b. VALUATION OF A TRUST\n\nWhen the trust is a resource it is necessary to obtain the value of the trust. Contact\nthe holder of the trust funds to get the necessary information to develop its value.\nThe information gathered to determine whether the trust is a resource or creates\nincome and the conclusions reached should be entered in the case file on a Record of\nContact (including a copy of the trust document). The information and a copy of the\ntrust document should be submitted to the Agency\'s Legal Division for evaluation on\nan Interpretation Request form through the area\'s regional supervisor.\n\n\n## N.4.c. INCOME FROM TRUST\n\nIf the claimant has a right to the income from the principal of the trust as it becomes\navailable (whether or not it is applied for his/her use on a current basis), it is income\nto him/her as it becomes available.\n\nIf the claimant has no right to the income from the trust principal and the income is\nadded to the principal, then the earnings from the trust principal are not income to\nthe claimant for Medicaid purposes.\n\n\n## N.4.d. ALABAMA FAMILY TRUSTS\n\nAlabama Family Trusts (AFT) do not have to be sent to the Legal Division for a decision\nas to how the assets of the trust will be handled. The district office managers and\nassistant managers will review the trusts based on the following criteria: There is\nusually a standard form that is used to create the AFT. The factor, which must be\npresent in the AFT to permit exclusion of the assets, is that the trust be irrevocable.\nThe AFT has a place where the applicant must actually circle the trust is revocable or\nirrevocable. If the trust is marked to be revocable, then the trust is a countable\nresource. If the applicant marks the AFT is irrevocable, then the assets of the trust\nare an excluded resource. When the district office manager/assistant manager is not\nsure if the AFT is a countable or excludable resource, the AFT should be submitted on\nan Interpretation Request Form. APPOINTMENT OF REPRESENTATIVE\n\nAn Appointment of Representative form is included in the application form.\nAdditional evidence to establish the authority of a person to sign an application for\nsomeone else must be submitted on an Appointment of Representative Form. A copy\nof legal authority representative papers, a durable power of attorney, or similar\nevidence of authority must be provided. In all such cases, the person\'s signature\naccepting appointment should still be obtained on Agency Form 204/205. If the\nadditional information required on the Appointment of Representative form is not\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-3" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00071 -->\n\nreceived within 30 days from the date the application is received, the application\nshould be denied.\n\nIf the person signing the application alleges that the claimant is mentally\nincompetent or physically unable to sign the application, but no person with legal\nauthority to represent the claimant has ever been legally designated, he or she\nshould submit a written statement describing his or her relationship to the claimant,\nwhy the claimant cannot sign the application, and the extent to which the\nrepresentative is responsible for the care of the claimant. The person signing for the\nclaimant must demonstrate his legal authority to bind the claimant. As in the case of\na person with legal authority to represent the claimant or an attorney-in-fact, the\nperson submitting the statement should still sign the acceptance of appointment\nportion of Agency Form 204/205.\n\nIt is preferable to have an original signature of the "Appointment of Representative"\nform. However, a facsimile copy of the Appointment of Representative form is\nacceptable.\n\nNOTE: In the case of an application accepted on a deceased individual, the\nAppointment of Representative form must still be completed.\n\n\n# IV.E. LENGTH OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nOnce established, eligibility continues until a change in the claimant\'s circumstances\nor agency policy occurs in the following type cases:\n\nNursing Home\nContinuous (C cases) or Pickle\nGrandfathered\nICF-MR\nDisabled Adult Child (DAC)\n\nWidow/Widower\n\nQualified Medicare Beneficiary (QMB)\n\nQualified Working Disabled Individuals (QDWI)\n\nSpecified Low Income Medicaid Beneficiary (SLMB)\n\nQualified Individual 1\n\nChildren under 21 in a Psychiatric Facility\nPost-Hospital Extended Care\nWaiver Groups:\nState of Alabama Independent Living (SAIL) Waiver\nElderly/Disabled (E/D) Waiver\n\nIntellectual Disabilities (ID) Waiver\n\nTechnology Assisted (TA) Waiver\nAlabama Community Transition (ACT) Waiver\nLiving At Home (LAH)\n\nOnce established, eligibility is certified for a limited period in the following type\ncases:\n\nHospital\nRetroactive\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-4" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00072 -->\n\n<!-- PageHeader="Newborn" -->\n\n\n# IV.F. REDETERMINATIONS OF ELIGIBILITY\n\nAn eligibility redetermination (annual review) must be made for every claimant at\nleast once every 12 months. A 30-day checklist will be sent when requesting\nadditional information at annual review. If additional time is requested to gather the\ninformation, the case record should be documented to include the date of contact,\nwhy the information requested is not submitted timely and additional time given.\n\nThe Alabama Medicaid Agency determines continuing eligibility and, if necessary,\nadjusts claimant\'s record to indicate available income and reflect change(s) in\nhis/her circumstances. A redetermination may be necessary before the annual\nreview for claimants whose circumstances have changed. An interim action may be\nnecessary at any time before a review.\n\nNOTE: In situations where a claimant has been terminated based on an annual review\nand the claimant subsequently complies with policy, the Medicaid Worker may\nreactivate eligibility without a new application form if the evidence is presented\nwithin 90 days of the termination notice. This same procedure would be applicable\nalso when a case is terminated for failure to provide information/verification due to\na change in the claimant\'s income or resources. If the requested verification is\nreceived within 90 days of the termination notice, eligibility should be reinstated for\nprevious months as long as all eligibility points are met in those months, this includes\nQMB cases.\n\n\n## F.1. CHANGES BETWEEN REVIEWS\n\nA change may be reported to the district office in many ways.\n\nAmong them are:\n\nF.1.a. Orally - Changes may come to the attention of the\nworker from the claimant, provider, or references in person or by telephone. Oral\nreports must be detailed in the Record of Contact.\n\nF.1.b. Correspondence - A letter from the claimant, sponsor,\nor notice from provider may be received.\n\nF.1.c. Intra-Agency - Other workers in the agency may notify\nAlabama Medicaid Office of a change in a claimant\'s situation.\n\n\n# IV.G. INQUIRY\n\nIf a case record is not established on an inquiry, complete a Record of Contact\nregarding what was discussed. This often proves very helpful when an application is\nlater filed - especially when references to income and resources are drastically\ndifferent from date of inquiry to date of application.\n\n\n# IV.H. In determining eligibility, the Medicaid caseworker must show that every\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-5" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00073 -->\n\neligibility requirement is met. Each point of eligibility must be substantiated,\nverified, and documented in the case record.\n\n\n## H.1. PERSONAL INTERVIEW\n\nA personal interview is required for every application received by Alabama Medicaid\nAgency except for the Limited Medicaid programs.\n\nIt is not required, prior to denial, to have a personal contact i.e. face to face or\ntelephone with claimants who are active SSI recipients, Limited Medicaid programs\nwhere applicants do not have Medicare Part A coverage, and applicants who\nobviously have excess liquid resources.\n\nInterviews may be held with the applicant/authorized representative by telephone or\nin the district office. INVESTIGATION\n\nIn preparation for the investigation, clear the files for available records on the\nclaimant and study the material in the record to become familiar with the facts at\nhand.\n\n\n## H.3. PROPERTY\n\n(I)\nProperty must be verified.\n\n(a) Send property form(s) to county Tax Assessor (other than county of\nresidence) as determined by the district office if the application indicates interest in\nproperty within the last 60 months.\n\n(b) Send property form to Tax Assessor in county of residence.\n\n(c) Send property form(s), at worker\'s discretion, to previous addresses\nif the information is questionable or not clear.\n\n(d) Property form should show name of claimant [and previous married\nnames(s); maiden name], name of spouse [and previous married names(s); maiden\nname].\n\n(e) The following exceptions may be applied when verifying property\nownership interest, for all applications other than QMB or SLMB, in the past 60\nmonths:\n\nIf a claimant alleges having once owned property which was disposed of MORE THAN\n60 months prior to the application for Medicaid, send Agency Form 224 (Tax Appraisal\nForm) to the appropriate county tax assessor(s) office(s) and obtain a statement on\nAgency Form 234 (Statement of Claimant or Other Person) from the claimant or\nsponsor about claimant\'s previous property ownership if the information is not\ncompleted on the signed application. No additional action is required if the\napplication is completed supplying information regarding the disposal of property.\n\nEXCEPTION: If a claimant or sponsor presents a copy of an acquisition deed but\nalleges that property was disposed of more than 60 months ago, verification of the\ndisposition (copy of disposal deed) must be obtained prior to the final eligibility\ndetermination.\n\nIf a claimant alleges current property ownership interest or if he alleges having\ndisposed of property WITHIN the 60-month period prior to the Medicaid application\ndate, verification documentation will continue to be required. Copies of all\ndocuments verifying property ownership interest and disposal of property interest\nMUST be obtained (deeds) and Agency Form 224 (Tax Appraisal Form) sent to the\nappropriate county tax assessor(s) office(s) prior to the final eligibility\ndetermination.\n\n\n### H.5.a. 2. LIQUID RESOURCES\n\n(II)\nLiquid resources must be verified. The following is offered as a guide\nfor means of verifying liquid resources.\n\n(a) Special interviewing procedures in the collateral interview.\n\n(1) How much cash do you have on hand, at home, in checking\naccount, in savings account, in credit union?\n\n(2)\nWhere do you cash checks?\n\n<!-- PageNumber="IV-8" -->\n<!-- PageBreak00076 -->\n\naccount?\n\n(3)\nWhere do you have checks deposited directly to your\n\n(4)\nWhere have you borrowed money in the past?\n\n(5)\nWhere do you have a special account, such as a Christmas\nclub?\n\n(6)\nWhere have you obtained a bank credit card?\n\n(8)\nWhere do you pay your home mortgage or rental payment?\n\n(9)\nWhere do you have money set aside for emergencies?\n\n(10)\nWhere do you have a safe deposit box?\n\n(11) Does your name appear on any account that you consider\nbeing someone else\'s?
